<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Intellectual Property Rights. List the main categories of IPR recognized under the Indian legal framework.**

**Model Answer:**

Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) refer to the bundle of legal rights granted to creators/inventors over intangible creations of the human mind, providing the rights-holder an exclusive commercial monopoly for a defined period, in exchange for public disclosure (in case of patents).

The main categories under Indian law are:

1. **Patent** — governed by the Patents Act, 1970
2. **Trademark** — governed by the Trade Marks Act, 1999
3. **Copyright** — governed by the Copyright Act, 1957
4. **Industrial Design** — governed by the Designs Act, 2000
5. **Geographical Indication** — governed by the GI of Goods Act, 1999
6. **Trade Secret** — protected under the Indian Contract Act, 1872 and the IT Act, 2000

> Each category is distinct in subject matter, term, and registration requirements. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is the term of protection for a patent and a trademark in India? State the relevant statutes.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Patent** — Term of **20 years** from the date of filing of the application, governed by **The Patents Act, 1970** (amended 2005, 2020). After expiry, the invention enters the public domain.
- **Trademark** — Term of **10 years**, **renewable indefinitely** (in successive 10-year blocks) upon application and payment of fees, governed by **The Trade Marks Act, 1999**.

> The perpetual renewability of trademarks (subject to use) is a key contrast with the fixed 20-year patent term. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"Intellectual Property Rights are the foundation of modern technology transfer mechanisms." Critically examine this statement with reference to patents, trademarks, and trade secrets.**

**Model Answer Outline:**

**Introduction (2 marks):**
- Define IPR and its relevance to technology transfer.
- Technology transfer is incomplete without proper IP protection; IP is the *legal currency* through which technology is transacted.

**Body — Critical Analysis (10 marks):**

**(a) Patents in Technology Transfer (4 marks):**
- Patents grant a 20-year exclusive monopoly, creating a period during which the inventor/institution can license or assign the technology without competition.
- Licensing mechanisms: exclusive license, non-exclusive license, sole license, cross-licensing.
- Royalties: typically 2–8% of net sales; can be milestone-based.
- Example: Pharmaceutical patents licensed by Indian generic companies post-2005 (post-TRIPS amendment) demonstrate patent-driven TT.
- Joint patents between university and industry facilitate collaborative R&D and TT.

**(b) Trademarks in Technology Transfer (3 marks):**
- Trademarks protect brand equity, which travels with the product in franchise, licensing, and merchandising arrangements.
- Example: Fast-food franchises (McDonald's, KFC) — the trademark is the *core asset* transferred, while operations are local.
- Trademark licensing: 5–10% royalty of gross sales is common in franchise models.

**(c) Trade Secrets in Technology Transfer (3 marks):**
- Trade secrets (know-how) are transferred under strict NDAs and confidentiality clauses.
- Example: Coca-Cola's formula, Kentucky Fried Chicken's "11 herbs & spices" — value preserved perpetually as long as secrecy holds.
- Know-how licensing accompanies patent licensing in most TT agreements (the *technical assistance* component).

**Conclusion (2 marks):**
- IPR is not merely a *legal protection* but the **operational mechanism** that enables valuation, negotiation, licensing, and enforcement of technology transfer. Without IPR, technology transfer lacks legal certainty, economic valuation, and conflict-resolution mechanism.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the different forms of Intellectual Property Rights recognized in India. Compare the protection offered by patents, copyrights, and trademarks in terms of (i) subject matter, (ii) term of protection, and (iii) registration requirement.**

**Model Answer Outline:**

**Introduction (2 marks):**
- India recognizes six major forms of IPR; the question demands detailed analysis of three (patents, copyrights, trademarks).

**Body — Detailed Explanation (10 marks):**

**(a) Forms of IPR in India (4 marks):**
1. Patent — for inventions (Patents Act, 1970)
2. Trademark — for brand identity (Trade Marks Act, 1999)
3. Copyright — for creative works (Copyright Act, 1957)
4. Industrial Design — for aesthetic appearance (Designs Act, 2000)
5. Geographical Indication — for region-linked products (GI Act, 1999)
6. Trade Secret — protected under common law and IT Act, 2000

**(b) Comparison Table (6 marks):**

| Criterion | Patent | Copyright | Trademark |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Subject Matter | New invention (product/process) | Original literary, artistic, musical, software works | Brand sign (word, logo, sound, shape) |
| (ii) Term | 20 years from filing | Life of author + 60 years (or 60 yrs for corporate works) | 10 years, renewable indefinitely |
| (iii) Registration | Mandatory (application + grant) | Not mandatory (automatic on creation; registration is evidentiary) | Optional (™ = unregistered; ® = registered) |
| Governing Statute | Patents Act, 1970 | Copyright Act, 1957 | Trade Marks Act, 1999 |
| Rights | Right to make, use, sell, import | Right to reproduce, distribute, perform, display | Right to use the mark + license + sue infringers |

**Conclusion (2 marks):**
- Each IP form protects a *different* aspect of innovation. A complete IP strategy for a tech product typically uses **all three** simultaneously, ensuring full-spectrum legal protection.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Patent" and "Copyright" subject matter** — Patents protect *functional inventions*; Copyright protects *expression of ideas*. Software is *copyrighted*; an algorithm is *not patentable* in India per Section 3(k) of the Patents Act unless tied to a hardware innovation.
> 2. **Mixing up terms** — Patent = 20 years; Trademark = 10 years (renewable); Copyright = Life+60 years. This is a **3-mark MCQ trap** in KTU Part A.
> 3. **Forgetting that Copyright is automatic** — No registration is required for copyright protection to arise; registration is *only evidence in court*.
> 4. **Not mentioning the relevant statute** — KTU examiners award marks for **citing the Act and year** (e.g., "Patents Act, 1970"). Omitting the year loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Treating GI and Trademark as the same** — A trademark is *private ownership* (one owner); a GI is *community/regional ownership* (e.g., all Darjeeling tea planters collectively).
> 6. **Ignoring the TRIPS obligation** — India amended its IP laws in 2005 specifically for TRIPS compliance (introducing product patents in pharma/agrochemicals). Failing to mention TRIPS in 14-mark answers loses 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Confusing "Assignment" with "Licensing"** in Technology Transfer context — Assignment = permanent transfer of ownership; Licensing = conditional, time-bound permission. Marks are lost if used interchangeably.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Page Revision Bullets for KTU Module 2 — IPR**

- **IPR = Legal rights over intangible creations of the mind** (the most concise definition to remember).
- **Two principal branches** (WIPO classification): **Industrial Property** (Patent, Trademark, Design, GI) + **Copyright** (literary, artistic, musical, software).
- **Trade Secret** is the *third*, un-registered, branch protected by common law + NDA.
- **Patent = 20 years** (fixed), Patents Act 1970; four-pillar test = **Novelty, Inventive Step, Industrial Applicability, Not Excluded (§3)**.
- **Trademark = 10 years renewable forever**, Trade Marks Act 1999; use ® after registration, ™ before.
- **Copyright = Life + 60 years (author)** or 60 years (corporate), Copyright Act 1957; **automatic on creation**.
- **Industrial Design = 10 + 5 years (max 15)**, Designs Act 2000.
- **GI = 10 years renewable**, GI Act 1999; classic example = **Darjeeling Tea**.
- **Trade Secret = perpetual if maintained**; example = **Coca-Cola formula**.
- **TRIPS (1994)** is the most comprehensive global IP treaty under WTO; **India is a signatory** and amended its laws in 2005 to comply.
- **Paris Convention (1883)** gave the **right of priority (12 months patents, 6 months marks)** — the first international IP framework.
- **Berne Convention (1886)** mandates **automatic copyright protection** without formalities in member states.
- **PCT (1970)** enables a single international patent application across 150+ countries.
- **Madrid Protocol (1989)** enables single international trademark registration.
- **Technology Transfer mechanisms** (IP-enabled): **Assignment (permanent)** vs **Licensing (temporary, royalty-based)**.
- **Royalty range (industry benchmark):** 2–8% of net sales for patent licensing; 5–10% for trademark/franchise.
- **WIPO** is the UN body administering international IP treaties; headquartered in **Geneva, Switzerland**.
- **Indian Patent Act Section 3(d)** is famous for preventing **evergreening** of pharmaceutical patents (Novartis v. Union of India, 2013).
- **Indian Patent Act Section 3(k)** excludes *"computer programs per se"* from patentability — but software *as part of a hardware invention* can be patented.
- **Infringement remedies (India):** civil (injunction, damages, accounts of profits), criminal (imprisonment up to 3 years + fine for trademark/copyright wilful piracy), administrative (customs seizure of counterfeit goods).
- **Key strategic insight:** One product → Multiple IPs (e.g., smartphone = patent + design + trademark + copyright + trade secret). This is called an **"IP stack"** and is the foundation of modern tech-business strategy.
- **For KTU 14-mark answers:** always structure as **Introduction → Body (with sub-headings and table) → Conclusion**; cite **statutes with year**; use **Mermaid diagram or matrix table** wherever possible to earn easy presentation marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: WIPO
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT416 - EXTENSION AND TRANSFER OF TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** WIPO # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **WIPO (World Intellectual Property Organization)** is a **specialized agency of the United Nations (UN)** established by the **WIPO Convention, signed in Stockholm on 14 July 1967**, which came into force on **26 April 1970**. Its headquarters are located in **Geneva, Switzerland**. WIPO is the global forum for **intellectual property (IP) services, policy, information, and cooperation**. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEFTT416)**, WIPO is studied as the **central international institutional mechanism** that governs the **creation, protection, enforcement, and transfer of intellectual property rights (IPR)** across national boundaries — making it the most critical entity in the international technology transfer ecosystem. ### Core Mandate of WIPO > [!NOTE] > WIPO's twin objectives are: > 1. **To promote the protection of intellectual property worldwide** through cooperation among states and intergovernmental organizations. > 2. **To ensure administrative cooperation among the unions for IP** established by the various multilateral treaties (e.g., Paris Union, Berne Union, Madrid Union, PCT Union). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine the world of innovation as a massive **international postal system** where inventors, artists, and brands constantly ship their "products of the mind" (patents, trademarks, designs, copyrights) across borders. Without a common rulebook, every country would speak a different language of protection — an Indian patent would have no automatic recognition in Japan, and a French trademark would have to be re-filed in 193 countries one-by-one. **WIPO acts as the "Universal Postal Union" for Intellectual Property** — it is the **single global platform** that: - Drafts the **common rules** (treaties) that countries sign onto, - Runs the **central filing offices** (e.g., PCT, Madrid, Hague) that allow ONE application to seek protection in many countries, - Provides the **classification systems** (Nice, IPC, Locarno) so that "an invention" or "a trademark" is described in the same technical language everywhere, - Acts as a **neutral forum** for settling disputes and shaping future IP policy. Just as a courier service has no trucks of its own but **coordinates the network**, WIPO does not own or grant IP rights itself — **national/regional offices grant the rights** — WIPO provides the **harmonized international framework** that makes the system work. ## 1.3 Key Standardized Facts and Metrics - **Founded:** 1967 (Convention signed) | **Operational:** 1970 - **Headquarters:** **Geneva, Switzerland** - **Membership:** **193 Member States** (as of 2024) — making it one of the **largest UN specialized agencies** - **Treaties Administered:** **26 international treaties** (as of recent updates) - **Official Languages:** **Arabic, Chinese, English, French, Russian, Spanish** (6 languages) - **Treaty Signatories (Madrid Protocol):** **130+ countries** - **PCT Filings (Annual):** Approximately **270,000+ international applications per year** - **Annual Budget:** Approx. **CHF 470 million** (Swiss Francs) > [!NOTE] > WIPO is **not a granting authority**. It does not issue patents or register trademarks globally. **It facilitates the process** through international filing systems; actual grants come from national IP offices. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Historical Evolution of WIPO WIPO did not emerge in a vacuum. Its creation was the culmination of a century-long effort to harmonize IP across borders: - **1883** — **Paris Convention** for the Protection of Industrial Property (first major IP treaty). - **1886** — **Berne Convention** for the Protection of Literary and Artistic Works. - **1891** — **Madrid Agreement** for trademarks. - **1967** — **WIPO Convention signed** in Stockholm. - **1970** — WIPO Convention enters into force; WIPO becomes operational. - **1974** — WIPO becomes a **specialized agency of the United Nations**, becoming the institutional successor to the **United International Bureaux for the Protection of Intellectual Property (BIRPI)**, which was founded in 1893. - **1994** — WIPO becomes a member of the **WTO TRIPS Council** observer capacity; the **TRIPS Agreement (1995)** ties IP to global trade. - **2000s-Present** — Expansion of WIPO's Development Agenda (2007) to integrate IP with development goals. ## 2.2 Organizational Structure of WIPO WIPO follows a structured governance model: ### A. General Assembly - Composed of all **Member States** that are parties to the WIPO Convention. - Meets in **ordinary session every two years** (odd years). - Highest decision-making body. ### B. Coordination Committee - Composed of **83 Member States** (elected by the General Assembly). - Advises on administrative and operational matters. - Meets annually. ### C. Program and Budget Committee - Reviews WIPO's **biennial program and budget**. - Approves financial allocations. ### D. Committee on Development and Intellectual Property (CDIP) - Oversees implementation of the **WIPO Development Agenda** (adopted 2007). ### E. Standing Committees - **SCP** — Standing Committee on the Law of Patents - **SCT** — Standing Committee on the Law of Trademarks, Industrial Designs and Geographical Indications - **SCCR** — Standing Committee on Copyright and Related Rights - **CWS** — Committee on WIPO Standards ### F. International Bureau (Secretariat) - Headed by the **Director General** (currently Daren Tang, Singapore; since 2020). - Located in Geneva. - Implements the programs and operates the international registration systems. ## 2.3 Core Functions of WIPO WIPO performs **four major functions** relevant to technology transfer: 1. **Norm-setting (Rule Making):** Develops and updates international IP treaties and standards. 2. **Global Registration Services:** Operates the **PCT, Madrid, Hague, Lisbon** systems for international IP filings. 3. **Policy & Technical Cooperation:** Provides legal/technical assistance to developing countries and LDCs. 4. **Information & Knowledge Dissemination:** Maintains databases (PATENTSCOPE, WIPO Lex, Global Brand Database, etc.) that enable technology transfer by making IP information freely searchable. ## 2.4 Key Treaties Administered by WIPO > [!IMPORTANT] > WIPO administers treaties that directly govern **technology transfer** mechanisms. The following are the most critical from a KTU exam perspective: ### A. Patent-Related Treaties - **Paris Convention (1883)** — Provides **national treatment** and **right of priority** (12 months) for patents. - **Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT) — 1970** — Single international patent application valid in 150+ countries. - **Budapest Treaty (1977)** — International recognition of **deposit of microorganisms** for patent purposes. - **PLT — Patent Law Treaty (2000)** — Harmonizes formal requirements for patent applications. ### B. Trademark & Design Treaties - **Madrid System (1891/1989)** — International registration of trademarks. - **Hague Agreement (1925/1999)** — International registration of industrial designs. - **Lisbon Agreement (1958/1967)** — International registration of appellations of origin (geographical indications). - **Nice Agreement (1957)** — International Classification of Goods and Services for trademarks. - **Vienna Agreement (1973)** — International Classification of Figurative Elements of Marks. ### C. Copyright Treaties - **Berne Convention (1886)** — Automatic copyright protection across member countries. - **Rome Convention (1961)** — Protection of performers, phonogram producers, broadcasting organizations. - **WIPO Copyright Treaty (WCT) — 1996** — "Internet treaties" — digital environment protection. - **WIPO Performances and Phonograms Treaty (WPPT) — 1996** — Digital rights for performers and producers. ### D. Classification Treaties - **Strasbourg Agreement (1971)** — International Patent Classification (IPC). - **Locarno Agreement (1968)** — International Classification for Industrial Designs. - **International Classification of Goods and Services (Nice)** — Trademark class system. ## 2.5 WIPO in the Context of Technology Transfer WIPO is fundamental to **technology transfer** because: - It enables inventors to **secure protection simultaneously in multiple countries** (PCT), reducing the cost and time of seeking patent protection — a prerequisite for licensing and cross-border technology transfer. - It provides **standardized classification systems** so that a technology disclosed in a Brazilian patent is searchable and understandable to a Japanese licensee. - Through its **Development Agenda (2007)**, WIPO promotes **balanced IP regimes** that support access to technology in developing countries. - The **WIPO Re:Search Consortium** provides a platform for sharing **IP assets for neglected tropical disease research** — a model of open technology transfer. ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Reference Table (WIPO at a Glance) | **Parameter** | **Detail** | |---|---| | Full Name | World Intellectual Property Organization | | Established | 1967 (Convention signed, Stockholm) | | Came into force | 26 April 1970 | | Headquarters | Geneva, Switzerland | | Type | UN Specialized Agency | | Member States | 193 | | Number of Treaties Administered | 26 | | Director General (Current) | Daren Tang (Singapore) | | Predecessor Organization | BIRPI (Bureaux Internationaux Réunis pour la Protection de la Propriété Intellectuelle), 1893 | | Became UN Agency | 1974 | | Main International Filing Systems | PCT, Madrid, Hague, Lisbon | | Major Patent Treaty | Paris Convention, PCT | | Major Trademark Treaty | Madrid System | | Major Design Treaty | Hague Agreement | | Major Copyright Treaty | Berne Convention | | Official Languages | Arabic, Chinese, English, French, Russian, Spanish (6) | | Development Agenda Adopted | 2007 | | WIPO Lex | Database of IP laws and treaties | | PATENTSCOPE | Global patent search engine | | TRIPS Linkage | Observer status at WTO TRIPS Council | ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility WIPO directly impacts engineering practice in the following ways: - **Patent Search before R&D:** Engineers use **PATENTSCOPE** to conduct **freedom-to-operate (FTO)** analysis before launching a new product. - **PCT Filings for Global Startups:** An Indian engineering startup seeking to enter the EU and US markets can file **one PCT application** within 12 months of their first Indian filing — saving millions in legal costs. - **Standardized Trademarks:** Manufacturing companies (e.g., Tata, Mahindra, Apple, Samsung) use the **Madrid System** to protect their brands across continents through a single application. - **Industrial Design Protection:** Product designers (automotive, electronics, fashion-tech) use the **Hague System** to protect the aesthetic appearance of products internationally. --- # SECTION 3: Analytical Matrix & Step-by-Step Reasoning ## 3.1 The Logical Framework: Why WIPO Exists (Cause-Effect Analysis) > [!IMPORTANT] > WIPO exists because of **five cascading logical problems** in international IP: **Step 1 — The National Sovereignty Problem** Each country has its own patent/trademark/copyright law. Without coordination, an inventor must file separately in every country, which is **expensive, slow, and inefficient**. **Step 2 — The Asymmetric Information Problem** Without a unified classification, a patent granted in Country A may be written in a technical language and format that Country B cannot efficiently search or evaluate. **Step 3 — The Priority Race Problem** Two inventors in two countries might independently invent the same technology. Without priority rules, the first to file in any given country wins — leading to a "race" and wasteful duplication. **Step 4 — The Enforcement Vacuum** A right granted in one country has no automatic effect in another. Without treaty frameworks, cross-border infringement and piracy are nearly impossible to police. **Step 5 — The Development Imbalance Problem** Developing countries lack institutional capacity to draft modern IP laws. WIPO provides **technical assistance** to bridge this gap. **Result:** WIPO provides the **centralized institutional solution** to all five problems through **treaties (legal), classification systems (information), and registration services (administrative)**. ## 3.2 The PCT Filing Process — Step-by-Step Logic (Core WIPO Service) The **Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT)** is WIPO's flagship service for technology transfer. A step-by-step breakdown: 1. **Step 1 — First Filing (Priority Date):** The inventor/applicant files a regular patent application in a national/regional office (e.g., India Patent Office). This establishes a **priority date**. 2. **Step 2 — PCT Application Filing (Within 12 months):** The applicant files a single **international application** under PCT with **WIPO (IB)** or a competent national office. This is the **PCT international filing date**. 3. **Step 3 — International Search (ISA):** An **International Searching Authority (ISA)** identifies prior art and issues an **International Search Report (ISR)** and **Written Opinion** — typically 16-17 months after priority date. 4. **Step 4 — International Publication:** WIPO publishes the international application **18 months after the priority date** in any of the 6 official languages. The application becomes **PATENTSCOPE** searchable worldwide. 5. **Step 5 — International Preliminary Examination (Optional):** The applicant can request an **International Preliminary Examination (IPEA)** to obtain a non-binding opinion on patentability. 6. **Step 6 — National Phase Entry (Within 30/31 months from priority date):** The applicant enters individual national/regional offices (e.g., USPTO, EPO, JPO, IPO India) to pursue actual patent grants. WIPO's role **ends here** — national offices grant the patents. > [!NOTE] > The **PCT does not grant patents**. It is a **filing and search system** that gives the applicant **up to 18 additional months** to decide in which countries to seek protection, while providing a strong preliminary assessment of patentability. ## 3.3 The Madrid System Logic (Trademarks) The Madrid System is the trademark equivalent of PCT: 1. **Step 1 — Home Registration:** Applicant must first have (or apply for) a trademark in a **Madrid System member office** (the "Office of Origin"). 2. **Step 2 — International Application via WIPO:** Applicant files a single **MM2 form** with WIPO's International Bureau in English, French, or Spanish. 3. **Step 3 — Formal Examination by WIPO:** WIPO checks formalities (classification, language, fees). 4. **Step 4 — Substantive Examination by National Offices:** Each designated country's IP office examines the mark on its own substantive grounds (within **12 or 18 months** depending on the country). 5. **Step 5 — Registration & Maintenance:** A single international registration can be renewed through WIPO centrally. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: WIPO Treaties Mapped to Technology Transfer Stages | **Stage of Technology Transfer** | **WIPO Treaty / Service Involved** | **Function** | |---|---|---| | **Invention Disclosure** | Paris Convention, PCT | Establishes priority dates; single international filing | | **Patent Search / FTO** | PATENTSCOPE, IPC (Strasbourg) | Standardized search of prior art globally | | **Patent Grant (multi-country)** | PCT, PLT | Harmonized formalities; deferred national phase | | **Trademark Protection (global brand)** | Madrid System, Nice Classification | Single international brand registration | | **Industrial Design Protection** | Hague Agreement, Locarno Classification | International design registration | | **Copyright in Digital Works** | Berne Convention, WCT, WPPT | Automatic cross-border copyright; digital rights | | **Geographical Indications** | Lisbon Agreement | Protects product origin-based IP | | **Microbial Inventions** | Budapest Treaty | Recognized deposit of biological material | | **Cross-Border Dispute Resolution** | WIPO Arbitration and Mediation Center | Neutral IP dispute resolution | | **Capacity Building (Developing Nations)** | WIPO Development Agenda (2007) | Technical/legal assistance for IP institutions | ## 3.5 Comparative Analysis: WIPO vs. WTO (TRIPS) A frequent KTU exam point of confusion: | **Parameter** | **WIPO** | **WTO / TRIPS** | |---|---|---| | Type | UN Specialized Agency | Trade Organization (treaty-based) | | Year | 1967 (WIPO Convention); 1970 (operational) | 1994 (Marrakesh Agreement); 1995 (TRIPS in force) | | Focus | IP norms, registration, classification, cooperation | Trade-related IP minimum standards | | Nature of Norms | Aspirational; voluntary accession | Mandatory; enforceable through WTO dispute settlement | | Technology Transfer Mandate | Explicit (Development Agenda, WIPO Re:Search) | Implicit (Article 7, 66, 67 of TRIPS) | | Dispute Resolution | WIPO Arbitration Center (optional) | WTO Dispute Settlement Mechanism (binding) | | Member States | 193 | 164 | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU:** WIPO and TRIPS are **complementary, not competing**. WIPO sets the **normative ideal**; TRIPS sets the **trade-enforceable minimum standard**. Most countries are members of both. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 WIPO Governance & Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB A["WIPO GENERAL ASSEMBLY
193 Member States
Meets Biennially"] --> B["COORDINATION COMMITTEE
83 Elected States
Meets Annually"] A --> C["PROGRAM AND BUDGET COMMITTEE
Reviews Biennial Budget"] A --> D["COMMITTEE ON DEVELOPMENT
AND IP - CDIP
Development Agenda Oversight"] A --> E["STANDING COMMITTEES"] E --> E1["SCP - Patents Law"] E --> E2["SCT - Trademarks Designs GI"] E --> E3["SCCR - Copyright Law"] E --> E4["CWS - WIPO Standards"] A --> F["INTERNATIONAL BUREAU
Secretariat - Geneva
Director General - Daren Tang"] F --> G1["PCT SYSTEM
International Patents"] F --> G2["MADRID SYSTEM
International Trademarks"] F --> G3["HAGUE SYSTEM
Industrial Designs"] F --> G4["LISBON SYSTEM
Geographical Indications"] F --> G5["PATENTSCOPE
Global Patent Database"] F --> G6["WIPO ARBITRATION
AND MEDIATION CENTER"] G1 --> H1["ISAs - International
Searching Authorities"] G1 --> H2["National Phase
Entry to 150+ Offices"] G2 --> H3["Office of Origin
Verification"] G2 --> H4["National Office
Substantive Examination"] ``` ## 4.2 WIPO's Four-Pillar Service Architecture (Mermaid Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR INPUT["Inventor / Applicant
or Member State"] --> PILLAR1 PILLAR1["PILLAR 1
NORM SETTING
Treaty Drafting and Updates"] PILLAR2["PILLAR 2
GLOBAL REGISTRATION
PCT - Madrid - Hague - Lisbon"] PILLAR3["PILLAR 3
POLICY AND TECHNICAL
COOPERATION
Development Agenda"] PILLAR4["PILLAR 4
INFORMATION SERVICES
PATENTSCOPE - WIPO Lex
Global Brand Database"] PILLAR1 --> OUTPUT1["Harmonized
International IP Law"] PILLAR2 --> OUTPUT2["Single Filing
Multi-Country Effect"] PILLAR3 --> OUTPUT3["Capacity Building
in Developing Nations"] PILLAR4 --> OUTPUT4["Searchable IP
Knowledge Base"] OUTPUT1 --> GOAL["FACILITATE
TECHNOLOGY TRANSFER
AND IP PROTECTION"] OUTPUT2 --> GOAL OUTPUT3 --> GOAL OUTPUT4 --> GOAL GOAL --> BENEFIT["Beneficiaries:
Inventors - Businesses
Researchers - Nations"] ``` ## 4.3 Technology Transfer Flow Enabled by WIPO (Mermaid Sequence Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Inventor Creates
New Technology
in Country A"] --> B["File National Patent
in Country A
Priority Date Established"] B --> C{"Decide to Seek
Global Protection?"} C -- "Yes" --> D["File PCT Application
with WIPO in Geneva
Within 12 Months"] C -- "No" --> Z1["Technology Stays
Domestic Only"] D --> E["International Search
Report by ISA

Application on
PATENTSCOPE"] F --> G["Technology Disclosed
to Global Audience

Granted by Local Offices

Technology Disclosure

Royalties Earned"] J --> K["Technology Successfully
Transferred to Adopting Country
Local Production Begins"] ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]** **Define WIPO and state its headquarters. Mention the year the WIPO Convention came into force.** **Model Answer:** **WIPO (World Intellectual Property Organization)** is a **specialized agency of the United Nations** dedicated to promoting the protection of intellectual property worldwide. It was established by the **WIPO Convention signed in Stockholm on 14 July 1967**, and the Convention came into force on **26 April 1970**. The headquarters of WIPO is located in **Geneva, Switzerland**. WIPO currently has **193 Member States** and administers **26 international treaties** relating to patents, trademarks, industrial designs, copyrights, and geographical indications. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, June 2024 Pattern]** **List any three international registration systems operated by WIPO and explain their purpose in brief.** **Model Answer:** WIPO operates the following major international registration systems: 1. **Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT) System** — Enables a single international patent application to seek protection in **150+ member countries**, giving the applicant 18 additional months to decide on national filings. Facilitates technology transfer by simplifying multi-country patent protection. 2. **Madrid System (for Trademarks)** — Allows a single international application to register a trademark in up to **130+ member countries** through WIPO, avoiding the need for separate national filings in each country. 3. **Hague System (for Industrial Designs)** — Provides a single international application to protect industrial designs in **90+ member countries**, simplifying design registration for product designers and manufacturers. These systems collectively **reduce the cost, complexity, and time** of securing IP protection across multiple jurisdictions, directly supporting **international technology transfer and global commerce**. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1 **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, 14 Marks Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the **organizational structure of WIPO** in detail, mentioning its main governing bodies and their functions. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the role of the **Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT)** in facilitating international technology transfer. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Answer to Part (a): WIPO's organizational structure is designed to provide **democratic, member-driven governance** with a professional secretariat. The main components are: **1. General Assembly (Supreme Body):** - Composed of all **193 Member States** that are parties to the WIPO Convention. - Meets in **ordinary session every two years** (in odd-numbered years). - Functions: Elects the Director General; appoints members of the Coordination Committee; adopts the biennial program and budget; determines the policies, direction, and overall strategy of WIPO. **2. Coordination Committee:** - Composed of **83 Member States** (elected by the General Assembly). - Functions: Advises on administrative, operational, and financial matters; prepares the agenda for the General Assembly. **3. Program and Budget Committee:** - Reviews the WIPO's biennial program of activities and budget proposals. - Functions: Examines the Director General's budget proposals and presents recommendations to the General Assembly. **4. Committee on Development and Intellectual Property (CDIP):** - Functions: Oversees the implementation of the **WIPO Development Agenda (adopted in 2007)** which integrates development concerns into WIPO's work. **5. Standing Committees (Specialized Norm-Setting Bodies):** - **SCP** — Standing Committee on the Law of Patents - **SCT** — Standing Committee on the Law of Trademarks, Industrial Designs, and Geographical Indications - **SCCR** — Standing Committee on Copyright and Related Rights - **CWS** — Committee on WIPO Standards - Functions: Discuss and prepare draft treaties, recommendations, and standards in their respective domains. **6. International Bureau (Secretariat):** - Headed by the **Director General** (current: Daren Tang, Singapore, since 2020). - Functions: Implements WIPO's programs, operates international registration systems, organizes meetings, and disseminates IP information globally. #### Model Answer to Part (b): The **Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT)**, signed in Washington in **1970** and currently having **150+ contracting states**, is the single most important WIPO mechanism for facilitating **international technology transfer**. **Role in Facilitating Technology Transfer:** **1. Unified Filing Mechanism:** The PCT allows an inventor to file **one international application** that is recognized in all PCT member states. This drastically reduces the **cost, paperwork, and procedural complexity** of seeking patent protection across multiple jurisdictions — a critical barrier to technology transfer. **2. Extension of the Priority Period:** Under the Paris Convention, an inventor has 12 months to file abroad after the first filing. The PCT **extends this by an additional 18 months**, giving the inventor **30 months total** to assess commercial viability, identify potential licensees, and secure funding before entering expensive national-phase filings. This window is invaluable for technology transfer negotiations. **3. International Search Report (ISR) and Written Opinion:** An **International Searching Authority (ISA)** conducts a thorough search and provides a preliminary opinion on novelty and inventive step. This helps the inventor **assess the strength of the patent** before committing to expensive national filings, reducing the risk of failed technology transfer investments. **4. International Publication and Global Disclosure:** WIPO publishes the PCT application **18 months after the priority date** and makes it searchable in **PATENTSCOPE**. This serves as a **global technology disclosure** platform, allowing potential licensees, manufacturers, and joint venture partners worldwide to discover and evaluate the invention — a direct pathway to technology transfer. **5. Deferred National Phase:** The inventor can selectively enter national phases only in countries where technology transfer opportunities exist. This **strategic selectivity** is crucial for efficient resource allocation in technology transfer. **6. International Preliminary Examination (IPE):** Optional examination by an IPEA provides a stronger preliminary assessment of patentability, giving inventors and potential licensees confidence before commercialization. **7. Real-World Impact:** Engineering firms, pharmaceutical companies, and tech startups use the PCT to seek protection in target markets for technology licensing, joint ventures, and cross-border R&D collaboration. The PCT has become the **preferred route for global IP protection** in 2024. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2 (Internal Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, 14 Marks Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the **major functions of WIPO** and describe any **four important treaties** administered by it. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a detailed note on the **WIPO Development Agenda (2007)** and its significance in promoting balanced technology transfer. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Answer to Part (a): **Major Functions of WIPO:** WIPO performs four core functions that together form the global IP ecosystem: **1. Norm-Setting (Treaty Making):** WIPO develops and updates international IP treaties through its Standing Committees. These treaties provide the **legal backbone** of cross-border IP protection. **2. Global Registration Services:** WIPO operates the **PCT, Madrid, Hague, and Lisbon systems**, allowing applicants to seek IP protection in multiple countries through single international filings. **3. Policy and Technical Cooperation:** WIPO provides **legal advice, technical assistance, and capacity-building** to developing countries and Least Developed Countries (LDCs) for establishing modern IP systems. **4. Information Dissemination and Knowledge Hubs:** WIPO maintains free global databases — **PATENTSCOPE (patents), Global Brand Database (trademarks), Global Design Database, WIPO Lex (laws/treaties)** — that make IP information universally accessible. **Four Important Treaties Administered by WIPO:** **(i) Paris Convention for the Protection of Industrial Property (1883):** The oldest and most fundamental IP treaty. Establishes two pillars — **National Treatment** (foreigners get the same IP rights as nationals) and **Right of Priority** (12 months for patents, 6 months for trademarks/designs) for subsequent filings in other member countries. **(ii) Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT) (1970):** Provides a unified procedure for filing patent applications internationally. A single PCT application is valid in **150+ countries**, simplifying global patent protection and technology transfer. **(iii) Berne Convention for the Protection of Literary and Artistic Works (1886):** Governs international copyright protection. Provides **automatic protection** of copyrighted works in all member countries without the need for formal registration, based on the principle of national treatment. **(iv) Madrid System (Madrid Agreement 1891 / Madrid Protocol 1989):** Allows international registration of trademarks through a single application. A trademark registered under the Madrid System is protected in **130+ member countries**, and renewal is handled centrally through WIPO. #### Model Answer to Part (b): **WIPO Development Agenda (2007) — A Detailed Note:** **Background and Adoption:** The **WIPO Development Agenda** was adopted by the WIPO General Assembly in **2007** (with implementation beginning in 2008), following extensive demands from **developing countries and civil society groups** that WIPO's activities had historically been overly focused on **IP enforcement and TRIPS-plus standards** at the expense of development concerns. **Core Recommendations:** The Development Agenda consists of **45 recommendations** organized under **6 clusters**: 1. **Technical Assistance and Capacity Building** — Strengthen IP institutions in developing countries. 2. **Norm-Setting, Public Domain, and Flexibilities** — Ensure the public domain is preserved; promote flexibilities in IP law for development. 3. **Technology Transfer, Information and Communication Technologies (ICTs), and the Knowledge Economy** — Promote access to knowledge and technology. 4. **Assessment, Evaluation, and Impact Studies** — Conduct empirical studies of IP's impact on development. 5. **Institutional and Mandate Considerations** — Ensure WIPO's governance is inclusive of all stakeholders. 6. **Other Issues** — Address emerging challenges like traditional knowledge and genetic resources. **Significance in Promoting Balanced Technology Transfer:** **(i) Reorienting WIPO's Mandate:** The Development Agenda explicitly added **"facilitating technology transfer"** as a key WIPO objective, balancing the traditional focus on **IP protection and enforcement**. **(ii) Public Domain Preservation:** WIPO now actively studies the **public domain** — the pool of knowledge that is **not protected by IP** and is freely available for use. This is a direct technology transfer resource for developing countries. **(iii) Flexibilities in IP Laws:** The Development Agenda promotes the use of **TRIPS flexibilities** (compulsory licensing, parallel imports, exceptions for education/research) by developing countries — tools that enable technology transfer on equitable terms. **(iv) WIPO Re:Search Consortium:** A flagship initiative where **multinational pharmaceutical companies share IP assets** (patents, compounds, data) for research on **neglected tropical diseases** affecting developing countries — a model of pro-development technology transfer. **(v) Traditional Knowledge and Genetic Resources:** WIPO's Intergovernmental Committee addresses protection of **traditional knowledge and genetic resources** — preventing biopiracy and ensuring fair benefit-sharing, especially relevant for **India and other biodiversity-rich nations**. **(vi) Capacity Building for Technology Absorption:** The Development Agenda funds training programs in **LDCs and developing economies** to build the technical and legal capacity required to **absorb, adapt, and re-innovate** upon transferred technologies. **(vii) WIPO Development Sector:** A dedicated WIPO department now coordinates **development-related programs**, ensuring technology transfer is a **central pillar** (not a side activity) of WIPO's work. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls to Avoid:** > - Do **NOT** confuse **WIPO** (UN agency for IP) with **WTO** (World Trade Organization). WIPO is older (1967) and is a UN specialized agency; WTO is from 1994 and is a trade body. **TRIPS** is a WTO agreement, **not** a WIPO treaty. > - Do **NOT** state that WIPO **grants patents or registers trademarks globally**. WIPO's PCT and Madrid systems provide **filing/search** services — actual **grants are issued by national IP offices**. > - Do **NOT** confuse the **BIRPI** (WIPO's predecessor) with WIPO itself. BIRPI was founded in 1893 and was replaced by WIPO in 1970. > - Do **NOT** state that PCT applications become "international patents" — there is **no such thing as an "international patent"** under PCT. The PCT produces a national-phase-ready application, not a global grant. > - Always mention the **headquarters (Geneva)**, **year of operation (1970)**, and **Director General (Daren Tang)** in definitional questions for full marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Revision Points for WIPO (Module 2, PEFTT416):** - **WIPO = World Intellectual Property Organization** — a **UN Specialized Agency**. - **Established:** 1967 (Convention signed in Stockholm) | **Operational:** 26 April 1970. - **Headquarters:** Geneva, Switzerland. - **Member States:** 193 (one of the largest UN agencies by membership). - **Treaties Administered:** 26 international IP treaties. - **WIPO is NOT a granting authority** — national IP offices grant patents/trademarks/designs. - **WIPO's Predecessor:** BIRPI (Bureaux Internationaux Réunis pour la Protection de la Propriété Intellectuelle), 1893. - **WIPO became a UN agency in 1974.** - **Four Core Functions:** Norm-setting, Global Registration Services, Policy/Technical Cooperation, Information Dissemination. - **Four Major International Registration Systems:** - **PCT** — Patents (150+ countries) - **Madrid** — Trademarks (130+ countries) - **Hague** — Industrial Designs (90+ countries) - **Lisbon** — Geographical Indications/Appellations of Origin - **Major Treaties to Remember by Name and Year:** - **Paris Convention (1883)** — Industrial Property - **Berne Convention (1886)** — Copyright - **Madrid Agreement (1891)** — Trademarks - **PCT (1970)** — International patent filing - **Nice Agreement (1957)** — Trademark classification - **Strasbourg Agreement (1971)** — International Patent Classification (IPC) - **Budapest Treaty (1977)** — Microorganism deposits - **WCT and WPPT (1996)** — Digital copyright ("Internet Treaties") - **PCT Filing Window:** 12 months from priority date to file PCT; 30/31 months to enter national phase. - **PCT Advantages:** Extended deadline; ISR; international publication; selective national phase entry. - **Madrid System:** Requires prior home registration; one application, 130+ countries; centralized renewal. - **WIPO Development Agenda:** Adopted 2007; 45 recommendations in 6 clusters; promotes balanced technology transfer. - **WIPO Re:Search:** Pharmaceutical IP sharing platform for neglected tropical disease research. - **Director General (as of 2024):** Daren Tang (Singapore), in office since 2020. - **Key WIPO Databases:** PATENTSCOPE (patents), Global Brand Database (trademarks), WIPO Lex (laws/treaties), Global Design Database. - **WIPO Arbitration and Mediation Center:** Neutral venue for resolving international IP disputes outside national courts. - **WIPO's Role in Technology Transfer:** Provides the legal and administrative framework that **enables** technology to cross borders via licensing, joint ventures, and cross-border R&D — but does **not directly transfer** technology itself. - **WIPO vs. WTO/TRIPS:** WIPO = aspirational norms + services; TRIPS = mandatory trade-enforced minimum standards. They are **complementary**, not competitive. - **National Treatment Principle:** Foreign IP right holders get the same treatment as nationals in member states (Paris & Berne Conventions). - **Priority Right:** 12 months (patents/utilities) or 6 months (trademarks/designs) to file abroad while preserving the original priority date. > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always structure your answer in a **3-part flow**: (1) Definition/Introduction, (2) Main Body with 4–6 detailed points, (3) Significance/Conclusion. Use subheadings, bullet points, and **bold** keywords liberally — KTU examiners reward **structured, well-organized answers** with **specific years, names, and figures**.

topics.title: Intellectual Properties: Patent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT416 - EXTENSION AND TRANSFER OF TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Intellectual Properties: Patent

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Patent (As per the Indian Patents Act, 1970, Section 2(1)(m)):**
> A *patent* is a statutory right granted for an invention, being a product or a process, which provides a new solution to a problem. It is an exclusive right granted by the Government to a patent holder to exclude others from making, using, selling, offering for sale, or importing the patented product or process for a limited period, in exchange for the public disclosure of the invention.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for the course *Extension and Transfer of Technology*, a patent is positioned as the most powerful instrument of **Intellectual Property Rights (IPR)** that enables the commercial exploitation, licensing, and technology transfer of inventions from R&D labs to the industry.

### Key Components of the Definition

| Component | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Statutory Right** | Right conferred by law (Patents Act, 1970 in India) |
| **Invention** | A new product or process providing a new technical solution |
| **Exclusive Right** | Right to exclude others (not the right to commercially exploit) |
| **Limited Period** | **20 years** from the date of filing (in India) |
| **Territorial** | Valid only in the country/region where granted |
| **Consideration** | Granted in exchange for public disclosure of the invention |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Patents are studied as a mechanism to protect technological innovations, facilitate their transfer between organizations, and generate revenue streams through licensing — making it a central pillar of Technology Transfer.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Land Deed" Analogy:**
> Imagine you invent a unique water purifier. Without a patent, anyone can copy your design and sell it — you have *no legal weapon* to stop them. A patent is like a **"land deed" issued by the government for your invention** — it does not give you the right to make the product, but it gives you the right to **prevent others from making, using, or selling it** without your permission for **20 years**.

A second simpler analogy — think of a **patent as a contract**:
- You (the inventor) **disclose** your invention fully to the public.
- In return, the Government gives you a **temporary monopoly** (20 years).
- After 20 years, the invention enters the **public domain** — anyone can use it freely.

This trade-off between *temporary exclusivity* and *public knowledge* is the philosophical foundation of the entire patent system worldwide.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Intellectual Property (Big Picture)

Before diving into patents, it is important to understand where a patent stands among the broader IPR family:

| Type of IP | Protects | Duration | KTU Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Patent** | Inventions (Product/Process) | **20 years** | Most relevant for Technology Transfer |
| **Trademark** | Brand name, logo, slogan | **10 years (renewable)** | Marketing of transferred tech |
| **Copyright** | Literary, artistic, musical works | **Life + 60 years** | Manuals, software, documentation |
| **Industrial Design** | Aesthetic appearance of an article | **10 years (renewable once for 5)** | Product styling |
| **Geographical Indication** | Goods tied to a region | **10 years (renewable)** | Agricultural technology |
| **Trade Secret** | Confidential business info | Indefinite (if secret) | Know-how in licensing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Distinction:** Among all IPs, **only patents protect functional/technical inventions** — making them the single most important IP for engineering graduates and technology transfer professionals.

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Module 2 — Syllabus Mapping

This topic directly addresses the following Course Outcomes (COs) of PEFTT416:

- **CO2:** Understand the legal frameworks governing intellectual property and technology transfer.
- **CO3:** Apply IP protection strategies to facilitate commercial transfer of technology.
- **CO4:** Evaluate the role of patents in research commercialization and licensing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Criteria for Patentability (The "Big Three")

Not every idea is patentable. An invention must satisfy **three** fundamental criteria, as codified under **Section 2(1)(j) of the Indian Patents Act, 1970**.

### 2.1.1 Novelty (Newness)

- The invention must be **new** — it must not have been published, used, sold, or disclosed anywhere in the world **before the date of filing**.
- Even your **own prior publication** can destroy novelty.
- **Test:** Would a "person skilled in the art" consider it surprising?

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (KTU Valuation):** Students often confuse Indian novelty with world-wide novelty. India follows the **Absolute (Worldwide) Novelty Test** — disclosure *anywhere in the world* before filing destroys novelty. There is **no grace period** in India for academic publications.

### 2.1.2 Inventive Step (Non-Obviousness)

- The invention must not be obvious to a **Person Skilled in the Art (PSITA)**.
- "Obvious" = something a competent professional in the field would naturally arrive at by applying common general knowledge.
- Minor modifications to existing products usually **fail** this test.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** Adding a known anti-corrosive coating to a known bridge design is *obvious* — not patentable. But a new self-healing polymer that autonomously repairs cracks is a valid *inventive step*.

### 2.1.3 Industrial Application (Utility)

- The invention must be capable of being **made or used in an industry**.
- Purely theoretical ideas (e.g., mathematical theorems) are not patentable.
- "Industry" here has a **wide meaning** — agriculture, fisheries, services, manufacturing, etc.

---

## 2.2 Non-Patentable Inventions (Section 3 & 4 of Indian Patents Act)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield List — Memorize This:**

The following are **explicitly excluded** from patentability in India:

1. **Frivolous inventions** — claims contrary to well-established natural laws.
2. **Inventions contrary to public order or morality** — e.g., a device designed solely to commit fraud.
3. **Discovery of any living thing or non-living substance occurring in nature**.
4. **New use of a known substance** — unless it involves a new process producing a new product or employs at least one new reactant.
5. **A mere new use of a known process, machine, or apparatus**.
6. **Admixture resulting in aggregation of properties** only.
7. **Methods of agriculture, horticulture, or animal husbandry** (other than microbial processes).
8. **Processes for medicinal, surgical, curative, prophylactic, or other treatments** of humans, animals, or plants.
9. **Plants and animals in whole or any part** thereof (other than microorganisms) — but **microorganisms, plant varieties, and seeds are patentable** under specific conditions.
10. **Mathematical or business methods, mental acts, computer programs *per se***.
11. **Schemes, rules, or methods of performing mental acts, playing games, or doing business**.
12. **Topographies of integrated circuits** (governed by separate Semi-Conductor IC Layout Design Act, 2000).
13. **Traditional knowledge** belonging to any community (TKDL is used as a defensive database).
14. **Atomic energy-related inventions** (controlled by the Atomic Energy Act, 1962).

---

## 2.3 Types of Patent Applications (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Type | Description | When Filed |
|---|---|---|
| **Ordinary Application** | Standard application without claiming priority | First-time filing in India |
| **Convention Application** | Claims priority date from earlier filing in a convention country | Within **12 months** of first foreign filing |
| **PCT International Application** | Single international filing under the Patent Cooperation Treaty | Within **12 months** of priority date; National phase within **31 months** |
| **Provisional Specification** | Placeholder application establishing priority date | When invention is at an early stage |
| **Complete Specification** | Full technical disclosure of the invention | Within **12 months** of provisional filing |
| **Divisional Application** | Split from a parent application | When application contains multiple inventions |
| **Patent of Addition** | Improvement on a patented invention | Anytime during the term of the main patent |

---

## 2.4 Rights Conferred by a Patent (Section 48 of the Act)

Once granted, a patent gives the patentee the **exclusive right** to:

1. **Make** the patented product.
2. **Use** the patented product or process.
3. **Sell or offer for sale** the patented product.
4. **Import** the patented product.
5. **License** the patent to others for consideration (royalties).

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** A patent is a **negative right** — it allows the patentee to *exclude others*, not to commercially exploit the invention themselves. The patentee must still manufacture or license (or else the patent can be revoked for non-working under **Section 84** — *Compulsory Licensing*).

---

## 2.5 Duration, Renewal, and Lapse

- **Term:** **20 years** from the date of filing the application (not from the grant date).
- **Renewal:** Annual renewal fees from the **3rd year** onwards must be paid.
- **Lapse:** If renewal fees are not paid, the patent **lapses** and is no longer enforceable.
- **Working Requirement:** The patentee must **work the patent in India** (manufacture or license) — failure may lead to a **Compulsory License (Section 84)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Many students write "patent is valid for 20 years from the date of grant." This is **WRONG**. It is **20 years from the date of filing** of the application.

---

## 2.6 The KTU Formula Sheet for Patents (No Math, but Memorize This!)

| Concept | Key Fact | KTU Memory Trick |
|---|---|---|
| Duration of patent | **20 years** from filing date | "20 from file" |
| Renewal starts | **3rd year** onwards | "Renews from 3" |
| Convention priority | **12 months** | "1 year to go abroad" |
| PCT national phase | **31 months** from priority | "2.5 years global" |
| Term of copyright | Life + **60 years** | Not this topic, but know the contrast |
| Compulsory license | **Section 84** | "84 = 8(4)ced" |
| Revocation period | Within **12 months** of grant (for non-working) | "1 year challenge" |
| Opposition period (pre-grant) | Any time before grant | "Open till grant" |
| Opposition period (post-grant) | **12 months** from publication of grant | "1 year post-grant" |
| Working statement | Filed every financial year (**Form 27**) | "Annual report on Form 27" |

---

## 2.7 Utility of Patent Knowledge in Engineering

For KTU B.Tech graduates, understanding patents is critical for:

1. **Technology Transfer** — Patents are the currency of licensing deals; valuation of firms is often based on patent portfolios.
2. **R&D Strategy** — Patent searches (using **Espacenet**, **Google Patents**, **Indian Patent Advanced Search System – IPAIRS**) prevent wasteful R&D on known solutions.
3. **Startup Creation** — Patents are key assets for fundraising and competitive moats.
4. **Employment Advantage** — Many product-based companies (Philips, GE, Qualcomm, Samsung) reward engineers who file patents.
5. **Defensive Publication** — Disclosing an invention in a journal can *prevent others* from patenting it (free, defensive IP).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Patent Filing Procedure

## 3.1 The Patent Filing Process in India (Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown)

The lifecycle of a patent in India follows a well-defined statutory procedure. Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step analytical flow** that students must understand for KTU examinations.

### Step 1: Invention Disclosure (Pre-Filing Stage)

**Logic:** Every patent journey begins with the inventor documenting the invention.

- The inventor writes a detailed **Invention Disclosure Form (IDF)** describing the problem, solution, and how the invention works.
- An **inventor declaration** is signed by all true contributors.
- A **prior art search** is conducted using patent databases (Google Patents, Espacenet, IPAIRS) to confirm novelty.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this step matters:** Filing a patent without prior art search is the single most common reason for patent rejection at KTU examination-style questions as well as in reality — claim 30% of all patent applications in India face objections on novelty.

### Step 2: Drafting the Specification (Technical Document)

**Logic:** A patent specification is a legally binding technical document.

There are two types:
1. **Provisional Specification** — Establishes priority date; filed when invention is at an early stage.
2. **Complete Specification** — Full technical disclosure, including:
   - Title of the invention
   - Field of the invention
   - Background / Prior Art
   - Summary of the invention
   - Detailed description (with reference to drawings)
   - Claims (the legally enforceable scope)
   - Abstract

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Crucial Point:** The **Claims** section is the *most important* part of a patent — it defines the legal boundaries of protection. Claims are of two types:
> - **Independent Claims** — Broad, stand-alone claims.
> - **Dependent Claims** — Narrower claims referring to independent claims.

### Step 3: Filing the Application (Form 1, 2, 3, 5, 26)

**Logic:** Application is filed with the **Indian Patent Office (IPO)** under one of four patent offices: **Kolkata (Head Office), Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai**.

Mandatory forms submitted:
- **Form 1:** Application for grant of patent.
- **Form 2:** Provisional or complete specification.
- **Form 3:** Statement and undertaking regarding foreign applications.
- **Form 5:** Declaration of inventorship.
- **Form 26:** Power of attorney (if filed through a patent agent).

The **filing date** is established on this day.

### Step 4: Publication (Form 18) — 18 Months Rule

**Logic:** The application is published in the **Patent Office Journal** automatically after **18 months** from the priority date. Early publication can be requested via **Form 18** (saves 6 months).

> [!NOTE]
> Once published, third parties can **oppose** the application (Pre-Grant Opposition — Section 25(1)) or raise objections.

### Step 5: Request for Examination (Form 18 — Re-filed within 36 months)

**Logic:** The applicant must **explicitly request** examination. Without this request, the application **abates** (auto-abandoned) after 36 months.

- Examination request must be filed within **48 months** (as per 2024 amended rules, formerly 36 months).
- **First Examination Report (FER)** is issued by the patent examiner.

### Step 6: Examination & First Examination Report (FER)

**Logic:** A patent examiner (from the Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks) reviews the application for:
- Novelty
- Inventive step
- Industrial applicability
- Sufficiency of disclosure
- Clarity of claims

The examiner issues an **Examination Report** with objections. The applicant must respond within **6 months** (extendable by 3 months) with arguments and/or amendments.

### Step 7: Hearing & Decision

**Logic:** If the applicant does not overcome the objections, a **hearing** is scheduled before the Controller. The Controller then:
- **Grants** the patent, OR
- **Refuses** the patent (appealable to the Intellectual Property Appellate Board – IPAB, and now to the relevant High Court).

### Step 8: Grant & Sealing

**Logic:** Upon grant, the patent number is published in the Patent Office Journal. The patentee receives the **Letters Patent** certificate and the patent is enforceable from this date onwards.

### Step 9: Renewal (Annual)

**Logic:** Renewal fees are payable starting from the **3rd year**. Failure to pay results in **lapse**.

### Step 10: Termination (20 Years)

**Logic:** The patent automatically expires at the end of **20 years** from the filing date. The invention enters the **public domain**.

---

## 3.2 The Analytical Decision Matrix — Is My Invention Patentable?

This is a logical flow matrix that KTU examiners love to test:

$$
\text{Patentability} = \text{Novelty} \cap \text{Inventive Step} \cap \text{Industrial Application} \cap \text{Not Excluded}
$$

**Logical Evaluation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } &(\text{Is it new worldwide?}) = \text{YES} \rightarrow \text{Proceed} \\
& \text{else return NOT PATENTABLE (Lack of Novelty)}. \\
\text{If } &(\text{Is it non-obvious to PSITA?}) = \text{YES} \rightarrow \text{Proceed} \\
& \text{else return NOT PATENTABLE (Lack of Inventive Step)}. \\
\text{If } &(\text{Is it industrially applicable?}) = \text{YES} \rightarrow \text{Proceed} \\
& \text{else return NOT PATENTABLE (Lack of Utility)}. \\
\text{If } &(\text{Is it in the non-patentable list?}) = \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{Proceed} \\
& \text{else return NOT PATENTABLE (Statutory Exclusion)}. \\
\text{If all } &4 = \text{YES} \rightarrow \text{GRANT PATENT}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **logical AND gate** is the heart of patent examination logic.

---

## 3.3 Patent Infringement & Remedies (Step-by-Step)

### What is Infringement?

**Logic:** Infringement occurs when a third party makes, uses, sells, offers for sale, or imports the patented invention **without authorization** from the patentee.

### Types of Infringement

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Direct Infringement** | Direct violation of patent claims (e.g., manufacturing the patented product) |
| **Indirect/Contributory Infringement** | Supplying components that have no substantial use except as part of the patented invention |
| **Induced Infringement** | Actively inducing another to infringe a patent |

### Defences Against Infringement

1. **Invalidity** — Showing the patent should not have been granted.
2. **Prior Use** — The defendant was using the invention before the priority date.
3. **Experimental Use** — Use solely for experimental or research purposes.
4. **Bolar Exception** — Use for regulatory approval of generics (very important in pharma).
5. **Licence/Permission** — Defendant was authorized.

### Remedies Available

1. **Injunctions** (interim and permanent) — Court order to stop the infringing activity.
2. **Damages or account of profits** — Monetary compensation.
3. **Delivery up / Destruction** of infringing goods.
4. **Declaration of non-infringement**.
5. **Anton Piller Order** — Surprise search of the infringer's premises.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic Tip:** When asked about remedies, always write **"injunctions, damages, and account of profits"** in that order — this is the standard legal triad valued in board answers.

---

## 3.4 International Patent Systems (Analytical Framework)

| System | Treaty/Act | Key Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| **PCT (Patent Cooperation Treaty)** | Administered by WIPO | Single international filing, defers national-phase costs for **30/31 months** |
| **Paris Convention** | 1883 | Provides **12-month priority** between member countries |
| **TRIPS Agreement** | WTO, 1995 | Sets **minimum IP standards** for all WTO members; mandates 20-year patent term |
| **European Patent Convention (EPC)** | 1973 | Centralized European patent grant via EPO |
| **Budapest Treaty** | 1977 | International recognition of microorganism deposits |
| **Hague Agreement** | 1999 | International registration of industrial designs (not patents) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** India is a signatory to **Paris Convention, TRIPS, and Budapest Treaty** but is **not** a member of the PCT as a *receiving office* for *national residents* (since 2022 amendments restrict access). However, Indian applicants can still file PCT applications via WIPO or the European Patent Office.

---

## 3.5 The Analytic Flow of Technology Transfer Through Patents

```
Innovation → Patent Filing → Patent Grant → Valuation →
Licensing / Assignment / Cross-Licensing → Commercialization
```

This is the **core thematic link** between *Patents* and *Transfer of Technology* in the KTU syllabus:

- **Licensing:** Patentee gives rights to another party for royalty.
- **Assignment:** Outright transfer of patent ownership.
- **Cross-Licensing:** Two parties grant each other access to their patent portfolios.
- **Patent Pools:** Multiple patentees pool patents for shared licensing (e.g., MPEG, Bluetooth).
- **Compulsory Licensing:** Government-invoked license when patent is not worked or is unaffordable (e.g., Bayer's Nexavar case in India, 2012).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Patent Filing Process — Complete Lifecycle Flow
