# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Define a patent. List any four non-patentable inventions under the Indian Patents Act, 1970.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> A patent is a statutory right granted by the Government to an inventor for a product or a process that is new, involves an inventive step, and is capable of industrial application. It confers on the patentee the exclusive right to make, use, sell, offer for sale, or import the patented product or process for **20 years** from the date of filing, in exchange for public disclosure.

**Four non-patentable inventions** *(under Section 3)*:
1. **Frivolous inventions** (claims contrary to natural laws).
2. **Inventions contrary to public order or morality**.
3. **Mathematical methods, business methods, and computer programs *per se***.
4. **Methods of treatment of humans, animals, or plants** (medical/surgical/prophylactic).

*(Any four from the list in Section 2.2 of these notes will fetch full marks.)*

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Differentiate between Patent, Trademark, and Copyright. (Any three points.)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Patent | Trademark | Copyright |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Protects** | Functional inventions (product/process) | Brand identity (name, logo, slogan) | Creative expressions (books, music, art) |
| **Term** | 20 years from filing | 10 years (renewable) | Life of author + 60 years |
| **Governing Law** | Patents Act, 1970 | Trademarks Act, 1999 | Copyright Act, 1957 |
| **Granting Authority** | Indian Patent Office (IPO) | Registrar of Trademarks | No formal registration needed in India (automatic on creation) |
| **Primary Purpose** | Encourage technical innovation | Prevent consumer confusion | Reward authorship |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and typically contains **two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each**. The student must answer **either** the full sub-part (a) **OR** the full sub-part (b), depending on the choice offered.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **criteria for patentability** of an invention as per the Indian Patents Act, 1970. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure for filing a patent application in India**, mentioning the various forms used. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Indian Patents Act, 1970, under **Section 2(1)(j)**, defines a patentable invention as *"any new product or process involving an inventive step and capable of industrial application."* The three core criteria are:

**1. Novelty (Newness) — 2 Marks:**
- The invention must be **new** at the **global level** (absolute novelty).
- Disclosure in any form — published articles, conference papers, public use, sale — anywhere in the world **before the date of filing** destroys novelty.
- **Example:** A new chemical composition discovered but not yet published is novel; the same composition published in a Chinese journal is no longer novel for Indian filing.

**2. Inventive Step (Non-Obviousness) — 2 Marks:**
- The invention must not be obvious to a **Person Skilled in the Art (PSITA)**.
- If a competent professional in the relevant field would arrive at the same invention by applying common general knowledge, the inventive step is lacking.
- **Example:** A **self-healing concrete** is a valid inventive step; merely adding a known waterproofing agent to known concrete is obvious.

**3. Industrial Application (Utility) — 1 Mark:**
- The invention must be capable of being **made or used in any kind of industry** (manufacturing, agriculture, fisheries, services).
- Purely theoretical discoveries (e.g., scientific theorems) are excluded.

**4. Non-Exclusion — 1 Mark:**
- The invention must **not** fall within the list of non-patentable subjects under **Sections 3 and 4** (e.g., business methods, atomic energy inventions, traditional knowledge).

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Patent Filing Procedure in India:**

**Step 1: Prior Art Search (0.5 mark):**
- Search patent databases (Google Patents, Espacenet, IPAIRS) to confirm novelty.

**Step 2: Drafting the Specification (1 mark):**
- **Provisional Specification** — establishes priority date; used when invention is at an conceptual stage.
- **Complete Specification** — full technical disclosure; includes title, field, background, summary, detailed description, claims, and abstract. Must be filed within **12 months** of the provisional.

**Step 3: Filing the Application (1.5 marks):**
- Filed with the appropriate **Indian Patent Office** (Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, or Kolkata).
- Forms submitted:
  - **Form 1** — Application for grant of patent.
  - **Form 2** — Provisional / Complete specification.
  - **Form 3** — Statement on foreign applications.
  - **Form 5** — Declaration of inventorship.
  - **Form 26** — Power of attorney (if filing through agent).
- The **filing date** is the priority date.

**Step 4: Publication (0.5 mark):**
- Automatically published in the **Patent Office Journal** after **18 months** from the priority date. Early publication possible via **Form 18**.

**Step 5: Request for Examination (1 mark):**
- Applicant must file **Form 18** requesting examination within **48 months** of priority (as per current rules). Without this, the application abates.

**Step 6: First Examination Report (FER) (1 mark):**
- Issued by the patent examiner; checks novelty, inventive step, industrial application, sufficiency, and clarity.
- Applicant must respond within **6 months** (extendable by 3 months) with arguments and/or amendments.

**Step 7: Hearing and Decision (0.5 mark):**
- If objections persist, a **hearing** is held before the **Controller of Patents**. The patent is either **granted** or **refused**.

**Step 8: Grant and Sealing (0.5 mark):**
- Upon grant, the patent number is published; the **Letters Patent** is issued; annual renewal fees are payable from the **3rd year** onwards.

**Step 9: Term:**
- **20 years** from the date of filing, after which the invention enters the public domain.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**(a)** What is **patent infringement**? Explain the **types of infringement** and the **remedies available** to a patentee. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **role of patents in technology transfer** with suitable examples. Explain **licensing, assignment, and compulsory licensing**. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**
**Patent infringement** is the unauthorized making, using, selling, offering for sale, or importing of a patented product or process in a country where a valid patent exists. It is governed by **Section 48** of the Patents Act, 1970 (exclusive rights of the patentee).

**Types of Infringement (3 marks):**

1. **Direct Infringement (1.5 marks):**
   - The infringer directly performs an act falling within the scope of the patent claims.
   - **Example:** Manufacturing a patented drug without the patentee's consent.

2. **Indirect / Contributory Infringement (0.75 mark):**
   - Supplying a component or material that has **no substantial non-infringing use** and is known to be intended for the patented invention.

3. **Induced Infringement (0.75 mark):**
   - Actively persuading or inducing another party to commit direct infringement (e.g., providing instructions to assemble a patented device).

**Remedies Available (3 marks):**

1. **Injunction (1 mark):**
   - **Interim injunction** during the trial; **Permanent injunction** at the end, ordering the infringer to stop.
2. **Damages or Account of Profits (1 mark):**
   - Monetary compensation equivalent to losses suffered or profits gained by the infringer.
3. **Delivery Up / Destruction (0.5 mark):**
   - Court order to destroy or hand over infringing goods.
4. **Declaration of Non-Infringement (0.5 mark):**
   - A defensive remedy sought by an alleged infringer.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Patents are the **most important currency of technology transfer** in the modern knowledge economy. They allow R&D-intensive organizations to recover investment, monetize innovation, and disseminate technology to the industry.

**Role of Patents in Technology Transfer (2 marks):**
- **Commercialization** of inventions developed in research labs.
- **Valuation tool** — patent portfolios determine company valuations (e.g., Qualcomm's patent licensing revenue).
- **Risk reduction** for licensees — patented technology is legally defensible.
- **Defensive protection** for SMEs and startups.
- **Enabling cross-border technology trade** through PCT.

**Modes of Technology Transfer through Patents (4 marks):**

1. **Licensing (1.5 marks):**
   - Patentee (licensor) grants permission to another party (licensee) to make, use, or sell the invention.
   - Two main types: **Exclusive license** (only one licensee) and **Non-exclusive license** (multiple licensees).
   - Generates **royalty income** for the patentee.
   - **Example:** Qualcomm licenses its 4G/5G patents to smartphone makers for royalties.

2. **Assignment (1.5 marks):**
   - Outright **transfer of ownership** of the patent from the original patentee to another party.
   - Registered with the Controller of Patents.
   - **Example:** A university spin-off company assigns its patent to a multinational in exchange for upfront payment and milestones.

3. **Compulsory Licensing (1 mark):**
   - Government-invoked license under **Section 84** of the Patents Act.
   - Granted when:
     - The patented invention is **not worked in India**.
     - The patented product is **not available at a reasonably affordable price**.
     - The patented invention is **not worked to the fullest extent** reasonably practicable.
   - **Example:** The famous **Bayer v. Natco case (2012)** — the Controller of Patents issued India's first compulsory license allowing Natco to sell a generic version of Bayer's cancer drug **Sorafenib Tosylate (Nexavar)** at a fraction of the cost.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Patent Questions — Avoid These!**
>
> 1. **Term Confusion:** Writing "20 years from the date of grant" instead of "20 years from the date of filing" — **deduct 1 mark**.
> 2. **Novelty Scope:** Writing "novel in India" instead of "novel worldwide" — **deduct 1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing Rights:** Writing that a patent gives the right to "sell" instead of "exclude others from selling" — **deduct 1 mark**.
> 4. **Form Mistakes:** Mixing up Form 1 (application) and Form 18 (publication/examination) — examiners flag this.
> 5. **Forgetting Renewal:** Omitting that renewal fees start from the 3rd year — this is a high-frequency question.
> 6. **Misstating Section 3 examples:** Writing "agricultural processes" without noting the exception for microbial processes — partial marking.
> 7. **Confusing Patent with Trade Secret:** Patents require **public disclosure**; trade secrets do not. This distinction is **favoured** by KTU examiners.
> 8. **Missing Compulsory Licence Section Number:** Always cite **Section 84** explicitly — KTU examiners expect statutory section references.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Patent**

- [x] **Patent = Statutory right** for a new product or process, granted in exchange for public disclosure.
- [x] **Duration = 20 years** from the **filing date** (NOT grant date).
- [x] **Three patentability criteria:** **Novelty** (worldwide), **Inventive Step** (non-obvious to PSITA), **Industrial Application** (utility).
- [x] **Non-patentable subjects:** business methods, computer programs per se, medical treatments, atomic energy inventions, traditional knowledge, and discoveries of natural substances.
- [x] **Forms to remember:** **Form 1** (application), **Form 2** (specification), **Form 3** (foreign applications), **Form 5** (inventorship), **Form 18** (publication + examination request), **Form 26** (power of attorney), **Form 27** (annual working statement).
- [x] **Publication rule:** Automatic after **18 months**; early publication possible via Form 18.
- [x] **Examination request:** Must be filed within **48 months** of priority (latest amended rules).
- [x] **Renewal fees:** From the **3rd year onwards**, annually.
- [x] **Rights conferred (Section 48):** Right to **make, use, sell, offer for sale, import, and license** — collectively, the **right to exclude others**.
- [x] **Infringement remedies:** Injunctions, damages, account of profits, delivery up.
- [x] **Compulsory Licensing (Section 84):** Issued when patent is not worked in India or is unaffordable.
- [x] **PCT:** Defer national phase costs for **30/31 months**; administered by WIPO.
- [x] **Paris Convention priority:** **12 months** from first foreign filing.
- [x] **Budapest Treaty:** For **deposit of microorganisms**.
- [x] **TRIPS Agreement:** Sets **20-year** minimum patent term for all WTO members.
- [x] **Working requirement:** Patentee must work or license the patent in India; failure invites compulsory licensing.
- [x] **Patent is a NEGATIVE right** — it gives the right to **exclude**, not to commercially exploit.
- [x] **Technology Transfer through patents** happens via **licensing, assignment, cross-licensing, and patent pools**.
- [x] **India's first compulsory license:** Bayer v. Natco (Sorafenib Tosylate / Nexavar, 2012).
- [x] **Key statutory reference:** Indian Patents Act, **1970** (as amended in 2005 to comply with TRIPS).
- [x] **Patent offices in India:** Kolkata (Head), Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Tip:** Always cite **section numbers** (e.g., *Section 2(1)(j)*, *Section 48*, *Section 84*) along with your answers. KTU 2024 scheme examiners award **partial credit** specifically for statutory precision.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trademarks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT416 - EXTENSION AND TRANSFER OF TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Trademarks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Trademarks

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Trademark** is a distinctive sign, symbol, word, phrase, logo, design, or a combination thereof, that is used by a person, business, or legal entity to uniquely identify and distinguish its goods or services from those of others in the marketplace. It serves as a proprietary mark of origin guaranteeing consistent quality and is legally protected under the **Trade Marks Act, 1999** of India, which is fully compliant with the **TRIPS Agreement (Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights)** of the **WTO (World Trade Organization)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** A trademark is an **intangible asset** of intellectual property that confers upon its proprietor the exclusive right to use the mark in commerce for the goods or services for which it is registered, and the right to prevent others from using a confusingly similar mark.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Face" of Your Business

Imagine walking into a crowded market. With hundreds of shops selling similar products, how do you instantly recognize your favourite brand? The answer is by its **logo, tag-line, colour scheme, and unique packaging** — all of which are trademarks.

Think of a trademark as the **"face" of a business**:
- Just as a person's face helps you identify them in a crowd, a trademark helps consumers identify a brand among thousands.
- Just as you trust a familiar face, consumers develop **trust and loyalty** toward a trademarked product.
- If someone else tries to copy or wear a similar "face" (i.e., imitate your brand), it causes **confusion and deception** — which is exactly what trademark law aims to prevent.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The symbol **™** is used for an unregistered trademark to claim intent to use, while the symbol **®** is reserved exclusively for trademarks that have been formally registered with the Trademark Registry of India.

## 1.3 Key Components of a Trademark (KTU Mandatory Terminology)

| Component | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Mark** | The actual visual or auditory sign | "Nike", "Swoosh logo" |
| **Brand Name** | The word(s) forming the mark | "Apple", "Coca-Cola" |
| **Logo / Device** | Graphical representation | Apple Inc.'s bitten apple |
| **Tagline** | Catchy phrase | "Just Do It" (Nike) |
| **Trade Dress** | Overall visual appearance | Tiffany & Co.'s blue box |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Legal Metric:** Trademarks are protected for a period of **10 years** from the date of filing, and can be renewed indefinitely for successive periods of **10 years** each, as per Section 25 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Trademarks (KTU Module 2 Core)

Trademarks are broadly classified into the following categories as per the **Nice Classification (International Classification of Goods and Services)** adopted by India:

### A. Based on Nature of the Mark:
1. **Word Mark** — Pure text (e.g., "Google", "Samsung")
2. **Device / Logo Mark** — A graphical symbol (e.g., the Apple logo)
3. **Composite Mark** — Combination of word and device (e.g., "Coca-Cola" with its iconic script)
4. **Three-Dimensional (3D) Mark** — Shape of the product or its packaging (e.g., Coca-Cola bottle shape)
5. **Sound Mark** — Audio signature (e.g., the Intel chime, MGM lion's roar)
6. **Colour Mark** — Specific colour combination (e.g., Cadbury's purple)
7. **Slogan / Tagline Mark** — Distinctive phrase
8. **Collective Mark** — Used by a group/association (e.g., "ISI" mark)
9. **Certification Mark** — Certifies quality/standard (e.g., "AGMARK" for agricultural products)
10. **Service Mark** — Used for services rather than goods (e.g., banking, telecom)

### B. Categories of Non-Registerable Trademarks (Section 9 of Trade Marks Act, 1999):
- Marks that are devoid of distinctive character
- Marks consisting exclusively of marks which may serve in trade to designate kind, quality, quantity, intended purpose, etc.
- Marks that are customary in established practices
- Marks that are likely to deceive or cause confusion
- Marks that are scandalous, obscene, or against public morality
- Marks that contain the name of a chemical element or compound

## 2.2 Essential Functions of a Trademark (The "Why")

A trademark performs four critical economic and legal functions:
1. **Identification Function** — Identifies the source/origin of goods
2. **Origin Function** — Guarantees that all goods bearing the mark come from a single source
3. **Quality Assurance Function** — Ensures consistent quality across all products
4. **Advertising / Investment Function** — Serves as a powerful marketing tool

## 2.3 The "Doctrine of Distinctiveness" (KTU High-Yield Concept)

This is the cornerstone of trademark law. The registrability of a mark depends on its inherent or acquired distinctiveness:

| Category | Distinctiveness | Registrability |
|---|---|---|
| **Fanciful / Coined** | Inherently distinctive (e.g., "Kodak", "Xerox") | ✅ Highly registrable |
| **Arbitrary** | Common words used in unrelated context (e.g., "Apple" for computers) | ✅ Strongly registrable |
| **Suggestive** | Suggests a quality of the product (e.g., "Greyhound" for bus service) | ✅ Registrable |
| **Descriptive** | Directly describes the product (e.g., "All Bran" cereal) | ❌ Not registrable unless secondary meaning is proven |
| **Generic** | Common name of the product (e.g., "Computer" for computers) | ❌ Never registrable |

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since Trademarks is a legal/management topic, the "formulas" below are **legal threshold conditions and statutory metrics** that examiners frequently test.

| Concept | Statutory Reference | Key Metric / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| **Term of Registration** | Section 25, TM Act, 1999 | **10 years** from date of filing |
| **Renewal Period** | Section 25(3) | Indefinite renewal in blocks of **10 years** |
| **Period for Renewal Application** | Section 25(4) | Within **6 months** before expiry |
| **Restoration Grace Period** | Section 25(4) | **6 months** post-expiry (with fees) |
| **Removal for Non-Use** | Section 47 | After **5 years** of continuous non-use |
| **Infringement Damages** | Section 29-30 | Civil suit + Damages + Injunction |
| **Classification of Goods** | Nice Classification | **45 Classes** (1–34 for goods, 35–45 for services) |
| **Form for Registration** | TM-A | Single class application |
| **Statutory Opposition Period** | Section 21 | **3 months** (extendable by 1 month) |
| **Madrid Protocol (International)** | India acceded in 2013 | Single application for multiple countries |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse the period of registration (**10 years**) with the period after which a mark can be removed for non-use (**5 years**). These are two entirely different statutory timelines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Process — Trademark Registration in India

The registration of a trademark is a multi-stage legal process. Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step procedure** as mandated by the Trade Marks Act, 1999, and followed by the **Registrar of Trademarks, India**.

## 3.1 Stage-by-Stage Registration Process

### **Step 1: Trademark Search**
Before filing, a comprehensive search is conducted in the **IP India Online Database** to ensure the proposed mark is not identical or confusingly similar to an existing registered mark.

$$\text{Search Scope} = \text{Trademark Database} \cup \text{Common Law Search} \cup \text{International WIPO Database}$$

### **Step 2: Filing of Application (Form TM-A)**
The applicant files **Form TM-A** either:
- **Online** through the IP India portal (https://ipindiaonline.gov.in), or
- **Offline** at the appropriate Trademark Registry office (Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata, Chennai, Ahmedabad).

The application must include:
- Applicant's name, address, and nationality
- Representation of the mark
- List of goods/services under the relevant Nice Class
- Date of first use (if any)
- User affidavit (if claiming prior use)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Point:** India follows the **"First-to-File"** system, not "First-to-Use". Hence, filing date is critical for establishing priority.

### **Step 3: Vienna Codification**
If the application contains a device/figurative mark, it is classified using the **Vienna Agreement** classification system for figurative elements.

### **Step 4: Examination by Registrar**
The Trademark Examiner reviews the application for:
1. **Formalities** (correct form, fees, classification)
2. **Absolute Grounds** of refusal (Section 9)
3. **Relative Grounds** of refusal (Section 11) — conflict with prior marks

The examiner issues either:
- **Acceptance** → Proceeds to publication
- **Examination Report with Objections** → Applicant must respond within **30 days**

### **Step 5: Publication in the Trademark Journal**
If accepted, the mark is advertised in the **Trademark Journal** (published weekly).

### **Step 6: Opposition Period (Section 21)**
Any third party may file a **Notice of Opposition** within:

$$T_{\text{opposition}} = 3 \text{ months} + \text{1 month extension (at discretion of Registrar)}$$

The opposition is a **quasi-judicial proceeding** before the Registrar, with possible appeal to the **IP Appellate Board (IPAB)** and subsequently to the **High Court**.

### **Step 7: Registration and Issuance of Certificate**
If no opposition is filed (or opposition is decided in favour of the applicant), the mark is **registered** and a **Registration Certificate** is issued.

### **Step 8: Renewal**
Renewal application must be filed:

$$T_{\text{renewal window}} = \text{Between } 6 \text{ months before expiry and } 6 \text{ months after expiry}$$

## 3.2 The "Likelihood of Confusion" Test (Infringement Analysis)

When determining infringement, courts apply the **Likelihood of Confusion Test**, which considers:

1. **Similarity of the marks** (sight, sound, meaning)
2. **Similarity of goods/services**
3. **Strength of the prior mark**
4. **Evidence of actual confusion**
5. **Channels of trade**
6. **Sophistication of buyers**
7. **Intent of the defendant**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** In **Cadila Health Care v. Cadila Pharmaceuticals (2001)**, the Supreme Court of India held that even a **phonetic similarity** that is likely to cause confusion among unwary purchasers constitutes infringement.

## 3.3 Assignment, Licensing, and Technology Transfer (Module 2 Linkage)

A trademark can be commercially exploited through:
- **Assignment** — Complete transfer of ownership (recorded with the Registrar)
- **Licensing** — Permission to use without transfer of ownership
- **Franchising** — Hybrid model combining trademark + know-how + technology
- **Technology Transfer Agreement** — Trademarks often bundled with patents and trade secrets

This directly links Trademarks to the broader course theme of **Extension and Transfer of Technology**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Trademark Registration Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Trademark Search] --> B[Step 2: File Form TM-A]
    B --> C[Step 3: Vienna Codification for Figurative Marks]
    C --> D[Step 4: Examination by Registrar]
    D --> E{Objections Raised?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Step 4a: Applicant Response within 30 days]
    F --> G{Hearing and Decision}
    G -->|Rejected| Z[Appeal to IPAB and High Court]
    G -->|Accepted| H[Step 5: Publication in TM Journal]
    E -->|No| H
    H --> I[Step 6: Opposition Period 3 to 4 months]
    I --> J{Opposition Filed?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Opposition Hearing and Decision]
    K --> L{Opposition Outcome}
    L -->|In favour of Opponent| Z
    L -->|In favour of Applicant| M[Step 7: Registration Certificate Issued]
    J -->|No| M
    M --> N[Step 8: Renewal after 10 years]
    N --> O[Continued Protection with 10-year Renewals]
```

## 4.2 Doctrine of Distinctiveness — Classification Ladder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Generic Terms] --> B[Descriptive Marks]
    B --> C[Suggestive Marks]
    C --> D[Arbitrary Marks]
    D --> E[Fanciful or Coined Marks]
    A -.Not registrable.-> X[Low Distinctiveness]
    B -.Requires secondary meaning.-> X
    C -.Registrable.-> Y[Medium Distinctiveness]
    D -.Strongly registrable.-> Y
    E -.Inherently distinctive.-> Y
```

## 4.3 Trademark Rights & Enforcement Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trademark Registration Certificate Issued] --> B[Exclusive Rights Granted to Proprietor]
    B --> C[Right to Use the Mark]
    B --> D[Right to Assign or License]
    B --> E[Right to Sue for Infringement]
    E --> F{Infringement Detected}
    F -->|Yes| G[Civil Suit for Injunction and Damages]
    F -->|Yes| H[Criminal Complaint under Section 103]
    F -->|Yes| I[Border Seizure under Customs Act]
    G --> J[Court Order for Damages]
    H --> K[Fines and Imprisonment]
    I --> L[Confiscation of Counterfeit Goods]
    J --> M[Resolution]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define a trademark. List any four functions it performs.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**
A trademark is a legally recognizable sign, symbol, word, or combination thereof that distinctly identifies the goods or services of one enterprise from those of others. It is governed by the **Trade Marks Act, 1999** in India.

**Four functions of a trademark:**
1. **Identification function** — Identifies the source of goods/services
2. **Origin function** — Guarantees a single source of origin
3. **Quality assurance function** — Maintains consistent quality standards
4. **Advertising/investment function** — Serves as a brand-promotion tool

### **Question 2: Differentiate between ™ and ® symbols.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | ™ Symbol | ® Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Meaning** | Trademark (claim of use) | Registered Trademark |
| **Legal Status** | Unregistered, no legal protection | Legally registered with TM Registry |
| **Usage** | Used from the moment of first use in commerce | Used only after registration certificate is issued |
| **Governing Law** | Common law rights | Statutory rights under TM Act, 1999 |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1): 14 Marks**
`[KTU University Exam - KTU 2024 Scheme, Model Question]`

**(a) Explain the step-by-step procedure for registration of a trademark in India. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The registration of a trademark in India is governed by the **Trade Marks Act, 1999** and administered by the **Controller General of Patents, Designs and Trade Marks**. The procedure is as follows:

**Step 1: Trademark Search** — A thorough search is conducted in the IP India database to verify the availability of the proposed mark and to ensure it is not conflicting with existing marks.

**Step 2: Filing of Application** — The applicant files **Form TM-A** either online or offline at any of the five Trademark Registry offices (Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata, Chennai, Ahmedabad). India follows a **First-to-File** system.

**Step 3: Vienna Codification** — For figurative marks, classification under the **Vienna Agreement** is carried out.

**Step 4: Examination** — The Registrar examines the application for compliance with **Section 9 (absolute grounds)** and **Section 11 (relative grounds)**. An **Examination Report** is issued with objections.

**Step 5: Publication** — Upon acceptance, the mark is published in the **Trademark Journal**.

**Step 6: Opposition** — Any aggrieved party can file a **Notice of Opposition** within **3 months** (extendable by 1 month) under **Section 21**.

**Step 7: Registration** — If no opposition is filed or it is resolved in favour of the applicant, the **Registration Certificate** is issued, valid for **10 years**.

**Step 8: Renewal** — Renewable every **10 years** indefinitely.

**(b) Discuss the Doctrine of Distinctiveness with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Doctrine of Distinctiveness**, established in the case of *Abercrombie & Fitch Co. v. Hunting World Inc.* (1976), classifies marks based on their inherent distinctiveness and serves as the foundation for trademark registrability.

**The Five Categories (in increasing order of distinctiveness):**

1. **Generic Terms** — Common names of products; never registrable.
   - *Example:* "Bicycle" for a bicycle manufacturing company.

2. **Descriptive Marks** — Describe a feature or quality; not registrable unless **secondary meaning** is established.
   - *Example:* "Holiday Inn" for hotels (acquired secondary meaning).

3. **Suggestive Marks** — Hint at a quality of the product; registrable.
   - *Example:* "Microsoft" (micro + software), "Greyhound" for bus services.

4. **Arbitrary Marks** — Common words used in an unrelated context; strongly registrable.
   - *Example:* "Apple" for computers, "Camel" for cigarettes.

5. **Fanciful / Coined Marks** — Invented words with no prior meaning; inherently distinctive.
   - *Example:* "Kodak", "Xerox", "Exxon", "Pepsi".

The higher a mark falls on the distinctiveness spectrum, the stronger the legal protection it receives. Fanciful marks enjoy the broadest scope of protection, while generic marks receive none.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2): 14 Marks**
`[KTU University Exam - KTU 2024 Scheme, Model Question]`

**(a) Explain the rights of a trademark holder and the remedies available for trademark infringement. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Rights of a Trademark Holder (Section 28, TM Act, 1999):**
1. **Exclusive right to use** the mark in connection with registered goods/services
2. **Right to obtain relief** in case of infringement
3. **Right to assign** the mark to another entity
4. **Right to license** the mark for commercial use
5. **Right to seek damages** for unauthorized use
6. **Right to prevent dilution** of well-known marks

**Remedies for Trademark Infringement (Sections 29, 30, 103, 104):**

**Civil Remedies:**
- **Injunction** (temporary, permanent, interlocutory)
- **Damages or account of profits**
- **Delivery-up** of infringing goods
- **Costs** of legal proceedings

**Criminal Remedies (Section 103):**
- Imprisonment up to **6 months** (extendable to **3 years** for repeat offences)
- Fine up to ₹**50,000** (extendable to ₹**2 lakh** for repeat offences)

**Border Protection:**
- Customs authorities empowered to seize counterfeit imports

**(b) Describe the international trademark protection system and the role of WIPO and the Madrid Protocol. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**International Trademark Protection Systems:**

1. **Paris Convention for the Protection of Industrial Property (1883)**
   - Provides the **Right of Priority** — 6 months to file in member countries
   - Establishes the principle of **national treatment**

2. **Madrid System (WIPO)**
   - Administered by the **World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO)**
   - **Madrid Agreement (1891)** and **Madrid Protocol (1989)**
   - India acceded to the **Madrid Protocol in 2013**
   - Enables filing of a **single international application** to seek protection in **multiple member countries**
   - **Trademark registered for 10 years**, renewable

3. **TRIPS Agreement (1994)**
   - Mandates minimum standards of trademark protection for all WTO members
   - Mandates protection of **well-known marks**
   - Establishes **national treatment** and **most-favoured-nation** principles

4. **Nice Classification (1957)**
   - International classification of goods (Classes 1–34) and services (Classes 35–45)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Do **not** confuse the **Madrid Protocol** (trademarks) with the **PCT (Patent Cooperation Treaty)** — they are entirely different treaties for different IP rights.
> 2. Infringement under **Section 29** is a **civil wrong**, while under **Section 103** it is a **criminal offence** — mention both remedies for full marks.
> 3. Do not write "trademark" as one word — the correct spelling is **"trade mark"** (two words) under Indian statute, though "trademark" is the American convention.
> 4. Forgetting to mention the **5-year non-use removal clause** under **Section 47** costs students 1 mark regularly.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ Trademark is governed by the **Trade Marks Act, 1999** in India.
- ✅ Statutory term of registration is **10 years**, renewable indefinitely.
- ✅ A mark can be removed for non-use after **5 continuous years** (Section 47).
- ✅ **™** = Unregistered; **®** = Registered — never interchange.
- ✅ India follows the **First-to-File** system (priority by filing date).
- ✅ India adopted the **Madrid Protocol in 2013** for international filings.
- ✅ **Nice Classification** has **45 classes** (1–34 goods, 35–45 services).
- ✅ Opposition window is **3 months + 1 month extension** (Section 21).
- ✅ The **Doctrine of Distinctiveness** has 5 levels: Generic → Descriptive → Suggestive → Arbitrary → Fanciful.
- ✅ Landmark case: **Cadila Health Care v. Cadila Pharmaceuticals (2001)** — phonetic similarity test.
- ✅ Trademark is an **intangible commercial asset** and can be **assigned, licensed, franchised, or bundled in technology transfer agreements**.
- ✅ Form for registration is **TM-A** (single class) and **TM-G** (multi-class).
- ✅ Trademark is one of the **eight pillars of Intellectual Property** under TRIPS.
- ✅ Trademark infringement can trigger **civil, criminal, and administrative** remedies concurrently.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Copyright
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT416 - EXTENSION AND TRANSFER OF TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Copyright

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Copyright** is a form of intellectual property right (IPR) granted by law to the creators of original literary, dramatic, musical, artistic, and certain other intellectual works. It is a bundle of exclusive legal rights vested in the creator (or assignee) of an original work, which protects the *expression of an idea* — not the idea itself. In India, Copyright is governed by the **Copyright Act, 1957** (as amended in 2012), which conforms to international standards such as the **Berne Convention for the Protection of Literary and Artistic Works (1886)** and the **Universal Copyright Convention (UCC, 1952)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Copyright is studied under the broader umbrella of **Intellectual Property Rights (IPR)** in technology transfer. It governs how creative and informational outputs (research papers, software, manuals, designs, course content) are legally protected when transferred between institutions, industries, or the public domain.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you write a beautifully crafted poem and publish it in a college magazine. Now suppose a publishing company copies your exact poem, prints it in their bestselling book, and earns lakhs in royalties — without your permission. How would you feel?

That is precisely what **Copyright** prevents. Think of it like this:

| Real-World Analogy | Copyright Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A house you built on your land | An original creative work you authored |
| Property deed / title | Copyright registration certificate |
| Trespassing laws | Anti-infringement provisions |
| Renting out your house | Licensing your work for royalties |
| Squatters / encroachers | Pirates / plagiarists |

Just as you "own" a house you built, you "own" every original word, note, brushstroke, or line of code you create — and the law gives you the **right to control who copies, distributes, performs, or displays it**, and to charge for that use.

### 1.3 The Idea–Expression Dichotomy (Foundational Principle)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:** Copyright protects the *expression*, not the underlying *idea*.
> - **Idea** = free for all to use (e.g., the concept of "a boy wizard goes to magic school" is not protected).
> - **Expression** = protected (e.g., the specific text of *Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone* is protected).

This is often the single most-tested concept in KTU Part A questions on Copyright.

### 1.4 Key Statutory Provisions in India (Copyright Act, 1957)

- **Section 13** – Works in which copyright subsists.
- **Section 14** – Meaning of copyright (the bundle of rights).
- **Section 16** – Term of copyright for various works.
- **Section 19** – Procedure for registration of copyright.
- **Section 51** – When a copyright is deemed to be infringed.
- **Section 52** – Acts not amounting to infringement (fair dealing exceptions).

The statutory authority is the **Copyright Office, Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT), Ministry of Commerce and Industry, Government of India**, with the **Copyright Board** (now replaced by the **Intellectual Property Appellate Board — IPAB**, and subsequently by the Commercial Courts) adjudicating disputes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constituents of Copyright Protection

For a work to receive copyright protection, it must cumulatively satisfy the following criteria:

1. **Originality** – The work must originate from the author; a mere copy of another's work cannot be copyrighted. The standard of originality in India is the *"modicum of creativity"* test (post *Eastern Book Company v. D.B. Modak*, 2008).
2. **Fixation in a Tangible Medium** – The work must be recorded in some physical or digital form (paper, canvas, hard drive, server, film reel). A spontaneous speech that is never recorded is not protected.
3. **Expression of an Idea** – Only the *form* of expression is protected, not the *idea, procedure, method of operation, or mathematical concept* behind it.
4. **Falls Within Statutory Categories** – Must belong to one of the classes listed in **Section 13** of the Copyright Act, 1957.

### 2.2 Categories of Copyrightable Works (Section 13)

| # | Category | Examples | KTU 2024 Relevance |
|---|----------|----------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Literary works** | Books, articles, research papers, software source code, databases with creative selection/arrangement | Research output transfer |
| 2 | **Dramatic works** | Plays, screenplays, choreography (if notated/recorded) | Edutainment content |
| 3 | **Musical works** | Compositions, lyrics, sound recordings | Educational media |
| 4 | **Artistic works** | Paintings, sculptures, photographs, architectural drawings, maps, diagrams | Extension education materials |
| 5 | **Cinematograph films** | Movies, documentaries, animation films | Krishi Darshan / agri-films |
| 6 | **Sound recordings** | Audio CDs, podcasts, voice notes | Radio extension programs |
| 7 | **Broadcasts & Cable Programmes** | TV broadcasts of Doordarshan Kisan | Mass media transfer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computer software and databases** are protected as "literary works" under the Copyright (Amendment) Act, 2012 read with the Information Technology Act, 2000. This is highly relevant for technology transfer involving digital agricultural apps, GIS databases, and farm management software.

### 2.3 Bundle of Rights Conferred by Copyright (Section 14)

The owner of copyright has **two broad classes of exclusive rights**:

**A. Economic Rights (Commercial):**
- Right to **reproduce** the work.
- Right to **issue copies** to the public.
- Right to **perform** the work in public.
- Right to **communicate** the work to the public (broadcast, satellite, internet).
- Right to **make cinematograph film / sound recording**.
- Right to **translate, adapt, or arrange** the work.
- Right to **sell, give on hire, or commercially exploit** the work.

**B. Moral Rights (Personal, Non-Transferable):**
- Right of **paternity** — to claim authorship of the work.
- Right of **integrity** — to prevent distortion, mutilation, or modification prejudicial to honor or reputation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Moral rights** are *inalienable* and continue to subsist even after assignment of economic rights. KTU examiners frequently test whether students can distinguish these two categories.

### 2.4 Term / Duration of Copyright (Section 22–29)

| Type of Work | Term of Protection |
|--------------|---------------------|
| Literary, Dramatic, Musical, Artistic works (published) | **Life of the author + 60 years** from the year following death |
| Anonymous or pseudonymous works | **60 years** from the year of publication |
| Cinematograph films | **60 years** from the year following publication |
| Sound recordings | **60 years** from the year following publication |
| Photographs | **60 years** from the year following publication |
| Government works | **60 years** from the year of publication |
| Works of international organizations | **60 years** from the year of publication |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield "Formula" Cheat Sheet (Copyright Equivalents)

> *Since Copyright is a legal domain, the "formulas" here represent application rules, not mathematical expressions.*

| Concept | Rule / Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Idea vs Expression** | $\text{Protection} = f(\text{Expression only, not Idea})$ | Exam: A chemistry teacher cannot copy a textbook verbatim |
| **Originality Test (India)** | $\text{Copyright subsists} \iff \text{Independent creation} + \text{Modicum of creativity}$ | *Eastern Book Company* precedent |
| **Fair Dealing Limit (Research)** | $\text{Fair Use} \le \text{Citation limits set by academic norms}$ | KTU thesis literature review |
| **Copyright Term (Post-Mortem)** | $T_{\text{term}} = T_{\text{life}} + 60\,\text{years}$ | Author died 2024 → protection till **2084** |
| **Joint Authorship** | $\text{Rights} \propto \text{Equal share, unless otherwise agreed}$ | Research projects with multiple authors |
| **Assignment Requirement** | $\text{Valid Assignment} \iff \text{Writing} + \text{Signed by Assignor}$ | Section 19, Copyright Act |

### 2.6 Concept of "Work Made for Hire" (Employer Ownership)

Under **Section 17** of the Copyright Act, in respect of literary, dramatic, or artistic works made by an author in the course of employment under a contract of service, the **employer** shall, in the absence of any agreement to the contrary, be the first owner of copyright.

**KTU Application:** When a faculty member develops a MOOC, instructional video, or extension manual as part of their official duty at the university, the **copyright vests in the University** (employer), not the individual faculty — unless a contrary contract exists.

### 2.7 Copyright Infringement (Section 51)

A copyright is infringed when a person, without a licence from the owner or the Copyright Board, does any act that is the exclusive right of the owner.

**Modes of Infringement:**
1. Direct copying (piracy) of the entire work.
2. Substantial similarity (qualitative, not quantitative) — even 5% copied can be infringement if it is the "heart" of the work.
3. Translation / adaptation without licence.
4. Distribution of infringing copies for trade.
5. Public communication (uploading on YouTube, sharing on WhatsApp groups) without permission.

### 2.8 Defences: Fair Dealing (Section 52)

The following acts do **not** constitute infringement:
- **Private use** (personal study, non-commercial research).
- **Criticism or review** — provided sufficient acknowledgement is given.
- **Reporting of current events** in newspapers, magazines, or broadcasts.
- **Use in judicial proceedings** or legislative reporting.
- **Quotation** for purposes such as illustration in a published work.
- **Reproduction for the visually / hearing impaired** (in accessible formats).
- **Parody** (judicially recognised post *Eastern Book Company*).

### 2.9 International Framework Governing Copyright

- **Berne Convention (1886)** — Automatic protection without registration; *"national treatment"* principle.
- **Universal Copyright Convention (1952)** — Administered by UNESCO.
- **TRIPS Agreement (1995)** — WTO members must enforce copyright under Articles 9–14.
- **WIPO Copyright Treaty (WCT, 1996)** — Covers digital / internet copyright.
- **WIPO Performances and Phonograms Treaty (WPPT, 1996)**.

> [!NOTE]
> India is a signatory to **Berne Convention, UCC, TRIPS, WCT, and WPPT**. This global harmonisation is crucial for cross-border technology transfer — e.g., when Indian agricultural software is licensed to a US-based agritech firm.

### 2.10 Real-World Engineering & Extension Utility

- Protects **agricultural research publications** and **extension bulletins** from plagiarism.
- Safeguards **GIS-based soil health maps** and proprietary farm databases.
- Enables **licensing of mobile agri-apps** to farmers at concessional rates.
- Crucial for **distance education** MOOC content, ensuring KTU faculty-developed material is not misused by unaccredited edtech platforms.
- Foundation for **technology transfer agreements** (TTAs) and **Material Transfer Agreements (MTAs)** between institutions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Decision Matrix

> *Since Copyright is a legal discipline, "derivations" are replaced with logical analytical frameworks. The full logical chain is given below without truncation.*

### 3.1 Analytical Step-by-Step: Is a Given Work Protected by Copyright?

**Problem Statement:** A KTU B.Tech student develops a custom-built Android app called *"KeralaFarmAssist"* using publicly available meteorological data and his own UI design. He claims copyright over the entire dataset. Evaluate his claim using the statutory framework.

**Step 1 — Identify the Work**

$$\text{Work} = \{\text{App source code}, \text{UI design}, \text{Database arrangement}\}$$

**Step 2 — Classify the Work (Section 13 Test)**

Each component must be tested:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{App source code} &\rightarrow \text{Literary work (Section 2(o), Copyright Act)} \\
\text{UI design} &\rightarrow \text{Artistic work (Section 2(c))} \\
\text{Database arrangement} &\rightarrow \text{Literary work if selection/arrangement is creative}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Originality Test**

The originality test under Indian law (post *EBC v. D.B. Modak*, 2008) requires:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Originality} &= \text{Independent creation} \land \text{Modicum of creativity} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

- The **source code** clearly meets the test (he wrote it independently).
- The **UI design** meets the test (creative layout).
- The **raw meteorological data** is **NOT** copyrightable — facts cannot be copyrighted (*Feist Publications v. Rural Telephone Service Co.*, US precedent followed in spirit in India). Only the *creative selection/arrangement* of data is protected.

**Step 4 — Apply the Idea–Expression Dichotomy**

The *idea* of helping Kerala farmers with weather data is free for all. The *expression* (the code, UI, creative database) is protected.

**Step 5 — Determine Term of Protection**

For software as a literary work:

$$
T_{\text{term}} = T_{\text{life of author}} + 60\ \text{years}
$$

**Step 6 — Identify Infringing Acts (if any)**

If another developer copies the **UI pixel-by-pixel** or the **entire source code**, it is infringement. If they merely use the *same data* in a different UI with original code, it is **not** infringement.

**Step 7 — Final Verdict**

| Component | Copyright Subsists? | Reason |
|-----------|-------------------|--------|
| Source code | ✅ Yes | Original literary work |
| UI design | ✅ Yes | Original artistic work |
| Raw meteorological data | ❌ No | Facts are not copyrightable |
| Creative arrangement of data | ✅ Yes | Selection/arrangement criterion met |

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Copyright vs. Other IPRs (High-Yield Comparison Table for KTU)

| Parameter | **Copyright** | **Patent** | **Trademark** | **Trade Secret** | **Geographical Indication (GI)** |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|---------------|------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Subject Matter** | Original expression | Invention / process | Brand identifier | Confidential business info | Goods tied to geographic origin |
| **Idea vs Expression** | Protects expression only | Protects idea (invention) | Protects identifier | Protects confidential info | Protects product identity |
| **Registration** | Optional (automatic on creation) | Mandatory | Mandatory | Not registered | Mandatory |
| **Duration** | Life + 60 years (India) | **20 years** | **10 years** (renewable) | As long as kept secret | **10 years** (renewable) |
| **Authority** | Copyright Office | Patent Office | Trademark Registry | No registration | GI Registry |
| **International Treaties** | Berne, UCC, TRIPS, WCT | PCT, TRIPS | Madrid, TRIPS | No specific treaty | Lisbon, TRIPS |
| **KTU Example** | MOOC video content, thesis | New seed germination process | "Kerala" KTU logo, "Pepper" brand | Hybrid seed formula | Malabar Pepper, Alphonso Mango |

### 3.3 Analytical Framework: Determining "Substantial Similarity" in Infringement Cases

The test is **qualitative, not quantitative**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Infringement} &\iff \text{Access} \land \text{Substantial Similarity} \\
\text{Substantial Similarity} &\propto \text{Similarity in "Idea-expression merger" or "Heart of the work"}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Access** = Defendant had reasonable opportunity to copy (work was published, etc.).
- **Substantial Similarity** = An ordinary observer would recognise the defendant's work as a copy.
- The **"Abstraction–Filtration–Comparison" test** (from US *Altai* and applied in Indian software cases):
  1. **Abstraction** — Break plaintiff's work into its constituent elements.
  2. **Filtration** — Remove unprotectable elements (ideas, facts, standard routines).
  3. **Comparison** — Compare remaining protectable elements with defendant's work.

### 3.4 Procedure for Copyright Registration in India (Section 45)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Filing Application} &\rightarrow \text{Form XIV to Registrar of Copyrights (with prescribed fee)} \\
\text{Step 2: Examination} &\rightarrow \text{Examiner reviews for discrepancies (within 30 days)} \\
\text{Step 3: Objections (if any)} &\rightarrow \text{14 days to reply by applicant} \\
\text{Step 4: Registration} &\rightarrow \text{Entry in Register of Copyrights} \\
\text{Step 5: Certificate Issued} &\rightarrow \text{Extract issued to applicant (Section 48)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Registration is **not a prerequisite** for acquiring copyright (per Berne Convention). Copyright arises automatically upon creation. However, registration confers **prima facie evidence** in court and is advisable for enforcement.

### 3.5 Analytical Chain: Compulsory Licensing (Section 31–32B)

When a copyright owner refuses to license a work that is essential for public interest (e.g., educational textbooks, agricultural extension manuals in a regional language), the Copyright Board may grant a **compulsory licence** on specific conditions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compulsory Licence} &\iff \text{Reasonable request refused} \land \text{Work withheld from public} \\
&\land \text{Work relates to Indian language edition} \land \text{After 7 years of first publication}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the KTU-recommended mechanism for ensuring that essential educational content is not monopolised.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Lifecycle of Copyright Protection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Author Creates Original Work"] --> B["Fixation in Tangible Medium"]
    B --> C{"Work Falls Under Section 13?"}
    C -- "No" --> D["No Copyright Protection"]
    C -- "Yes" --> E["Copyright Subsists Automatically"]
    E --> F{"Registration Required?"}

    I --> J{"Objections Raised?"}
    J -- "Yes" --> K["Applicant Replies within 14 Days"]
    K --> L{"Dispute Resolved?"}

    L -- "No" --> N["Refusal of Registration"]
    J -- "No" --> M
    M --> O["Copyright Certificate Issued Section 48"]
    H --> P["Duration Begins Life of Author plus 60 Years"]
    O --> P
    P --> Q{"Work Published or Unpublished?"}
    Q -- "Published" --> R["60 Years from Publication"]
    Q -- "Unpublished" --> S["60 Years from Author Death"]
    R --> T["Work Enters Public Domain"]
    S --> T
    T --> U["Anyone May Use Freely No Licence Needed"]
```

```mermaid
    B --> B6["Right to Make Cinematograph Film"]
    B --> B7["Right to Sell or Hire"]
    C --> C1["Right of Paternity Claim Authorship"]
    C --> C2["Right of Integrity Prevent Distortion"]
    B1 --> D["Licensing and Assignment"]
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    B4 --> D
    B5 --> D
    B6 --> D
    B7 --> D
    D --> E["Royalty Stream"]
    E --> F["Commercial Exploitation of Work"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Infringement vs Fair Dealing Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Alleged Copying Detected"] --> Q1{"Was the Original Work Copyrighted?"}
    Q1 -- "No" --> R1["No Infringement Work in Public Domain"]
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> Q2{"Did the Defendant have Access to the Work?"}
    Q2 -- "No" --> R2["No Infringement Independent Creation Presumed"]
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> Q3{"Is there Substantial Similarity?"}
    Q3 -- "No" --> R3["No Infringement De Minimis Use"]
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> Q4{"Does Fair Dealing Defence Apply Section 52?"}
    Q4 -- "No" --> R4["Infringement Established Civil and Criminal Liability"]
    Q4 -- "Yes" --> Q5{"Purpose of Use?"}
    Q5 -- "Private Study Research" --> R5["Permitted Acknowledgement Required"]
    Q5 -- "Criticism or Review" --> R6["Permitted with Sufficient Acknowledgement"]
    Q5 -- "Reporting Current Events" --> R7["Permitted"]
    Q5 -- "Judicial or Legislative Use" --> R8["Permitted"]
    Q5 -- "Commercial Exploitation" --> R9["Not Permitted Infringement"]
    R4 --> Penalties["Damages Injunction Account of Profits Criminal Prosecution under Section 63"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Copyright in Technology Transfer Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    B --> C{"Type of Output"}

    C -- "Research Paper" --> D2["Copyright as Literary Work"]
    C -- "Extension Video" --> D3["Copyright as Cinematograph Film"]
    C -- "Database" --> D4["Copyright as Compiled Work"]
    D1 --> E["Technology Transfer Office KTU or Univ"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F{"Mode of Transfer"}
    F -- "Licensing" --> G1["Royalty Income to Inventor and University"]
    F -- "Assignment" --> G2["Outright Sale of Rights"]
    F -- "Open Source" --> G3["Public Access with Permissive Licence"]
    F -- "MTA Material Transfer" --> G4["Specified Research Use Only"]

    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
```
