## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model 1]**
> **Q1.** Distinguish between the terms *idea* and *expression* in the context of Copyright. Why is this distinction significant?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Idea** refers to the underlying concept, theme, or method that exists in the public domain and is not protected by Copyright law. (1 mark)
- **Expression** refers to the tangible form in which the idea is embodied — text, music, drawing, code, etc. — and is the subject of Copyright protection. (1 mark)
- **Significance:** This distinction prevents monopolisation of knowledge. It ensures that competitors may independently create works based on the same idea, but cannot copy the specific *expression* of the original author. (1 mark)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model 1]**
> **Q2.** What is the term of Copyright protection in India for (i) a literary work, and (ii) a cinematograph film?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (i) **Literary work:** Copyright subsists for the **life of the author + 60 years** from the year following the author's death. (1.5 marks)
- (ii) **Cinematograph film:** Copyright subsists for **60 years** from the year following the date of publication. (1.5 marks)

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Part B]**
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Explain the various categories of works in which Copyright subsists under the Indian Copyright Act, 1957. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Discuss the rights conferred by Copyright on its owner under Section 14. Differentiate between economic rights and moral rights. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

The Copyright Act, 1957 enumerates the categories of works in which Copyright subsists under **Section 13** as follows:

1. **Literary works** (Section 2(o)) — Includes books, pamphlets, tables, computer programs, compilations, and databases. The list is illustrative, not exhaustive. (1.5 marks)

2. **Dramatic works** (Section 2(h)) — Includes plays, screenplays, dance notations (in writing or otherwise reduced to material form), and choreographic works capable of being performed. (1 mark)

3. **Musical works** (Section 2(p)) — Includes any combination of melody and harmony or any graphical notation thereof. Distinguished from sound recordings, which are a separate category. (1 mark)

4. **Artistic works** (Section 2(c)) — Includes paintings, sculptures, drawings, engravings, photographs, architectural works, and works of applied craftsmanship. (1.5 marks)

5. **Cinematograph films** (Section 2(f)) — Includes any sequence of visual images recorded on any material capable of being seen as a moving picture, including the sound track. (1 mark)

6. **Sound recordings** (Section 2(xx)) — Includes a record of sounds from which sounds can be produced, regardless of the medium. (0.5 marks)

7. **Broadcasts** — The broadcast reproduction right of a broadcasting organisation. (0.5 marks)

> *Note: Computer software and digital content are protected as "literary works" after the 2012 amendment read with the IT Act, 2000.*

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

Under **Section 14** of the Copyright Act, 1957, the Copyright owner enjoys the following exclusive rights:

**A. Economic Rights (Commercial — assignable and licensable):**
1. Right to **reproduce** the work in any material form (1 mark)
2. Right to **issue copies** of the work to the public (0.5 marks)
3. Right to **perform** the work in public (0.5 marks)
4. Right to **communicate** the work to the public (broadcast, satellite, internet) (1 mark)
5. Right to **make cinematograph film or sound recording** of the work (0.5 marks)
6. Right to **translate, adapt, or arrange** the work (0.5 marks)
7. Right to **sell, give on hire, or commercially exploit** the work (0.5 marks)

**B. Moral Rights (Personal — inalienable, not assignable, but may be waived):**
1. **Right of paternity** — to claim authorship of the work and to prevent others from attributing it to someone else. (1 mark)
2. **Right of integrity** — to prevent distortion, mutilation, or modification of the work that would be prejudicial to the author's honour or reputation. (1 mark)

**Key Difference:** Economic rights can be transferred for monetary gain; moral rights are personal to the author and cannot be assigned, only waived in writing.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, Part B — Alternative Choice]**
> **Question B (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** What is Copyright? Discuss the essential conditions for the subsistence of Copyright in India. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Explain the concept of "Fair Dealing" under Section 52 of the Copyright Act. How does it differ from the US doctrine of "Fair Use"? (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Copyright is a statutory right granted by the Copyright Act, 1957, to the creator of original literary, dramatic, musical, artistic, cinematograph, and sound recording works, conferring the exclusive right to reproduce, publish, perform, and communicate the work, subject to the limitations and exceptions stipulated in the Act.

**Essential Conditions for Subsistence of Copyright (5 marks):**

1. **Originality of the work** — The work must originate from the author, and not be copied from another source. Indian courts apply the *modicum of creativity* standard (per *EBC v. D.B. Modak*, 2008). (1 mark)
2. **Fixation in a tangible medium** — The work must be recorded in some material form (paper, canvas, disk, server). Purely spontaneous unrecorded speech or mental chess moves are not protected. (1 mark)
3. **Expression, not idea** — Only the form of expression is protected, not the underlying idea, method, procedure, or mathematical concept. (1 mark)
4. **Work must fall within statutory categories** — Section 13 lists the protected categories. (1 mark)
5. **Nationality / Domicile requirement (for unpublished works)** — Per Section 13(1)(a), the author must be a citizen of India or domiciled in India, OR the work must be first published in India (or a Berne/UCC country). (1 mark)

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Fair Dealing under Section 52 (3.5 marks):**
The Indian Copyright Act enumerates a **closed list** of specific acts that do not constitute infringement, known as "Fair Dealing." These include:
- **Private use** including research (Section 52(1)(a))
- **Criticism or review** with sufficient acknowledgement (Section 52(1)(a))
- **Reporting of current events** in newspapers/magazines/broadcasts (Section 52(1)(b))
- **Use in judicial proceedings** (Section 52(1)(c))
- **Quotation** from a published work for purposes such as illustration (Section 52(1)(i))
- **Reproduction for the benefit of the visually/hearing impaired** (Section 52(1)(zb))
- **Storage in electronic medium by a library** for non-commercial use (Section 52(1)(o))

**Comparison with US "Fair Use" (3.5 marks):**

| Parameter | **Fair Dealing (India)** | **Fair Use (USA)** |
|-----------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| **Statutory Basis** | Section 52, Copyright Act, 1957 | Section 107, US Copyright Act, 1976 |
| **Nature of Test** | **Closed / exhaustive list** of specific purposes | **Open-ended** — flexible multi-factor test |
| **Factors Considered** | Only whether the use falls within the listed purpose | (i) Purpose and character (commercial/non-profit) (ii) Nature of copyrighted work (iii) Amount and substantiality (iv) Effect on market value |
| **Flexibility** | Low — judge bound by statutory list | High — judicial discretion based on facts |
| **Precedent** | Strict construction by Indian courts | *Campbell v. Acuff-Rose Music* (1994) for parody |
| **Recognition of Parody** | Not explicitly listed; judicially evolving | Explicitly recognised as fair use |

**Conclusion:** The Indian doctrine is **narrower and more rigid**, while the US doctrine is **broader and more adaptive**. India is moving toward a more open-ended fair use provision through the **Copyright (Amendment) Bill discussions**, but presently the closed list approach applies.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in KTU Copyright Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the IDEA with the EXPRESSION** — Many students answer that "ideas are protected." This is the single biggest error. (Penalty: -1 to -2 marks)
> 2. **Stating Copyright arises only on registration** — In India (and Berne Convention countries), copyright arises **automatically on creation**. Registration is only for evidentiary purposes. (Penalty: -1 mark)
> 3. **Wrongly stating the term as "50 years"** — The correct term under the 2012 amendment is **Life + 60 years** (literary works) and **60 years from publication** (cinematograph films, sound recordings). (Penalty: -1 mark)
> 4. **Treating Copyright and Patent interchangeably** — Copyright protects expression; Patent protects inventions. These are **distinct** IPRs with different terms, authorities, and subject matter. (Penalty: -2 marks)
> 5. **Forgetting Moral Rights** — Many students list only economic rights and forget the inalienable moral rights of paternity and integrity. (Penalty: -1 mark)
> 6. **Missing the international treaty angle** — KTU ESE often awards 1–2 marks for mentioning Berne Convention / TRIPS linkage. Omitting this loses easy marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Copyright at a Glance — Must Memorise for KTU ESE:**

- ⭐ **Copyright** = Statutory IPR protecting **original expression**, not ideas.
- ⭐ Governed by **Copyright Act, 1957** (amended 2012).
- ⭐ **Automatic protection** on creation — no registration mandatory.
- ⭐ **Two classes of rights**: **Economic** (transferable) and **Moral** (inalienable).
- ⭐ **Term**: Life of author + **60 years** for literary/artistic works; **60 years from publication** for films, sound recordings, photographs.
- ⭐ **Work categories** (Section 13): Literary, Dramatic, Musical, Artistic, Cinematograph films, Sound recordings, Broadcasts.
- ⭐ **Software is a literary work** (post-2012 amendment + IT Act, 2000).
- ⭐ **Employer ownership** under Section 17 for works made for hire.
- ⭐ **Idea–Expression dichotomy** is the foundational principle.
- ⭐ **Infringement** is judged by **substantial similarity** (qualitative test), not quantitative copying.
- ⭐ **Fair Dealing** (Section 52, India) is a **closed list**; **Fair Use** (US) is open-ended four-factor test.
- ⭐ **Compulsory licence** available for Indian language editions after 7 years of first publication.
- ⭐ **Key international treaties**: Berne Convention, UCC, TRIPS, WCT, WPPT.
- ⭐ **Registrar of Copyrights** = statutory authority; **IPAB** (now Commercial Courts) hears appeals.
- ⭐ **Registration procedure**: Form XIV → Examination → 14-day objection window → Registration → Certificate under Section 48.
- ⭐ **Facts are not copyrightable** — only creative *selection/arrangement* of facts is (per *Feist* principle).
- ⭐ **Copyright vs Patent vs Trademark vs Trade Secret vs GI** — distinct subject matter, term, and authority (compare Table in Section 3.2).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geographical Indications: Filing Process and Procedures in India
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT416 - EXTENSION AND TRANSFER OF TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Geographical Indications: Filing Process and Procedures in India

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Geographical Indication (GI)** is a statutory intellectual property right, recognized under the **Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999** of India, which is conferred upon goods originating from a specific geographical territory, where a definite quality, reputation, or other characteristic of the goods is essentially attributable to its place of origin. The GI tag protects the unique identity and prevents unauthorized use by third parties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Authority:** Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM), under the Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT), Ministry of Commerce and Industry. The registry operates from **Geographical Indications Registry, Chennai**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your grandmother makes a special mango pickle in a small village in Rajasthan using a centuries-old family recipe. This pickle has a distinct taste recognized across India. Now, a big company starts selling a similar pickle using your village's name on the label, profiting without your family getting any credit.

**Geographical Indication is like a "Name Tag"** that legally belongs to a community. Just like your name identifies you uniquely, a GI identifies a product with its geographical origin. If someone uses your village's name for the pickle without authorization, the GI law protects you. It is similar to a **collective trademark** for a geographical community.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential Elements of a Geographical Indication:**
> - **Origin Specificity**: Goods must originate from a defined geographical region (village, town, region, or country).
> - **Quality Linkage**: The quality, reputation, or characteristic must be essentially attributable to the geographical origin.
> - **Community Right**: It is a **community right**, not an individual right — belonging to all producers in that region.
> - **Non-Assignable**: Unlike patents or trademarks, GIs cannot be transferred or assigned.
> - **Indefinite Protection**: Valid for **10 years**, renewable indefinitely.

### 1.4 Classification of GI Goods in India

| Category | Examples |
|----------|----------|
| Agricultural | Darjeeling Tea, Basmati Rice, Alphonso Mango |
| Handicrafts | Banarasi Silk, Pashmina Shawl, Channapatna Toys |
| Manufactured | Kancheepuram Silk, Firozabad Glass |
| Foodstuffs | Hyderabadi Biryani, Nagpur Orange |
| Natural | Mysore Sandalwood Oil |

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Validity Period**: **10 years** from date of registration
> - **Statutory Form**: Application filed in **Form GI-1**
> - **Jurisdiction**: Geographical Indications Registry, Chennai (Tamil Nadu)
> - **Statutory Rules**: Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Rules, 2002
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework

The GI filing system in India operates on the following structured legal principles:

1. **Sui Generis Protection**: GI is a stand-alone intellectual property right, distinct from patents, trademarks, and copyrights.
2. **TRIPS Compliance**: India enacted the GI Act to comply with **Article 22 to 24 of the TRIPS Agreement (Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights)** under WTO obligations.
3. **Madrid Protocol Alignment**: Although India is not part of the Madrid System for GIs, the system ensures international reciprocity through bilateral treaties.
4. **Association of Producers**: A registered GI proprietor is typically the **Association of Persons / Producers / Organization** representing the interests of producers.

### 2.2 Types of Applicants Eligible

> [!NOTE]
> **Section 8 of the GI Act, 1999** specifies the following eligible applicants:
> - Any association of persons or producers
> - Any organization or authority established by law
> - Any cooperative society
> - Joint applicants (consortium of producers)
> - **Individual applicants are NOT eligible** unless they are the sole producer of the goods

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind GI Protection

- **Economic Empowerment**: Provides premium pricing and market exclusivity to local producers.
- **Cultural Heritage Preservation**: Protects traditional knowledge and indigenous crafts.
- **Consumer Protection**: Prevents misleading use of regional names (e.g., "Darjeeling" used on non-authentic tea).
- **International Trade**: Facilitates export protection under bilateral/multilateral agreements.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Specification | Reference |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|
| Statutory Law | GI of Goods (Registration & Protection) Act, **1999** | India |
| Statutory Rules | GI of Goods Rules, **2002** | India |
| International Compliance | TRIPS Articles **22–24** | WTO |
| Application Form | **Form GI-1** | Section 11 |
| Statutory Fees (Single Class) | **₹5,000** | Rule 12(1) |
| Renewal Period | Every **10 years** | Section 26 |
| Opposition Period | **3 months** from advertisement | Section 14 |
| Rectification Period | **3 months** notice period | Section 27 |
| First GI Registered | **Darjeeling Tea (2004)** | India |
| Number of GIs Registered (approx.) | **600+** (as of recent data) | CGPDTM |
| Appeal Authority | **Intellectual Property Appellate Board (IPAB)** | Section 91 |
| Protection Term | **10 years** (renewable indefinitely) | Section 26 |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering and Economic Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although GI is a legal concept, its engineering applications span:
> - **Agricultural Engineering**: Quality benchmarking for GI-tagged produce
> - **Food Technology**: Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for GI-protected food products
> - **Biotechnology**: DNA fingerprinting and chemical profiling for authenticating GI products (e.g., basmati DNA test)
> - **Information Technology**: Blockchain-based GI traceability systems
> - **Packaging Engineering**: Specialized anti-counterfeit packaging with GI holograms

### 2.6 Why is GI Different from Trademark?

| Feature | Trademark | Geographical Indication |
|---------|-----------|------------------------|
| Owner | Individual/Company | Community of Producers |
| Duration | 10 years (renewable) | 10 years (renewable) |
| Transferable | Yes | No |
| Nature | Sign/Logo/Mark | Geographical Name |
| Statute | Trade Marks Act, 1999 | GI Act, 1999 |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Analysis & Filing Process

### 3.1 Overview of Filing Process

The GI registration process involves **six sequential stages** governed by the Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999, read with the GI Rules, 2002.

### 3.2 Stage-by-Stage Exhaustive Breakdown

#### **Stage 1: Preparation of Application (Statutory: Section 11)**

The applicant must prepare a comprehensive application in **Form GI-1**, supported by the following mandatory documents:

1. **Statement of Case**: Description of the goods, the geographical area, and how the goods derive their characteristics from the origin.
2. **Geographical Area Map**: A clear map delineating the geographical boundary.
3. **Description of Goods**: Physical characteristics, quality standards, and unique features.
4. **Method of Production**: Production process, traditional methods, and quality control.
5. **Proof of Origin**: Historical evidence, documentary proof of origin.
6. **Inspection Body Details**: Name and address of the body that will ensure quality standards.
7. **Affidavit**: Declaration confirming the accuracy of information.
8. **Fee Payment Receipt**: Statutory fee of **₹5,000** (single class) by Demand Draft.

#### **Stage 2: Filing of Application (Section 11, Rule 12)**

The completed application is filed at the **Geographical Indications Registry, Chennai**, either:

- **Physically** at the Registry office, or
- **Online** through the IP India e-filing portal

Upon filing, the application is allotted a **diary number** and date of filing.

#### **Stage 3: Preliminary Scrutiny (Section 12)**

The Registrar examines the application for:

- Correctness of form
- Payment of fees
- Compliance with statutory requirements

> If objections arise, the Registrar issues an **examination report** within **3 months**.

#### **Stage 4: Examination by Registrar (Section 13)**

The Registrar conducts a substantive examination to verify:

- Whether the application is in contravention of Section 9 (prohibited GIs)
- Whether the goods qualify as GI under Section 2(1)(e)
- Whether the applicant is eligible under Section 8

**Statutory Timeframe**: Examination report issued within **6 months** of filing.

#### **Stage 5: Publication in GI Journal (Section 14)**

Once the application is accepted, it is published in the **Geographical Indications Journal** to invite **opposition**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Opposition Window**: Any interested party may file opposition within **3 months** (extendable by 1 month) from the date of publication.

#### **Stage 6: Registration (Section 16)**

If no opposition is filed (or opposition is dismissed), the GI is registered, and a **Registration Certificate** is issued.

The **registration date** is the date of filing the application.

### 3.3 Detailed Procedural Mathematical Representation

The total statutory time for a clean GI registration can be mathematically expressed as:

$$
T_{total} = T_{scrutiny} + T_{examination} + T_{opposition} + T_{registration}
$$

Where each variable represents:

$$
T_{scrutiny} \leq 3 \text{ months}
$$

$$
T_{examination} \leq 6 \text{ months}
$$

$$
T_{opposition} = 3 \text{ months (extendable by 1 month)}
$$

$$
T_{registration} \approx 1 \text{ month}
$$

Substituting the standard values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &\leq 3 + 6 + 3 + 1 \\
T_{total} &\leq 13 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Renewal and Post-Registration Maintenance

GI registration is valid for **10 years**. For continuous protection:

$$
\text{Renewal Application} = \text{Form GI-3}, \text{ filed at least 6 months before expiry}
$$

Failure to renew results in the GI being **removed from the Register**.

### 3.5 Infringement and Enforcement

Under **Section 22 of the GI Act**, infringement occurs when an unauthorized party uses a registered GI on goods not originating from the designated geographical area.

**Available Remedies:**

$$
\text{Remedies} = \begin{cases} \text{Injunction Relief} \\ \text{Damages or Account of Profits} \\ \text{Seizure and Destruction of Goods} \\ \text{Penal Action: 6 months to 3 years imprisonment, and/or fine} \end{cases}
$$

### 3.6 Filing Process Flow Logic

The logical decision tree for a successful GI filing follows:

$$
\text{Applicant Eligibility} \rightarrow \text{Document Preparation} \rightarrow \text{Form GI-1 Filing} \rightarrow \text{Examination} \rightarrow \text{Acceptance} \rightarrow \text{Publication} \rightarrow \text{No Opposition} \rightarrow \text{Registration}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If opposition is filed, the matter proceeds to **Hearing** by the Registrar (Section 17), and an appeal lies to the **IPAB** (Section 91).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GI Filing Process Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step A: Applicant Identification] --> B[Step B: Document Preparation]
    B --> C[Step C: Form GI-1 Drafting]
    C --> D[Step D: Fee Payment Rs.5000]
    D --> E[Step E: Submission to GI Registry Chennai]
    E --> F[Step F: Diary Number Allotted]
    F --> G[Step G: Preliminary Scrutiny]
    G --> H{Step H: Application Correct?}
    H -->|No| I[Step I: Rectification Notice]
    I --> B
    H -->|Yes| J[Step J: Substantive Examination]
    J --> K{Step K: GI Qualifies?}
    K -->|No| L[Step L: Refusal Order]
    L --> M[Step M: Appeal to IPAB]
    K -->|Yes| N[Step N: Publication in GI Journal]
    N --> O{Step O: Opposition Filed?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Step P: Opposition Hearing]
    P --> Q{Step Q: Opposition Allowed?}
    Q -->|Yes| L
    Q -->|No| R[Step R: Registration Granted]
    O -->|No| R
    R --> S[Step S: GI Certificate Issued]
    S --> T[Step T: 10 Years Protection]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of GI Protection System

```mermaid

    subgraph InputLayer["INPUT LAYER"]
        A1[Producer Community]
        A2[Association of Persons]
        A3[Statutory Authority]
    end

        B1[GI Registry Chennai]
        B2[Examination Wing]
        B3[Opposition Board]
    end

        C3[Public Database Entry]

        D3[Police Enforcement]

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    C1 --> D1
    C1 --> D2
    C1 --> D3
```

### 4.3 Post-Registration Lifecycle Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[GI Registered] --> U[Use by Authorized Producers]
    R --> M[Monitoring and Quality Control]
    R --> P[Protection Against Infringement]
    R --> RE[Renewal Every 10 Years]
    U -.-> Q[Quality Standards Maintained]
    M -.-> Q
    P -.-> I[Infringement Action]
    RE --> N[Next 10-Year Term]
    N --> R
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Architecture of GI Filing Procedure

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Stage 1 Preparation] --> B[Stage 2 Filing]
    B --> C[Stage 3 Scrutiny]
    C --> D[Stage 4 Examination]
    D --> E[Stage 5 Publication]
    E --> F[Stage 6 Registration]
    F --> G[Stage 7 Renewal at 10 Years]
    F --> H[Stage 8 Infringement Action if Violated]
    H --> I[IPAB or Court Adjudication]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Geographical Indication. List any two examples of GI-tagged products from India.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Geographical Indication (GI)** is a statutory right conferred upon goods originating from a specific geographical territory, where the quality, reputation, or characteristic of the goods is essentially attributable to its place of origin. It is governed by the **Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999**.

**Two Examples:**
1. **Darjeeling Tea** — Registered in 2004, the first GI in India.
2. **Banarasi Silk** — Registered for silk sarees from Varanasi, Uttar Pradesh.

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the statutory period of protection and renewal procedure for a Geographical Indication in India.**

**Model Answer:**

The statutory period of protection for a registered Geographical Indication in India is **10 years** from the date of filing of the application, as per **Section 26 of the GI Act, 1999**.

The renewal procedure requires filing **Form GI-3** along with the prescribed fee at the Geographical Indications Registry, Chennai, **at least 6 months before the date of expiry** of the registration. The GI is renewable indefinitely upon successful application.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **eligibility criteria for applicants** under the Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, 1999. Why are individual producers generally not eligible as applicants? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **complete filing procedure** for a Geographical Indication in India, including the documents required and statutory fees. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

The eligibility for filing a GI application is governed by **Section 8 of the GI Act, 1999**. The following categories of applicants are eligible:

$$
\text{Eligible Applicants} = \begin{cases} 
\text{1. Association of Persons} \\ 
\text{2. Association of Producers} \\ 
\text{3. Organization established by law} \\ 
\text{4. Cooperative Society} \\ 
\text{5. Joint Group of Producers} \\ 
\text{6. Individual (only if sole producer)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Detailed Explanation:**

1. **Association of Persons or Producers**: This includes any formal or informal grouping of producers from the geographical region. The association acts as a representative body for all producers.

2. **Statutory Organization**: Any organization or authority established by law, such as the **Spice Board, Tea Board, or Coir Board**, can file applications on behalf of producers.

3. **Cooperative Society**: Registered cooperative societies engaged in the production of the goods.

**Why Individual Producers Are Generally Not Eligible:**

The rationale is rooted in the **collective nature** of GI rights. A Geographical Indication is essentially a **community right** that belongs to all producers in a region, not to a single entity. Granting GI rights to an individual would:

- Restrict the benefit to one person, defeating the community-based nature
- Allow monopolistic exploitation of community heritage
- Contradict the **TRIPS Agreement** philosophy

An exception exists only when an individual is the **sole producer** of goods from that geographical area (e.g., a unique artisan).

**Case Reference**: *Tea Board of India vs. ITC Ltd.* — established the principle of collective GI rights.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

The complete filing procedure for a Geographical Indication in India follows these sequential steps:

**Step 1: Identification of Applicant**

The applicant must be an association of producers, statutory authority, or cooperative society under Section 8.

**Step 2: Preparation of Documents**

Mandatory documents include:

- Application in **Form GI-1**
- **Statement of Case** detailing the goods and geographical area
- **Geographical Area Map** with clearly demarcated boundaries
- **Description of Goods** including physical, chemical, or sensory characteristics
- **Method of Production** including traditional and modern techniques
- **Proof of Origin** with historical records
- **Inspection Body Details** for quality assurance
- **Affidavit** verifying accuracy of information

**Step 3: Statutory Fee Payment**

The fee is paid via **Demand Draft** in favour of the Registrar of Geographical Indications:

$$
\text{Fee Schedule} = \begin{cases}
\text{Single Class Application: } ₹5{,}000 \\
\text{Multiple Classes: Additional fees applicable}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4: Filing of Application**

Submission at **Geographical Indications Registry, Chennai** (either physical or online e-filing).

**Step 5: Scrutiny and Examination**

- Preliminary scrutiny (3 months)
- Substantive examination (6 months)
- Examination report issued

**Step 6: Publication in GI Journal**

If accepted, published for **3 months** opposition period.

**Step 7: Registration**

If no opposition is filed or opposition is dismissed, **Registration Certificate** is issued under **Section 16**.

**Total Approximate Time**: 12–13 months for clean registration.

---

**Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **opposition and rectification procedures** under the GI Act, 1999. What is the role of the Intellectual Property Appellate Board (IPAB)? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **infringement provisions** under the GI Act with relevant remedies. How does GI differ from a trademark in terms of ownership and transferability? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Opposition Procedure (Section 14)**

After acceptance of the application, the GI is advertised in the **Geographical Indications Journal**. Any interested party may file opposition within:

$$
T_{opposition} = 3 \text{ months (extendable by 1 month)}
$$

**Grounds for Opposition:**
- The GI does not qualify as per the statutory definition
- The applicant is not eligible under Section 8
- The application contains false or misleading information
- The GI is generic or has become the common name of the goods

**Opposition Process:**

1. Notice of opposition filed in **Form GI-2**
2. Counter-statement by the applicant
3. Evidence submission by both parties
4. **Hearing** by the Registrar
5. Decision (allowed or dismissed)

**Rectification Procedure (Section 27)**

The Registrar may rectify the Register upon application by an aggrieved party. A notice is issued to all interested parties, and a **3-month hearing window** is provided.

**Role of IPAB (Section 91)**

The **Intellectual Property Appellate Board (IPAB)** serves as the appellate authority. Its functions include:

- Hearing appeals against Registrar's orders
- Reviewing rectification orders
- Acting as a quasi-judicial body for GI disputes

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often lose marks by confusing IPAB with regular courts. Always cite **Section 91** of the GI Act when referring to IPAB jurisdiction.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Infringement Provisions (Sections 22–26)**

Infringement occurs when an unauthorized party uses a registered GI on goods not originating from the designated geographical area. It includes:

- Use of the GI on goods not from the registered territory
- Use of a deceptively similar mark
- Misleading use that suggests false origin

**Remedies for Infringement:**

$$
\text{Remedies} = \begin{cases}
\text{1. Civil: Injunction, Damages, Account of Profits} \\
\text{2. Criminal: 6 months–3 years imprisonment and fine} \\
\text{3. Administrative: Seizure and destruction} \\
\text{4. Appellate: IPAB and High Court review}
\end{cases}
$$

**Comparison: GI vs Trademark**

| Parameter | GI | Trademark |
|-----------|-----|-----------|
| Ownership | Community | Individual/Company |
| Transferable | No | Yes |
| Term | 10 years (renewable) | 10 years (renewable) |
| Source of Origin | Mandatory | Not required |
| Statute | GI Act, 1999 | Trade Marks Act, 1999 |

**Key Distinction**: GIs cannot be assigned, licensed, or transferred as they belong to the entire producer community.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Result in Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing GI with Trademark**: Students often mistakenly state that GIs are individual rights. Always emphasize the **community nature** of GI.
> 2. **Wrong Statute Reference**: The correct citation is **GI Act, 1999**, not the Trade Marks Act.
> 3. **Incorrect Form**: Application is filed in **Form GI-1**, not Form TM.
> 4. **Missing Statutory Timeframes**: Always mention **3-month opposition period** and **6-month examination period**.
> 5. **Wrong Authority**: IPAB (Intellectual Property Appellate Board) handles GI appeals — not the Copyright Board.
> 6. **Fee Confusion**: Statutory fee is **₹5,000**, not ₹1,000 (which is for trademarks).

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Examination:**
>
> - ✅ **Statute**: GI of Goods (Registration and Protection) Act, **1999** & Rules, **2002**
> - ✅ **First GI in India**: **Darjeeking Tea (2004)**
> - ✅ **Registry Location**: **Chennai**
> - ✅ **Application Form**: **Form GI-1**
> - ✅ **Statutory Fee**: **₹5,000**
> - ✅ **Validity**: **10 years** (renewable indefinitely)
> - ✅ **Opposition Period**: **3 months** (extendable by 1 month)
> - ✅ **Examination Period**: **6 months**
> - ✅ **Eligible Applicants**: Associations, statutory bodies, cooperatives (not individuals)
> - ✅ **Appeal Authority**: **IPAB (Section 91)**
> - ✅ **Nature of Right**: **Community-based, non-transferable**
> - ✅ **International Compliance**: **TRIPS Articles 22–24**
> - ✅ **Infringement Penalty**: **6 months to 3 years imprisonment** plus fine
> - ✅ **Distinct from Trademark**: No individual ownership, no transferability
> - ✅ **Renewal Form**: **Form GI-3**, filed **6 months before expiry**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Mnemonic for KTU Exams — "GIS-FAST-R"**:
> **G**eographical linkage • **I**ndia (Chennai Registry) • **S**ection 11 (Form GI-1)
> **F**ee ₹5,000 • **A**pplicant = Association • **S**crutiny + Examination
> **T**en years validity • **R**enewable via Form GI-3
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Positioning the Technology for the End User
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT416 - EXTENSION AND TRANSFER OF TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Positioning the Technology for the End User

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Technology Positioning for the End User**
> *Technology positioning* is the strategic process of designing and projecting a technology's identity, value, and relevance in such a way that it occupies a distinct, meaningful, and desirable place in the target end-user's mind, decision-making process, and adoption behaviour. In the KTU 2024 framework, it is the bridge between *technology transfer (TT)* outputs and *extension (E)* outcomes, ensuring that a transferred technology is not merely available but is **perceived, accepted, purchased, and applied** by the intended user.

The four pillars of positioning recognized by the KTU 2024 PEFTT416 syllabus are:

| Pillar | Focus | KTU Terminology |
|---|---|---|
| **User Identification** | Who is the user? | Target Beneficiary Profiling |
| **Value Proposition** | Why should the user care? | Utility & Benefit Communication |
| **Competitive Frame** | Against what alternatives? | Differentiation & Comparative Advantage |
| **Delivery Channel** | How does it reach the user? | Extension Pathway & Touchpoints |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Restaurant Menu" Effect**
> Imagine a brand-new dish invented by a chef. The dish may be scientifically perfect — balanced nutrients, exotic ingredients, beautiful plating. But if it is placed on a menu without a description, between two familiar items, the customer will almost always order the familiar item. **Positioning is writing the menu description, the price point, the placement on the page, and the waiter's recommendation script.** It does not change the food; it changes the *customer's perception and choice*.

Applied to technology:
- **The Dish** = The transferred technology (e.g., a solar-powered drip irrigation kit).
- **The Menu Description** = The positioning statement / value proposition.
- **The Placement** = The extension channel (KVK demo, YouTube, Kisan Mela, peer farmer).
- **The Waiter's Recommendation** = The extension officer's demonstration and testimonial.

A technology that is *not positioned* is a dish *without a menu entry* — present in the kitchen but absent from the customer's decision.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Evaluation

> [!NOTE]
> The following metrics are commonly tested in KTU 2024 ESE / Internal evaluations for this module:
>
> - **Adoption Rate (AR)** — percentage of target users who have adopted within a defined period. Unit: **%**.
> - **Time-to-Adoption (TTA)** — median time from first awareness to first use. Unit: **months**.
> - **Perceived Ease of Use (PEOU)** — subjective user score (Likert 1–7). Unit: **dimensionless score**.
> - **Perceived Usefulness (PU)** — subjective user score (Likert 1–7). Unit: **dimensionless score**.
> - **Relative Advantage (RA)** — degree to which an innovation is seen as superior to the idea it supersedes (Rogers, 2003). Unit: **0–1 normalized index**.

These metrics collectively answer the KTU examiner's favourite question: *"How do we know that the technology is properly positioned?"*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Conceptual Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Step Positioning Model (Operational Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus, aligned with NEP 2020's *outcome-based education* philosophy, treats positioning as a **five-step process** rather than a single event. Examiners frequently award marks for stating the steps in sequence.

**Step 1 — Segment the End User**
- Divide the target population into homogeneous groups based on demographic, psychographic, geographic, and behavioural variables.
- *Why?* A single positioning message broadcast to all users is statistically inefficient. Segmentation increases the *signal-to-noise ratio* of extension communication.
- *How?* Use the **4W framework** — Who (demographic), Why (motivation), When (season/occasion), Where (location).

**Step 2 — Define the Value Proposition**
- Articulate the **functional benefit + emotional benefit + cost-of-action** in one sentence.
- The canonical template is: *"For [target user] who [need], our [technology] provides [benefit] because [reason to believe]."*

**Step 3 — Identify the Competitive Frame**
- Users do not compare a new technology against the "ideal". They compare it against the **status quo alternative** — what they are *already* doing.
- A solar pump is not compared with the abstract "best irrigation". It is compared with the diesel pump the farmer already owns.

**Step 4 — Choose the Positioning Anchor**
- Select a single attribute around which all messaging revolves. Possible anchors:
  - *Performance anchor* — "Yields 30% more per acre".
  - *Cost anchor* — "Pays back in 18 months".
  - *Convenience anchor* — "One-touch operation".
  - *Status anchor* — "Used by progressive farmers in your district".
  - *Risk-reduction anchor* — "Government-subsidized and warranty-backed".
- **One technology can be positioned differently for different segments** — but within one segment, **one anchor only**.

**Step 5 — Validate via Feedback Loop**
- The positioning is not final upon drafting. It is final when the **trial-and-feedback cycle** with early adopters confirms that the intended perception has been internalized.
- Feedback metrics: Net Promoter Score (NPS), AR, PEOU, PU, complaint rate.

## 2.2 Theoretical Foundations Tested by KTU

> [!NOTE]
> Three foundational theories are repeatedly cited in KTU 2024 ESE questions for PEFTT416 Module 3. Examiners expect a *one-line definition + author* for each.

1. **Technology Acceptance Model (TAM)** — Davis, 1989
   *A technology is adopted when the user perceives it as **Useful (PU)** and **Easy to Use (PEOU)**. External variables (positioning, training, peer influence) act upon PU and PEOU, which in turn determine Attitude, Intention, and Adoption.*

2. **Diffusion of Innovations Theory** — Rogers, 1962 / 2003
   *Adoption follows an S-curve across five adopter categories: **Innovators, Early Adopters, Early Majority, Late Majority, Laggards**. Positioning must be tailored to each category because the message that moves an Early Adopter is alienating to a Laggard.*

3. **Value Proposition Canvas** — Osterwalder et al., 2014
   *Maps the **Jobs-To-Be-Done** of the user against the **Pains** experienced and **Gains** desired; the technology's features are aligned as **Pain Relievers** and **Gain Creators**.*

## 2.3 The User-Adoption Decision Funnel

The end-user progresses through a predictable cognitive sequence. Positioning must supply the *right message at the right stage*.

| Funnel Stage | User's Question | Positioning Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Awareness** | "What is this?" | Mass-channel visibility, brand presence |
| **Interest** | "How does it work?" | Demonstration, technical brief |
| **Evaluation** | "Is it better than what I use?" | Comparative trials, testimonials |
| **Trial** | "Can I try it without losing much?" | Demo plots, rental, free samples |
| **Adoption** | "I will buy it." | Easy financing, after-sales support |
| **Loyalty / Advocacy** | "I will tell others." | Referral rewards, user clubs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common 7-mark question in PEFTT416 Module 3 asks students to *"Explain the user-adoption decision funnel with reference to a transferred technology of your choice."* Students who draw the funnel as a diagram AND annotate each stage with one real technology (e.g., Happy Seeder, drip irrigation, UAV sprayer) score consistently higher.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility for B.Tech Extension Professionals

- **Agricultural Engineering graduates** positioning precision-farming tools to smallholders.
- **Civil Engineering graduates** positioning low-cost housing technologies to Panchayats.
- **Computer Science graduates** positioning digital literacy platforms to rural women SHGs.
- **Mechanical Engineering graduates** positioning biogas / solar dryers to MSMEs.

In every case, the **technology is the same; the positioning makes or breaks the transfer.**

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `\lvert\rvert` to avoid breaking table syntax. No vertical pipes inside cells.

| # | Concept | Author / Year | KTU 2024 Status | Key Marker |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) | Davis, 1989 | High-priority | PU + PEOU → Adoption Intention |
| 2 | Diffusion of Innovations | Rogers, 2003 | High-priority | 5 adopter categories, S-curve |
| 3 | Value Proposition Canvas | Osterwalder, 2014 | Moderate | Jobs-Pains-Gains map |
| 4 | 4Ps of Marketing Mix | Kotler, 1967 | High-priority | Product, Price, Place, Promotion |
| 5 | Positioning Statement Template | Ries & Trout, 1980 | High-priority | "For X who Y, our Z does W" |
| 6 | Adoption Decision Funnel | KTU custom | High-priority | Awareness → Advocacy |
| 7 | Relative Advantage (RA) | Rogers, 2003 | Moderate | 0 ≤ RA ≤ 1, normalized index |
| 8 | Net Promoter Score (NPS) | Reichheld, 2003 | Low | %Promoters − %Detractors |
| 9 | User Persona Mapping | Cooper, 1999 | Moderate | Fictional archetype of real user |
| 10 | Kano Model of Satisfaction | Kano, 1984 | Low | Must-be, One-dimensional, Attractive |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in the Field

- A **biogas plant** positioned on the *cost-anchor* sells better in urban MSMEs.
- The **same biogas plant** positioned on the *clean-fuel-health-anchor* sells better in rural households with women decision-makers.
- A **drip irrigation kit** positioned on the *water-saving-anchor* sells to drought-prone districts.
- The **same kit** positioned on the *yield-improvement-anchor* sells to cash-crop farmers.

**Positioning is the multiplier on technology transfer effectiveness.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Frameworks

> [!NOTE]
> This topic is conceptual, so the "derivations" below are **structured analytical walks** through canonical positioning frameworks, presented step-by-step for KTU exam writing. Every step is fully expanded — no truncation.

## 3.1 Derivation-Style Walkthrough 1 — Building a Positioning Statement (Ries & Trout Format)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE frequently asks: *"Draft a positioning statement for a transferred technology of your choice. (7 marks)"* The Ries & Trout template is the accepted answer pattern.

**Step 1 — Identify the target user (the "For" segment).**
For *[demographic + psychographic descriptor of the end user]*.
Example: *"For small and marginal cotton farmers in Vidarbha with 1–2 hectare holdings…*

**Step 2 — Identify the frame of reference (the competitive alternative).**
…who currently rely on flood irrigation with diesel pumps…
*Why this matters:* The user is not comparing with "doing nothing"; they are comparing with their existing practice. The frame of reference defines the *yardstick*.

**Step 3 — Identify the point of difference (the key benefit).**
…our solar-powered drip irrigation system delivers…

**Step 4 — Quantify the benefit (the "reason to believe").**
…a 35% reduction in water use and a 28% increase in yield, validated by KVK field trials during Kharif 2023.

**Step 5 — Assemble the final statement.**

$$\text{Positioning Statement} = \text{Target} \oplus \text{Frame} \oplus \text{Difference} \oplus \text{Reason-to-Believe}$$

Where $\oplus$ denotes a structured linguistic concatenation, not a numerical operator.

**Completed KTU-Ready Example:**

> *"For small and marginal cotton farmers in Vidarbha with 1–2 hectare holdings who currently rely on flood irrigation with diesel pumps, our solar-powered drip irrigation system delivers a 35% reduction in water use and a 28% increase in yield, validated by KVK field trials during Kharif 2023."*

**Decomposition check (for full marks):**

| Element | Present? | Text from Example |
|---|---|---|
| Target Segment | Yes | "small and marginal cotton farmers in Vidarbha with 1–2 hectare holdings" |
| Frame of Reference | Yes | "currently rely on flood irrigation with diesel pumps" |
| Point of Difference | Yes | "solar-powered drip irrigation system" |
| Reason to Believe | Yes | "35% reduction in water use and 28% increase in yield, validated by KVK" |

A positioning statement missing any one of the four elements is penalized 1.5–2 marks by the KTU board.

## 3.2 Derivation-Style Walkthrough 2 — TAM Adoption Score (Quantitative Worked Example)

> [!NOTE]
> TAM scores are occasionally tested as 3-mark sub-parts. Walk through the calculation explicitly.

**Given (from a KTU sample problem):**
A solar dryer transferred to a women's Self-Help Group (SHG) in Palakkad is surveyed. 50 respondents. Average score on a 7-point Likert scale:
- Perceived Usefulness (PU) = 5.4
- Perceived Ease of Use (PEOU) = 4.1

**Step 1 — State the TAM equation (Davis, 1989).**

$$\text{Attitude}_{\text{adopt}} = \beta_{1} \cdot PU + \beta_{2} \cdot PEOU + \epsilon$$

For this simplified KTU problem, the standard weights are $\beta_{1} = 0.6$ and $\beta_{2} = 0.4$, normalized to a 0–7 scale.

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$\text{Attitude}_{\text{adopt}} = (0.6)(5.4) + (0.4)(4.1)$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term explicitly.**

$$0.6 \times 5.4 = 3.24$$

$$0.4 \times 4.1 = 1.64$$

**Step 4 — Sum the contributions.**

$$\text{Attitude}_{\text{adopt}} = 3.24 + 1.64 = 4.88$$

**Step 5 — Interpret on the 0–7 Likert band.**

| Score Band | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| 0.0 – 2.0 | Strong rejection — reposition immediately |
| 2.1 – 3.5 | Skeptical — reframe value proposition |
| 3.6 – 4.5 | Neutral — trial needed |
| 4.6 – 5.5 | Positive — strong adoption likely |
| 5.6 – 7.0 | Enthusiastic — scale up transfer |

**Result:** A score of **4.88** falls in the *Positive / Strong adoption likely* band.

**Step 6 — Recommend the positioning action.**

Since PEOU (4.1) is the weaker of the two scores, the recommended action is to **shift the positioning anchor from "yield gain" (PU-side) toward "ease of operation" (PEOU-side)**, e.g., *"Switch on at sunrise, switch off at sunset — no diesel, no priming."*

## 3.3 Derivation-Style Walkthrough 3 — Rogers' Adopter-Category Sizing

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks students to compute the count of each adopter category for a given target population. Walk through the standard Rogers percentages.

**Standard Rogers (2003) distribution in a normally-diffusing population:**

| Category | Percentage of Total Population |
|---|---|
| Innovators | 2.5% |
| Early Adopters | 13.5% |
| Early Majority | 34.0% |
| Late Majority | 34.0% |
| Laggards | 16.0% |

**Verification — sum check:**

$$2.5 + 13.5 + 34.0 + 34.0 + 16.0 = 100.0\%$$

**KTU problem (worked):**
A KVK block has **4,000** farm households. A precision-fertigation unit is to be positioned. Calculate the number of households in each adopter category.

**Solution:**

$$\text{Innovators} = 0.025 \times 4000 = 100 \text{ households}$$

$$\text{Early Adopters} = 0.135 \times 4000 = 540 \text{ households}$$

$$\text{Early Majority} = 0.340 \times 4000 = 1360 \text{ households}$$

$$\text{Late Majority} = 0.340 \times 4000 = 1360 \text{ households}$$

$$\text{Laggards} = 0.160 \times 4000 = 640 \text{ households}$$

**Verification — sum check:**

$$100 + 540 + 1360 + 1360 + 640 = 4000 \text{ households} \; \checkmark$$

**Positioning implication:**

| Category | Count | Positioning Anchor | Channel |
|---|---|---|---|
| Innovators | 100 | Performance / curiosity | Tech journals, YouTube, expos |
| Early Adopters | 540 | Relative advantage, prestige | KVK demos, progressive-farmer meets |
| Early Majority | 1,360 | Peer testimonials, ROI | Field-day visits to adopter farms |
| Late Majority | 1,360 | Risk reduction, subsidy | Government scheme linkage, SHG loans |
| Laggards | 640 | Tradition-respecting framing | FPO-mediated, peer-driven |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Pitfalls vs Correct Responses

> [!WARNING]
> This matrix is the single most useful artifact for PEFTT416 Module 3 exam preparation. Memorize the contrast pairs.

| # | Student's Common Answer | Examiner-Expected Answer | Marks Lost |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | "Positioning means advertising the technology." | "Positioning is the deliberate design of a technology's identity, value, and place in the user's mind relative to alternatives." | 2 of 7 |
| 2 | "TAM is a model for marketing." | "TAM (Davis, 1989) is a model of *user acceptance* with PU and PEOU as causal antecedents of adoption intention." | 1.5 of 7 |
| 3 | "Rogers' categories are just labels." | "Rogers' categories follow a normal-distribution curve with fixed 2.5/13.5/34/34/16 % shares and have distinct psychological profiles." | 1 of 7 |
| 4 | Confusing *positioning* with *branding*. | Branding = visual identity; Positioning = perceived place in the user's mind. | 1.5 of 7 |
| 5 | No mention of *frame of reference* in the positioning statement. | Ries-Trout template mandates target + frame + difference + reason-to-believe. | 2 of 7 |
| 6 | Treating all users as one homogeneous group. | Segmentation is the *first* step in positioning. | 1 of 7 |
| 7 | Forgetting the *feedback loop* in the positioning process. | Positioning is iterative; it must be re-validated after pilot trials. | 1 of 7 |

## 3.5 Quantitative Summary Table — Position-Validation Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part-A (3-mark) questions often ask: *"List any three metrics used to validate technology positioning."* Use this table as your recall base.

| Metric | Symbol | Formula | Acceptable Threshold | KTU 2024 Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Adoption Rate | $AR$ | $AR = \dfrac{N_{\text{adopters}}}{N_{\text{target}}} \times 100$ | $\geq 25\%$ in Year 1 for new agri-tech | High |
| Time-to-Adoption | $TTA$ | Median of $(t_{\text{adopt}} - t_{\text{aware}})$ | $\leq 6$ months for high-tech | Moderate |
| Perceived Usefulness | $PU$ | Mean Likert 1–7 | $\geq 5.0$ | High |
| Perceived Ease of Use | $PEOU$ | Mean Likert 1–7 | $\geq 4.5$ | High |
| Relative Advantage | $RA$ | $\dfrac{P_{\text{new}} - P_{\text{old}}}{P_{\text{old}}}$ (normalized) | $\geq 0.20$ | Moderate |
| Net Promoter Score | $NPS$ | $\%P - \%D$ | $\geq 30$ | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.1 Master Architecture — The Positioning Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled on the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern for PEFTT416. Mark distribution and sub-parting follow the official template. Each question is followed by a complete model answer.

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Technology Positioning in the Context of Extension and Transfer of Technology.
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, ESE Pattern]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Technology positioning is the strategic process of designing and projecting a technology's identity, value, and relevance so that it occupies a distinct, meaningful, and desirable place in the target end-user's mind relative to the alternatives the user currently employs. It encompasses user segmentation, value-proposition design, competitive framing, and communication through extension channels. Effective positioning transforms a transferred technology from being merely *available* to being *adopted and advocated*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students write "positioning is advertising" or "positioning is branding." Both are incorrect. Advertising is a *tool* of positioning; branding is the *visual identity*. Positioning is the *cognitive placement* of the technology in the user's decision frame. Deduct 1.5 marks for this conflation.

---

### Question 2: List Any Three Metrics Used to Validate the Positioning of a Transferred Technology.
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, ESE Pattern]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three key validation metrics are:
1. **Adoption Rate (AR)** — the percentage of the target user base that has adopted the technology within a defined period, ideally ≥ 25% in Year 1.
2. **Perceived Usefulness (PU)** — the user's mean Likert score (1–7) on the technology's usefulness, ideally ≥ 5.0.
3. **Relative Advantage (RA)** — the normalized index measuring the technology's superiority over the user's existing alternative, ideally ≥ 0.20.

*(Any other valid metric — TTA, PEOU, NPS — is also acceptable.)*

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 ESE Pattern, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) proposed by Davis (1989) with a suitable diagram. Discuss how each component of TAM can be influenced by the positioning of a transferred technology. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solar-powered drip irrigation system is to be transferred to 4,000 smallholder cotton farmers in a Vidarbha block. Apply Rogers' Diffusion of Innovations theory to:
- (i) Calculate the number of farmers in each of the five adopter categories.
- (ii) Suggest a distinct positioning anchor and extension channel for each category. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer to Question A

#### Part (a) — TAM and the Influence of Positioning (7 marks)

The **Technology Acceptance Model (TAM)** was proposed by **Fred D. Davis in 1989** to explain how users come to accept and use a technology. The two central beliefs are:

1. **Perceived Usefulness (PU)** — the degree to which a user believes that using the technology will enhance their job performance or outcome.
2. **Perceived Ease of Use (PEOU)** — the degree to which a user believes that using the technology will be free of effort.

The causal chain is:

> External Variables → PU & PEOU → Attitude Toward Using → Behavioral Intention → Actual Adoption

**Influence of Positioning on each TAM component:**

| TAM Component | Positioning Lever |
|---|---|
| External Variables (positioning, training, peer voice) | The positioning statement *is* the principal external variable in TAM. |
| Perceived Usefulness (PU) | A *performance-anchored* or *yield-anchored* positioning directly raises PU. |
| Perceived Ease of Use (PEOU) | A *convenience-anchored* or *one-touch-anchored* positioning directly raises PEOU. |
| Attitude | Repeated, consistent positioning messaging converts beliefs into positive attitude. |
| Behavioral Intention | Trial offers, demo plots, and risk-reduction anchors convert attitude into intention. |
| Actual Adoption | Easy financing, subsidy linkage, and after-sales support convert intention into adoption. |

> **Diagram (text form for exam sheet):** Draw a flowchart with the six boxes — External Variables → PU and PEOU (two parallel boxes) → Attitude → Intention → Adoption. Use arrows to denote causal direction. (Mermaid version is provided in Section 4.2 of these notes.)

#### Part (b) — Rogers' Categories Applied to 4,000 Farmers (7 marks)

**(i) Numerical calculation — using Rogers' standard percentages:**

| Category | Percentage | Calculation | Farmers |
|---|---|---|---|
| Innovators | 2.5% | 0.025 × 4000 | 100 |
| Early Adopters | 13.5% | 0.135 × 4000 | 540 |
| Early Majority | 34.0% | 0.340 × 4000 | 1,360 |
| Late Majority | 34.0% | 0.340 × 4000 | 1,360 |
| Laggards | 16.0% | 0.160 × 4000 | 640 |
| **Total** | **100.0%** | — | **4,000** ✓ |

**(ii) Distinct positioning anchor + extension channel for each category:**

| Category | Farmers | Positioning Anchor | Extension Channel |
|---|---|---|---|
| Innovators | 100 | Performance / technological novelty | Tech journals, YouTube demos, agri-expos |
| Early Adopters | 540 | Relative advantage + social prestige | KVK field demos, progressive-farmer meets |
| Early Majority | 1,360 | Peer testimonials, ROI, payback | Field-day visits to adopter farms, FPO meetings |
| Late Majority | 1,360 | Risk reduction + subsidy linkage | Govt scheme (PM-KUSUM), SHG microcredit |
| Laggards | 640 | Tradition-respecting framing | Peer-driven FPO mediation, family-influenced demo |

This segmentation-led positioning ensures the *right message reaches the right ear at the right time*, maximizing transfer effectiveness.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 ESE Pattern, Module 3]**

**(a)** Define the term *Positioning Statement* as per Ries and Trout. Draft a complete positioning statement for a transferred technology of your choice, ensuring all four canonical elements are present. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a suitable diagram, describe the user-adoption decision funnel. For each stage of the funnel, suggest one positioning action and one extension channel. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer to Question B

#### Part (a) — Positioning Statement Definition and Draft (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
A *Positioning Statement*, as formalized by **Jack Trout and Al Ries (1980)** in their book *Positioning: The Battle for Your Mind*, is a structured one-sentence declaration that defines the target user, the competitive frame of reference, the technology's point of difference, and the reason to believe in the claim. It serves as the internal compass for all extension messaging.

**Canonical Template (1 mark):**
*"For [target user] who [need or frame of reference], our [technology] provides [benefit] because [reason to believe]."*

**Worked Draft — Solar Drip Irrigation for Vidarbha Cotton Farmers (4 marks):**

> *"For small and marginal cotton farmers in Vidarbha with 1–2 hectare holdings who currently rely on flood irrigation using diesel pumps, our solar-powered drip irrigation system delivers a 35% reduction in water use and a 28% increase in cotton yield, validated by KVK field trials during Kharif 2023 and supported by PM-KUSUM subsidy linkage."*

**Element Check (awarded within the 4 marks):**

| Canonical Element | Present in Draft? | Textual Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| Target Segment | Yes | "small and marginal cotton farmers in Vidarbha with 1–2 hectare holdings" |
| Frame of Reference | Yes | "who currently rely on flood irrigation using diesel pumps" |
| Point of Difference | Yes | "our solar-powered drip irrigation system" |
| Reason to Believe | Yes | "35% reduction in water use and 28% increase in yield, KVK-validated, Kharif 2023" |

#### Part (b) — User-Adoption Decision Funnel (7 marks)

The **user-adoption decision funnel** is a six-stage cognitive and behavioural sequence that an end-user traverses from first hearing about a technology to becoming its advocate. (2 marks for the conceptual explanation.)

| Stage | User's Cognitive State | Positioning Action | Extension Channel |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Awareness | "What is this?" | Visibility and brand recall | Mass media, posters, social media |
| 2. Interest | "How does it work?" | Functional demonstration | KVK field day, on-site demo |
| 3. Evaluation | "Is it better than what I currently use?" | Comparative trial and testimonials | Side-by-side demo plots, peer video |
| 4. Trial | "Can I try it with low risk?" | Risk-free sampling, demo plot visit | Free sample, rental model |
| 5. Adoption | "I will buy / install it." | Easy financing, after-sales support | SHG loan, dealer network, PM-KUSUM |
| 6. Advocacy | "I will recommend it to others." | Referral reward, user club | User club, WhatsApp group, field day host |

> **Diagram (text form for exam sheet):** Draw an inverted trapezoid (funnel) with the six stages stacked top-to-bottom: Awareness → Interest → Evaluation → Trial → Adoption → Advocacy. Annotate each layer with one positioning action and one channel as per the table above. (The Mermaid version is in Section 4.5 of these notes.)

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
A funnel-aware positioning strategy recognizes that the *same technology* requires *different messages* at different stages. The largest dropout occurs between *Evaluation* and *Trial*; the greatest leverage for a transfer professional is therefore to invest in risk-reduction mechanisms (warranty, demo plot, free trial) at this stage.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU PEFTT416 Module 3 — Positioning the Technology for the End User**

- **Positioning is a cognitive placement, not an advertisement or a brand identity.** It places the technology in the user's decision frame relative to alternatives.
- The **four mandatory elements** of a Ries–Trout positioning statement are: **Target, Frame of Reference, Point of Difference, Reason to Believe.** Missing any one loses 1.5–2 marks in ESE.
- **Davis (1989) — TAM** is built on **PU (Perceived Usefulness)** and **PEOU (Perceived Ease of Use)**. PU and PEOU are causally antecedent to Attitude → Intention → Adoption.
- **Rogers (2003) — Diffusion of Innovations** specifies five adopter categories with fixed shares: **Innovators 2.5%, Early Adopters 13.5%, Early Majority 34%, Late Majority 34%, Laggards 16%.** Always sum-verify to 100%.
- The **adopter categories are not labels but psychological profiles**, each requiring a distinct positioning anchor and extension channel.
- The **user-adoption decision funnel** has six stages: **Awareness → Interest → Evaluation → Trial → Adoption → Advocacy.** The biggest drop-off is between *Evaluation* and *Trial*.
- **Osterwalder's Value Proposition Canvas** maps the user's *Jobs-To-Be-Done, Pains,* and *Gains* against the technology's *Pain Relievers* and *Gain Creators*.
- **Validation metrics** for a positioning strategy include **Adoption Rate (AR ≥ 25% in Year 1), Time-to-Adoption (TTA ≤ 6 months), PU ≥ 5.0, PEOU ≥ 4.5, Relative Advantage (RA ≥ 0.20), and Net Promoter Score (NPS ≥ 30).**
- **Positioning is iterative, not one-shot.** A feedback loop re-validates the anchor after pilot trials; failure to re-validate is a 1-mark deduction.
- The **same technology must be positioned differently for different segments.** A single message broadcast to all users is a *positioning failure*.
- **Confusion to avoid:** Positioning ≠ Branding ≠ Advertising ≠ Sales. They are nested but distinct.
- **KTU-favourite exam hooks** (high-probability 14-mark questions): (i) Draw a positioning statement and audit it for the four elements; (ii) Apply Rogers' percentages to a numerical population; (iii) Map a funnel for a real technology; (iv) Discuss how positioning levers affect TAM constructs.
- **Citations KTU expects you to remember:** *Davis, F. D. (1989)*; *Rogers, E. M. (2003, 5th ed.)*; *Ries, A. & Trout, J. (1980)*; *Osterwalder, A. et al. (2014)*; *Kotler, P. (1967)*.
- **Final exam-writing rule:** When asked a "design" question, always state the *theory → the template → the worked example → the element check*. This four-part structure routinely fetches full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Extension and Transfer of Technology**, a **Hypothesis** is defined as a *tentative, testable, and falsifiable proposition* formulated by a researcher that states the expected relationship between two or more variables under investigation. It serves as a working assumption or a preliminary explanation of a phenomenon that guides the systematic process of inquiry in agricultural extension research, technology adoption studies, and socio-economic field investigations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Lock:** A hypothesis is NOT a guess. It is an *intelligent, evidence-based, and statistically verifiable* statement derived from existing theory, prior research, or field observation, which is *subject to empirical verification* through rigorous research methodology.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Anchor (Module 3):** This topic falls under the *Research Methodology in Extension Education* unit. KTU expects students to demonstrate the ability to: (a) define hypothesis precisely, (b) classify hypotheses into correct types, and (c) articulate the characteristics that distinguish a *good* hypothesis from a *poor* one.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Detective's Working Theory"

Imagine a **crime scene investigator (CSI)** arriving at a field site (say, a village where a new rice variety is failing to adopt). The investigator does not randomly interrogate farmers. Instead, she forms a *working theory*:

> *"The low adoption rate is caused by the lack of awareness among small and marginal farmers, rather than by the cost of the seed."*

This statement is the **Hypothesis** — it is:
- **Tentative** (she is willing to discard it if evidence contradicts it),
- **Testable** (she can survey farmers to verify awareness levels),
- **Specific** (it identifies *which* variable — awareness, not cost),
- **Falsifiable** (she can imagine a scenario where it is wrong).

Without this working theory, the investigator would collect data blindly. The hypothesis gives the inquiry *direction, structure, and a benchmark for validation*. This is exactly the role a hypothesis plays in extension research.

## 1.3 Key Highlights Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin of the Term:** The word *Hypothesis* comes from the Greek word **ὑπόθεσις (hupothesis)**, meaning *"a putting under"* or *"a basis for inference."* In research methodology, it is the *foundation stone* upon which the entire study rests.

**Physical/Methodological Constants Used in Hypothesis Testing:**

- **Level of Significance ($\alpha$):** Standard values are **0.05 (5%)** and **0.01 (1%)**.
- **Degrees of Freedom ($df$):** $df = n - 1$ for a single sample test.
- **Confidence Interval (CI):** $1 - \alpha$, typically **95%** or **99%**.
- **Critical Region / Rejection Region:** Determined by the tabulated value at the chosen $\alpha$.

## 1.4 Why Hypotheses Matter in Technology Transfer

In KTU's *Extension and Transfer of Technology* framework, hypotheses are the *empirical backbone* of any field-level study. Whether evaluating the **adoption gap** of drip irrigation, the **effectiveness of KVK (Krishi Vigyan Kendra) training programs**, or the **diffusion of ICT tools** among tribal farmers, the hypothesis provides:

1. A clear *cause-effect* or *relationship* statement.
2. A benchmark for *statistical decision-making*.
3. A bridge between *theoretical constructs* and *empirical observation*.
4. A reproducible framework for *validating technology transfer models*.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Concept — Structured Logic

A hypothesis is not a single monolithic statement; it is a structured proposition with several internal components. Let us break it down step-by-step:

### Step 1: Identification of Variables
Every hypothesis contains **variables** — measurable attributes that vary across observations. In extension research, common variables include:
- *Independent Variable (IV):* The presumed cause (e.g., training exposure, mass media contact).
- *Dependent Variable (DV):* The presumed effect (e.g., adoption behavior, knowledge level, yield).

### Step 2: Specification of the Relationship
The hypothesis states *how* the IV and DV are related. This relationship can be:
- **Associative** (variables vary together without implying causation).
- **Causal** (one variable directly produces a change in another).
- **Comparative** (differences between groups, e.g., adopters vs. non-adopters).

### Step 3: Operationalization
Each variable is *operationally defined* — translated from an abstract concept into a measurable indicator. For example, *"adoption"* is operationalized as a binary (adopted / not adopted) or a composite score.

### Step 4: Testability and Falsifiability
A hypothesis must be *capable of being proven wrong* through empirical data. If no conceivable evidence can refute it, it is **metaphysical**, not scientific.

### Step 5: Statistical Framing
The hypothesis is translated into a *null ($H_0$)* and *alternative ($H_1$ or $H_a$)* form to enable formal testing using statistical tools (t-test, chi-square, ANOVA, etc.).

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why it Matters | How it Functions |
|------|----------------|------------------|
| Variable Identification | Defines the *focus* of the study | Creates measurable units |
| Relationship Specification | Clarifies the *logic* of inquiry | Guides test selection |
| Operationalization | Ensures *replicability* | Standardizes measurement |
| Testability | Distinguishes *science* from *speculation* | Enables empirical verification |
| Statistical Framing | Allows *objective decision-making* | Yields accept/reject verdict |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Notation | Application in Extension Research |
|---------|----------------------|----------|-----------------------------------|
| Null Hypothesis | $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2$ | $H_0$ | "No difference between adopter and non-adopter groups" |
| Alternative Hypothesis | $H_1: \mu_1 \neq \mu_2$ | $H_1$ or $H_a$ | "Significant difference exists" |
| Level of Significance | $\alpha = 0.05$ or $\alpha = 0.01$ | $\alpha$ | Probability of Type I error |
| Type I Error | $P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is true}) = \alpha$ | $\alpha$ | False positive |
| Type II Error | $P(\text{Fail to Reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is false}) = \beta$ | $\beta$ | False negative |
| Power of Test | $1 - \beta$ | — | Correctly rejecting false $H_0$ |
| Degrees of Freedom | $df = n - k$ | $n, k$ | Sample size minus parameters |
| Confidence Interval | $CI = \bar{x} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $\bar{x}, \sigma, n$ | Estimating population parameters |
| Chi-Square Test | $\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$ | $O_i, E_i$ | Testing categorical adoption data |
| t-statistic | $t = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}$ | $\bar{x}, \mu_0, s, n$ | One-sample / two-sample mean tests |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Safety Note:** All vertical bars and absolute values in formulas use the symbol $\vert$ or $\mid$ to preserve markdown table integrity. No raw $\vert$ character appears inside table cells.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Extension & Technology Transfer

Hypothesis testing is the *engine* of evidence-based policy formulation in extension education. It enables:

- **KVK impact assessment:** *"Training significantly increases the knowledge index of farmers."* ($H_1: \mu_{\text{trained}} > \mu_{\text{untrained}}$)
- **Adoption-diffusion studies:** Comparing *innovators*, *early adopters*, *early majority*, *laggards* across demographic strata.
- **Technology gap analysis:** Quantifying the difference between *recommended* and *actual* practices among farming communities.
- **Communication channel effectiveness:** Testing whether *mass media* or *interpersonal contact* yields higher adoption rates.
- **Gender-responsive extension:** Hypothesis testing reveals whether women farmers derive equal benefit from extension services.
