**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** Revenue Expenditure refers to the recurring, short-term operational costs incurred during the implementation and operation of a technology transfer project, such as salaries of extension staff, cost of fuel, training materials, and daily maintenance.
- **Difference 1 (1 Mark):** Capital Expenditure is a one-time, long-term investment on fixed assets like equipment, buildings, and technology licensing, which creates a permanent asset and is depreciated over time. In contrast, Revenue Expenditure is fully expensed in the year it occurs.
- **Difference 2 (1 Mark):** CapEx affects the **balance sheet** (asset side), while RevEx affects the **profit and loss account** of the year in which it is incurred. Misclassification of one as the other leads to faulty valuation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)? State the decision rule for accepting a technology based on BCR.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 Marks):** The Benefit-Cost Ratio is the ratio of the present worth of total benefits (revenues) to the present worth of total costs (expenses) over the life of a technology transfer project, both discounted at a common rate.
- **Formula (0.5 Mark):** $\text{BCR} = \frac{\text{Present Worth of Benefits}}{\text{Present Worth of Costs}}$
- **Decision Rule (1 Mark):** A technology transfer project is accepted if BCR ≥ 1, meaning the discounted benefits exceed or equal the discounted costs. A BCR < 1 indicates the project is uneconomical and should be rejected.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse NPV decision rule with BCR:** NPV ≥ 0 means Accept, BCR ≥ 1 means Accept. Mixing them up is the most common mistake and costs **2 marks instantly**.
> - **Always discount:** Forgetting to apply the discount factor $(1+i)^t$ for future cash flows results in **wrong answers and zero marks** for the NPV/BCR computation step.
> - **Show formula before substitution:** KTU examiners award 1 mark for writing the correct formula before plugging in numbers. Skipping the formula loses this mark.
> - **Initial CapEx sign:** The initial investment is a **negative** cash flow at t = 0. Many students write it as positive, leading to a wrong NPV.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Valuation of a Greenhouse Technology Transfer Project*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

A Krishi Vigyan Kendra is evaluating a polyhouse technology for vegetable cultivation. The data is as follows:
- Initial Capital Cost (CapEx): **₹2,00,000** at Year 0.
- Annual Revenue from sale of vegetables: **₹90,000** for 5 years.
- Annual Revenue Expenditure (RevEx): **₹30,000** for 5 years.
- Salvage Value at the end of Year 5: **₹20,000**.
- Discount Rate: **i = 10%**.

**(a)** Compute the **Net Present Value (NPV)** of the project and state whether the technology should be transferred.

**(b)** Compute the **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** and the **Payback Period (PBP)**. Comment on the financial viability.

---

##### Model Solution — Part A(a): NPV Computation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Identify net annual cash flow.

$$
\text{Net Cash Flow (Years 1 to 5)} = 90{,}000 - 30{,}000 = \text{₹}60{,}000
$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Add the salvage value to the Year 5 cash flow.

$$
\text{Year 5 net flow} = 60{,}000 + 20{,}000 = \text{₹}80{,}000
$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** State the NPV formula.

$$
NPV = -C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{5} \frac{\text{Net Cash Flow}_t}{(1.10)^t}
$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Compute the Present Worth of an Annuity Factor (PWAF) for 5 years at 10%.

$$
PWAF = \frac{(1.10)^5 - 1}{0.10 \cdot (1.10)^5} = 3.7908
$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Compute the present value of the uniform net cash flows (Years 1–4) and Year 5 separately.

For Years 1–4 (₹60,000 each):

$$
PV_{1-4} = 60{,}000 \times PWAF_{4} = 60{,}000 \times 3.1699 = 1{,}90{,}194
$$

For Year 5 (₹80,000):

$$
PV_5 = \frac{80{,}000}{(1.10)^5} = \frac{80{,}000}{1.61051} = 49{,}673
$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Sum and subtract initial CapEx.

$$
NPV = -2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}90{,}194 + 49{,}673
$$

$$
NPV = -2{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}39{,}867
$$

$$
NPV = +\text{₹}39{,}867
$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Decision statement.

Since NPV is positive (₹39,867 > 0), the **polyhouse technology transfer project is financially viable and should be accepted**.

---

##### Model Solution — Part A(b): BCR and PBP (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** State the BCR formula.

$$
BCR = \frac{\text{Present Worth of Benefits}}{\text{Present Worth of Costs}}
$$

**Step 2 (1.5 Marks):** Compute Present Worth of Benefits (PWB) — only revenues are discounted; salvage is added to Year 5 revenue.

$$
PWB = 90{,}000 \times 3.7908 + \frac{20{,}000}{(1.10)^5}
$$

$$
PWB = 3{,}41{,}172 + 12{,}418 = \text{₹}3{,}53{,}590
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 Marks):** Compute Present Worth of Costs (PWC) — initial CapEx at Year 0 plus discounted RevEx.

$$
PWC = 2{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}000 \times 3.7908
$$

$$
PWC = 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}13{,}724 = \text{₹}3{,}13{,}724
$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Compute BCR.

$$
BCR = \frac{3{,}53{,}590}{3{,}13{,}724} = 1.127
$$

**Decision:** Since BCR > 1, the project is **economically justified**.

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Compute Payback Period.

$$
PBP = \frac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Flow}} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{60{,}000} = 3.33 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Comment on viability.

The polyhouse technology pays for itself in approximately **3 years and 4 months**, which is well within the 5-year project horizon. Combined with a positive NPV of ₹39,867 and a BCR of 1.127, the project is **strongly recommended for transfer and adoption**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Comparative Valuation of Two Extension Technologies*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

An extension officer is comparing two technologies for transfer to a farming community:

| Parameter | Technology X (Solar Pump) | Technology Y (Biofertilizer Unit) |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Initial Cost (₹) | 1,50,000 | 80,000 |
| Annual Revenue (₹) | 60,000 | 35,000 |
| Annual Expense (₹) | 15,000 | 10,000 |
| Project Life (years) | 5 | 5 |
| Salvage Value (₹) | 10,000 | 5,000 |
| Discount Rate (%) | 10 | 10 |

**(a)** Compute the **NPV** of both technologies and rank them.

**(b)** Compute the **BCR** and **IRR** (approximate) for both, and recommend which one should be transferred.

---

##### Model Solution — Part B(a): NPV of Both Technologies (7 Marks)

**For Technology X (Solar Pump):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Net annual cash flow.

$$
R_t - C_t = 60{,}000 - 15{,}000 = \text{₹}45{,}000
$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Add salvage to Year 5.

$$
\text{Year 5 flow} = 45{,}000 + 10{,}000 = \text{₹}55{,}000
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 Marks):** Compute NPV.

$$
NPV_X = -1{,}50{,}000 + 45{,}000 \times 3.7908 - 45{,}000 \times 3.1699 + 45{,}000 \times 3.1699 + 55{,}000 \times 0.6209
$$

A cleaner approach: Discount all 5 years' net flow uniformly at ₹45,000 and add salvage at Year 5.

$$
NPV_X = -1{,}50{,}000 + 45{,}000 \times 3.7908 + \frac{10{,}000}{(1.10)^5}
$$

$$
NPV_X = -1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}70{,}586 + 6{,}209 = +\text{₹}26{,}795
$$

**For Technology Y (Biofertilizer Unit):**

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Net annual cash flow.

$$
35{,}000 - 10{,}000 = \text{₹}25{,}000
$$

**Step 5 (1.5 Marks):** Compute NPV.

$$
NPV_Y = -80{,}000 + 25{,}000 \times 3.7908 + \frac{5{,}000}{1.61051}
$$

$$
NPV_Y = -80{,}000 + 94{,}770 + 3{,}105 = +\text{₹}17{,}875
$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Ranking.

- $NPV_X$ = ₹26,795 (higher)
- $NPV_Y$ = ₹17,875
- **Rank 1: Technology X (Solar Pump)** is preferred on NPV criterion.

---

##### Model Solution — Part B(b): BCR and IRR, with Recommendation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Compute Present Worth of Benefits (PWB) for both.

For X:

$$
PWB_X = 60{,}000 \times 3.7908 + \frac{10{,}000}{1.61051} = 2{,}27{,}448 + 6{,}209 = \text{₹}2{,}33{,}657
$$

For Y:

$$
PWB_Y = 35{,}000 \times 3.7908 + \frac{5{,}000}{1.61051} = 1{,}32{,}678 + 3{,}105 = \text{₹}1{,}35{,}783
$$

**Step 2 (1.5 Marks):** Compute Present Worth of Costs (PWC) for both.

For X:

$$
PWC_X = 1{,}50{,}000 + 15{,}000 \times 3.7908 = 1{,}50{,}000 + 56{,}862 = \text{₹}2{,}06{,}862
$$

For Y:

$$
PWC_Y = 80{,}000 + 10{,}000 \times 3.7908 = 80{,}000 + 37{,}908 = \text{₹}1{,}17{,}908
$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Compute BCR.

$$
BCR_X = \frac{2{,}33{,}657}{2{,}06{,}862} = 1.130
$$

$$
BCR_Y = \frac{1{,}35{,}783}{1{,}17{,}908} = 1.153
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 Marks):** Approximate IRR (using linear interpolation between 10% and 20%):

For X, compute NPV at 20%:

$$
NPV_{X,20\%} = -1{,}50{,}000 + 45{,}000 \times 2.9906 + \frac{10{,}000}{2.4883}
$$

$$
NPV_{X,20\%} = -1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}34{,}577 + 4{,}019 = -11{,}404
$$

By interpolation:

$$
IRR_X \approx 10\% + \frac{26{,}795}{26{,}795 + 11{,}404} \times 10\% = 10\% + 7.01\% = 17.01\%
$$

For Y (similar method yields approximately **18%**).

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Comparative ranking.

| Indicator | Tech X | Tech Y | Winner |
|-----------|--------|--------|--------|
| NPV (₹) | 26,795 | 17,875 | **X** |
| BCR | 1.130 | 1.153 | **Y** |
| IRR (approx %) | 17.01 | 18.00 | **Y** |

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Recommendation.

When NPV and BCR give **conflicting rankings**, NPV is the **superior criterion** because it captures the absolute rupee value added. Therefore, **Technology X (Solar Pump) should be transferred**, as it adds more net economic value to the farming community. However, if the extension agency has a strict capital constraint and wants maximum benefit per rupee invested, **Technology Y (Biofertilizer Unit)** with the higher BCR of 1.153 would be preferred.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision — Must Memorize Before KTU Exam**

- **REV Definition:** Quantification of monetary inflows and outflows of a technology transfer project to judge its economic worth.
- **CapEx = one-time asset creation** (buildings, equipment, license). **RevEx = recurring operations** (salaries, fuel, maintenance).
- **Time Value of Money:** A rupee today > a rupee tomorrow. Always **discount** future flows.
- **NPV Decision Rule:** NPV ≥ 0 → Accept. NPV < 0 → Reject.
- **BCR Decision Rule:** BCR ≥ 1 → Accept. BCR < 1 → Reject.
- **IRR Decision Rule:** IRR ≥ discount rate → Accept.
- **Payback Rule:** Shorter payback is preferred; should be less than the project horizon.
- **Conflicting NPV vs BCR:** **Trust NPV** (absolute value) over BCR (relative ratio) when they disagree.
- **Standard Discount Rate (India, agri):** **12%** (NITI Aayog) or **10%** (typical KTU textbook).
- **PWAF (5 yrs, 10%):** 3.7908. **PWAF (5 yrs, 12%):** 3.6048. **PWAF (10 yrs, 10%):** 6.1446.
- **BEP formula:** $Q = F / (P - V)$. Useful for adoption threshold analysis in extension.
- **Salvage Value** is added to the **last year's revenue stream** before discounting.
- **Initial CapEx** is **negative** at t = 0; never discount it.
- **NPV > BCR when ranking:** NPV is superior for mutually exclusive projects.
- **BCR is good for:** Independent projects, ranking where scale is not important.
- **Common KTU errors to avoid:**
  1. Forgetting to discount.
  2. Wrong sign for initial investment.
  3. Mixing up BCR ≥ 1 rule with NPV ≥ 0 rule.
  4. Classifying RevEx as CapEx or vice versa.
  5. Omitting salvage value.
  6. Computing payback using gross revenue instead of net cash flow.
- **Key extension-specific revenue items:** increased farm yield, royalty, training fees, sale of seeds/planting material, processing charges.
- **Key extension-specific expense items:** field demonstrations, kisan melas, farmer field schools, audio-visual aids, travel, extension officer salaries.
- **Valuation is essential** because farmers adopt technology only when it is **financially attractive**, regardless of its technical merit.
- **Social vs Private Valuation:** Extension projects often include indirect social benefits (employment, sustainability) that the market does not price — these should be flagged in the KTU answer for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation of Technology: Inventory Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT416 - EXTENSION AND TRANSFER OF TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Evaluation of Technology: Inventory Management ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Inventory Management** is the systematic process of planning, organizing, directing, and controlling the inventory of an organization — encompassing raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), finished goods, and consumables — to ensure optimal stock levels that minimize total costs while meeting operational and customer service requirements. In the context of **Extension and Transfer of Technology**, inventory management applies to: - **Farm inputs** (seeds, fertilizers, pesticides, machinery) - **Post-harvest produce** (grains, perishables, processed goods) - **Extension materials** (publications, audio-visual aids, demonstration kits) - **Transfer of technology** logistics (prototype storage, sample distribution) ### Key Categories of Inventory | Category | Description | Example in Agribusiness | |----------|-------------|--------------------------| | **Raw Materials** | Inputs awaiting processing | Seeds, fertilizers at processing plant | | **Work-in-Progress (WIP)** | Partially completed items | Semi-processed grains | | **Finished Goods** | Completed products ready for sale | Packaged rice, flour | | **MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Operating)** | Support items | Spare parts, lubricants | | **Pipeline/Transit Inventory** | Items in transit | Goods in delivery vehicles | ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Kitchen Pantry" Analogy:** Imagine your home kitchen. Too little stock (rice, oil, spices) means you can't cook when needed — your family starves. Too much stock means money is locked up, food spoils, and rats invade. **Inventory management is the art of keeping "just enough" — not too much, not too little.** In a farm extension setup, this means keeping just enough seeds for the next sowing season, just enough fertilizer for the current crop cycle, and storing produce optimally until market prices are favorable. ### Objectives of Inventory Management 1. **Ensure uninterrupted supply** of materials for production/sales 2. **Minimize total inventory costs** (ordering + holding + shortage) 3. **Eliminate stockouts** and backorders 4. **Reduce obsolescence, spoilage, and wastage** 5. **Maintain optimum investment** in inventory 6. **Facilitate smooth technology transfer** by ensuring availability of inputs ### Why Inventory Management Matters in Technology Transfer > [!IMPORTANT] > In agricultural extension, the **success of any transferred technology** (e.g., drip irrigation, hybrid seeds, biofertilizers) depends on the **timely availability of inputs and outputs**. Poor inventory planning is a leading cause of technology adoption failure in rural India. ### Two Fundamental Inventory Systems | System | Description | Best Suited For | |--------|-------------|-----------------| | **Continuous Review (Q-System)** | Inventory checked continuously; order placed when level hits reorder point | High-value, fast-moving items | | **Periodic Review (P-System)** | Inventory checked at fixed intervals; order quantity varies | Multiple low-cost items, agricultural inputs | ### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Reorder Level (ROL)** — quantity at which fresh order is triggered - **Safety Stock (SS)** — buffer against demand/lead time variability - **Lead Time (LT)** — time gap between order placement and receipt - **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** — cost-minimizing order size - **Inventory Carrying Cost %** — typically **15% to 30%** of inventory value per year in Indian agricultural contexts - **Service Level (Z-score)** — probability of avoiding stockout ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### The Structure of Total Inventory Cost The total cost of inventory (TC) is the sum of three primary cost components: $$TC = \text{Ordering Cost} + \text{Holding Cost} + \text{Shortage Cost}$$ Where: - **Ordering Cost (also called Setup Cost)**: Fixed cost incurred every time an order is placed — includes administrative costs, transportation, inspection. - **Holding Cost (also called Carrying Cost)**: Cost of maintaining inventory in storage — includes warehousing, insurance, taxes, depreciation, spoilage, opportunity cost of capital. - **Shortage Cost (Stockout Cost)**: Cost of running out of stock — lost sales, backorder penalties, production stoppage. ### Deterministic Inventory Model (EOQ) — Wilson's Formula > [!NOTE] > The **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** model, developed by **Ford W. Harris (1913)** and refined by **R.H. Wilson**, identifies the order quantity that minimizes the total of ordering and holding costs, assuming **constant demand rate, fixed lead time, and no stockouts**. #### Assumptions of Basic EOQ Model 1. Demand is **known and constant** over time 2. Lead time is **known and constant** 3. Order is received **instantaneously** (infinite replenishment rate) 4. No **quantity discounts** 5. Only **ordering** and **holding costs** are considered 6. No **stockouts** are permitted 7. **Independent** demand items #### The Cost Curves Logic | Curve | Behavior as Q increases | Reason | |-------|-------------------------|--------| | Ordering Cost (D·S/Q) | **Decreases** (hyperbolic) | Fewer orders needed | | Holding Cost (Q·H/2) | **Increases** (linear) | More average stock held | | Total Cost | **U-shaped** (convex) | Sum has a unique minimum | The optimal **EOQ** occurs exactly at the point where: $$\text{Ordering Cost} = \text{Holding Cost}$$ ### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is **exam-critical**. Memorize these formulas — they appear in nearly every Part B question on inventory management. | # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | |---|---------|---------|-------------------| | 1 | **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | D = annual demand (units), S = ordering cost (₹/order), H = holding cost (₹/unit/year) | | 2 | **Optimal Number of Orders** | $N = \dfrac{D}{Q^*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{DH}{2S}}$ | N = number of orders/year | | 3 | **Optimal Order Interval** | $T^* = \dfrac{Q^*}{D} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2S}{DH}}$ | T* = time between orders (years) | | 4 | **Minimum Total Cost** | $TC^* = \sqrt{2DSH}$ | TC* in ₹/year | | 5 | **Total Cost (general Q)** | $TC = \dfrac{DS}{Q} + \dfrac{QH}{2}$ | Sum of ordering + holding | | 6 | **Holding Cost from Unit Price** | $H = I \cdot C$ | I = carrying cost rate (fraction), C = unit cost (₹) | | 7 | **Reorder Point (ROP)** | $ROP = d \cdot L + SS$ | d = daily demand, L = lead time (days), SS = safety stock | | 8 | **Safety Stock (Variable Demand)** | $SS = Z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | Z = service level coefficient, σ_d = std. dev. of daily demand | | 9 | **Safety Stock (Variable Lead Time)** | $SS = Z \cdot d \cdot \sigma_L$ | σ_L = std. dev. of lead time | | 10 | **Combined Uncertainty SS** | $SS = Z \cdot \sqrt{(d \cdot \sigma_L)^2 + (\sigma_d \cdot L)^2}$ | Both demand and lead time vary | | 11 | **Inventory Turnover Ratio** | $ITR = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | Times per year | | 12 | **Average Inventory (EOQ)** | $\bar{I} = \dfrac{Q^*}{2}$ | Units | | 13 | **Days of Supply** | $DOS = \dfrac{\text{Average Inventory}}{d}$ | Days | | 14 | **Stockout Risk** | $P(\text{stockout}) = 1 - \text{Service Level}$ | Probability (0 to 1) | ### Z-Score Reference Table (Service Levels) — *High-Yield for Numericals* | Service Level | Z-value | Use Case | |---------------|---------|----------| | 90.0% | **1.28** | Low criticality items | | 95.0% | **1.65** | Standard farm inputs | | 97.5% | **1.96** | Critical consumables | | 99.0% | **2.33** | High-cost perishables | | 99.87% | **3.00** | Six-sigma precision | ### Why These Models Matter in Engineering Extension In **Technology Transfer Programs** (e.g., T&V system, ATMA, KVKs), inventories of: - **Vermicompost units** (raw FYM, finished compost) - **Dairy cooperatives** (cattle feed, milk pouches) - **Custom Hiring Centers** (tractor parts, implements) - **Post-Harvest Centers** (graded produce, packaging) …all follow these same EOQ and ROP principles at scale. The math is identical whether the inventory is **bolts or paddy**. ### Advanced Inventory Models Overview | Model | When to Use | Modification | |-------|-------------|--------------| | **Production Order Quantity (POQ)** | When replenishment is gradual (production rate > demand rate) | $Q^*_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{p-d}}$ | | **Quantity Discount Model** | When suppliers offer bulk discounts | All-Units or Incremental discount | | **Backorder Model** | When stockouts are tolerated | Add backorder cost B | | **JIT (Just-In-Time)** | When holding costs are very high | Minimize or eliminate inventory | | **ABC Analysis** | When many items with varying values | Pareto-based classification | | **VED Analysis** | Spare parts inventory | Vital / Essential / Desirable | ### ABC Analysis (Always Better Control) — *High-Yield for Theory* > [!IMPORTANT] > **ABC Analysis** is a categorization technique based on the **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)**: > - **Class A items**: ~10–20% of items, ~70–80% of annual consumption value → **tight control**, frequent review > - **Class B items**: ~20–30% of items, ~15–25% of value → **moderate control** > - **Class C items**: ~50–70% of items, ~5–10% of value → **loose control**, bulk ordering In a **KVK (Krishi Vigyan Kendra)**, this translates to: - **A items**: Hybrid seed packets, biofertilizers (high value, critical) - **B items**: Standard fertilizers, small tools - **C items**: Stationery, packaging labels ### VED Analysis (Vital, Essential, Desirable) | Category | Action on Stockout | |----------|-------------------| | **Vital (V)** | Cannot function; must always stock | | **Essential (E)** | Affects efficiency; reasonable buffer | | **Desirable (D)** | Minor inconvenience; minimal stock | ### FSN, HML, SDE, GOLF — *Other Classification Systems* | System | Basis | Categories | |--------|-------|-----------| | **FSN** | Movement frequency | Fast / Slow / Non-moving | | **HML** | Unit price | High / Medium / Low | | **SDE** | Scarcity | Scarce / Difficult / Easy | | **GOLF** | Government sources | Government / Open market / Local / Foreign | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### Derivation 1: Deriving the EOQ Formula **Starting Point:** The total annual cost of inventory (excluding shortage cost) is: $$TC(Q) = \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{QH}{2}$$ Where: - $\frac{DS}{Q}$ = Annual ordering cost (D/Q orders × S per order) - $\frac{QH}{2}$ = Annual holding cost (average inventory Q/2 × H per unit per year) **Step 1:** Take the first derivative of TC with respect to Q and set it equal to zero (FOC for minimum): $$\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$ **Step 2:** Solve for Q: $$\frac{DS}{Q^2} = \frac{H}{2}$$ $$Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H}$$ $$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$ **Step 3:** Verify minimum using second-order condition (SOC): $$\frac{d^2(TC)}{dQ^2} = \frac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0 \quad \text{(for all } Q > 0\text{)}$$ Since the second derivative is positive, the function is **convex** and Q* is indeed a **minimum**. ✓ **Step 4:** Confirm the elegant property that at EOQ, ordering cost = holding cost: $$\text{At } Q^*: \quad \frac{DS}{Q^*} = \frac{Q^* \cdot H}{2}$$ Substituting $Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$: $$\frac{DS}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} = \frac{H}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} = \frac{Q^* H}{2}$$ Both sides equal $\sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}}$. This confirms the **U-curve symmetry**. **Step 5:** Substitute Q* back into TC to get minimum total cost: $$TC^* = \frac{DS}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} + \frac{H}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$ $$TC^* = \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} + \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} = \sqrt{2DSH}$$ $$\boxed{TC^* = \sqrt{2DSH}}$$ ### Derivation 2: Reorder Point with Safety Stock **Setup:** - Daily demand $d$ is normally distributed with mean $\bar{d}$ and standard deviation $\sigma_d$ - Lead time $L$ is normally distributed with mean $\bar{L}$ and standard deviation $\sigma_L$ **Step 1:** Demand during lead time (DL) is a random variable with: $$\text{Mean of DL} = \bar{d} \cdot \bar{L}$$ **Step 2:** Variance of demand during lead time (assuming independence): $$\text{Var}(DL) = \bar{d}^2 \cdot \sigma_L^2 + \bar{L} \cdot \sigma_d^2$$ **Step 3:** Standard deviation of DL: $$\sigma_{DL} = \sqrt{\bar{d}^2 \cdot \sigma_L^2 + \bar{L} \cdot \sigma_d^2}$$ **Step 4:** Safety stock formula: $$SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{DL} = Z \cdot \sqrt{\bar{d}^2 \cdot \sigma_L^2 + \bar{L} \cdot \sigma_d^2}$$ **Step 5:** Reorder point: $$ROP = \bar{d} \cdot \bar{L} + SS$$ ### Worked Numerical Example 1 (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem) > **Problem:** A farm equipment store sells 10,000 units of tractor engine oil per year. The cost of placing one order is ₹500. The holding cost is ₹4 per unit per year. Calculate: (a) EOQ, (b) Optimal number of orders per year, (c) Optimal time between orders, (d) Total minimum cost. **Given:** - $D = 10{,}000$ units/year - $S = ₹500$/order - $H = ₹4$/unit/year **Solution:** **(a) EOQ:** $$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 500}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{10{,}000{,}000}{4}} = \sqrt{2{,}500{,}000} = 1{,}581.14 \text{ units}$$ $$Q^* \approx 1{,}581 \text{ units}$$ **(b) Optimal Number of Orders:** $$N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{10{,}000}{1{,}581.14} \approx 6.32 \text{ orders/year}$$ **(c) Optimal Time Between Orders:** $$T^* = \frac{Q^*}{D} = \frac{1{,}581.14}{10{,}000} = 0.158 \text{ years} \approx 58 \text{ days}$$ **(d) Minimum Total Cost:** $$TC^* = \sqrt{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 500 \times 4} = \sqrt{40{,}000{,}000} = 6{,}324.56$$ $$TC^* \approx ₹6{,}324.56 \text{ per year}$$ **Verification:** At Q*, ordering cost = holding cost = ₹3,162.28 each. ✓ ### Worked Numerical Example 2 (Safety Stock & ROP) > **Problem:** A fertilizer cooperative uses urea at a mean rate of 200 bags/day. The standard deviation of daily demand is 30 bags. Lead time averages 5 days with std. dev. of 1 day. The manager wants a 95% service level. Find ROP and Safety Stock. **Given:** - $\bar{d} = 200$ bags/day - $\sigma_d = 30$ bags/day - $\bar{L} = 5$ days - $\sigma_L = 1$ day - $Z_{0.95} = 1.65$ **Solution:** **Step 1:** Standard deviation of demand during lead time: $$\sigma_{DL} = \sqrt{(200)^2 (1)^2 + (5)(30)^2}$$ $$= \sqrt{40{,}000 + 4{,}500} = \sqrt{44{,}500}$$ $$\sigma_{DL} = 210.95 \text{ bags}$$ **Step 2:** Safety stock: $$SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{DL} = 1.65 \times 210.95 = 348.07 \text{ bags}$$ $$SS \approx 348 \text{ bags}$$ **Step 3:** Reorder point: $$ROP = \bar{d} \cdot \bar{L} + SS = (200)(5) + 348 = 1{,}000 + 348 = 1{,}348 \text{ bags}$$ **Interpretation:** When stock falls to **1,348 bags**, place a new order. The 348-bag safety stock protects against unexpected demand surges or delivery delays. ### Analytical Comparison Matrix: Inventory Models | Model | Demand | Lead Time | Stockouts | Cost Components | Best Application | |-------|--------|-----------|-----------|-----------------|------------------| | **Basic EOQ** | Constant | Constant | Not allowed | O + H | Standard farm inputs | | **EOQ with Safety Stock** | Variable | Constant | Tolerated (with SS) | O + H + SS holding | Realistic farm scenario | | **EOQ with Variable LT** | Constant | Variable | Tolerated | O + H | Seasonal inputs | | **Production Quantity** | Constant | Continuous | Not allowed | O + H (adjusted) | On-farm production | | **Quantity Discount** | Constant | Constant | Not allowed | O + H + Purchase | Bulk fertilizer buying | | **Backorder Model** | Constant | Constant | Allowed (planned) | O + H + Backorder | Non-perishable produce | | **JIT** | Variable | Near-zero | Not allowed | Setup + Minimal H | Fresh produce, dairy | ### Real-World Engineering Utility In **agricultural extension technology transfer**, EOQ/ROP principles are applied in: - **Dairy Cooperatives (AMUL model)**: Milk procurement and feed inventory - **FPOs (Farmer Producer Organizations)**: Collective input procurement - **KVK input shops**: Seed, biofertilizer, and pesticide stocking - **Custom Hiring Centers**: Spare parts and consumables - **Cold storage chains**: Perishable inventory with strict ROP rules - **Post-Harvest Technology Centers**: Graded produce staging for market ### Quick-Reference Derivations Summary | # | What was derived | Result | |---|------------------|--------| | 1 | EOQ minimizing TC | $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ | | 2 | Minimum total cost | $TC^* = \sqrt{2DSH}$ | | 3 | Optimal order count | $N = \sqrt{DH/(2S)}$ | | 4 | ROP with safety stock | $ROP = \bar{d} \cdot \bar{L} + Z \cdot \sigma_{DL}$ | | 5 | Combined-uncertainty SS | $SS = Z\sqrt{\bar{d}^2\sigma_L^2 + \bar{L}\sigma_d^2}$ | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Diagram 1: Inventory Flow & Cost Components (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Supplier/Vendor] --> B[Inbound Logistics & Lead Time L] B --> C[Reorder Point ROP Check] C --> D{Stock Level vs ROP} D -->|Above ROP| E[Continue Operations] D -->|At or Below ROP| F[Trigger New Order Q*] F --> G[Place Order Cost S rupees] E --> H[Inventory in Storage] H --> I[Daily Demand d units] I --> J{Stock Available?} J -->|Yes| K[Meet Demand] J -->|No| L[Stockout Penalty Cost] H --> M[Carrying Cost H per unit per year] M --> N[Total Cost TC] G --> N L --> N N --> O[Minimize TC equals optimal Q*] classDef decision fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b58900,color:#000 classDef cost fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#c1121f,color:#000 classDef process fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#000 class D,J decision class G,L,M,N,O cost class A,B,C,E,F,H,I,K process ``` ### Diagram 2: Cost Curve Behavior Around EOQ (Conceptual Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BEHAVIOR[Cost Behavior as Order Quantity Q Increases] OC[Ordering Cost DS/Q
Decreases as hyperbola] HC[Holding Cost QH slash 2
Increases linearly] SC[Safety Stock Cost
Increases with service level] TOT[Total Cost TC
U shaped minimum at EOQ] end OC --> TOT HC --> TOT SC --> TOT TOT --> MIN[Minimum TC equals sqrt 2DSH
at Q star equals sqrt 2DS slash H] classDef cost fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#c1121f,color:#000 classDef minn fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#000 class OC,HC,SC,TOT cost class MIN minn ``` ### Diagram 3: ABC Analysis Classification Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TB START[All Inventory Items] --> SORT[Sort by Annual Consumption Value] SORT --> RANK[Compute Cumulative Percentage] RANK --> CLASSIFY{Classify} CLASSIFY -->|Top 10 to 20 percent| A[Class A Items
70 to 80 percent value
Tight Control
Frequent Review] CLASSIFY -->|Next 20 to 30 percent| B[Class B Items
15 to 25 percent value
Moderate Control] CLASSIFY -->|Bottom 50 to 70 percent| C[Class C Items
5 to 10 percent value
Loose Control
Bulk Ordering] A -->|Example| AEX[Hybrid seeds, biofertilizers, vaccines] B -->|Example| BEX[Standard NPK bags, garden tools] C -->|Example| CEX[Stationery, packaging tape, labels] classDef critical fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#c1121f,color:#000 classDef moderate fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b58900,color:#000 classDef low fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#000 class A,AEX critical class B,BEX moderate class C,CEX low ``` ### Diagram 4: Reorder Point & Safety Stock Concept ```mermaid flowchart TB AX[Time Axis Days] --> STAGE1[Stock at Maximum equals Q*] STAGE1 --> STAGE2[Steady Consumption at rate d per day] STAGE2 --> STAGE3{Reorder Level Reached?} STAGE3 -->|Yes at ROP| STAGE4[Place New Order Q*] STAGE4 --> STAGE5[Lead Time L days pass] STAGE5 --> STAGE6{Lead Time Variable?} STAGE6 -->|Yes early| STAGE7[Stock builds up unused buffer] STAGE6 -->|On time| STAGE8[New order arrives as stock depletes] STAGE6 -->|Late| STAGE9[Stock reaches Safety Stock SS just in time] STAGE7 --> STAGE10[Cycle Repeats] STAGE8 --> STAGE10 STAGE9 --> STAGE10 classDef timing fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#000 classDef safety fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#c1121f,color:#000 classDef process fill:#dde0e3,stroke:#495057,color:#000 class STAGE5,STAGE6,STAGE8 timing class STAGE9 safety class STAGE1,STAGE2,STAGE3,STAGE4,STAGE7,STAGE10 process ``` ### Diagram 5: Inventory Decision Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB TOP[Inventory Management System] --> P1[1. Classify Items ABC VED FSN HML] P1 --> P2[2. Forecast Annual Demand D] P2 --> P3[3. Determine Order Cost S and Holding Cost H] P3 --> P4[4. Compute EOQ equals sqrt 2DS slash H] P4 --> P5[5. Compute ROP and Safety Stock SS] P5 --> P6[6. Set Reorder Trigger and Review Cycle] P6 --> P7[7. Monitor Service Level and Stockouts] P7 --> P8[8. Adjust Parameters if Demand or Costs Change] P8 --> FEEDBACK[Feedback to Step 2] FEEDBACK --> P2 classDef process fill:#d8f3dc,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#000 classDef feedback fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b58900,color:#000 class TOP,P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6,P7,P8 process class FEEDBACK feedback ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, 3 marks]** > **Q: Define Economic Order Quantity (EOQ). State any two assumptions of the basic EOQ model.** **Model Answer:** EOQ is the optimal order quantity that minimizes the total annual inventory cost (sum of ordering and holding costs). It is mathematically given by: $$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$ **Two assumptions:** 1. Demand is known, constant, and independent. 2. Lead time is constant and replenishment is instantaneous. 3. (Any other valid assumption: no stockouts allowed, no quantity discounts, only ordering and holding costs are considered.) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, 3 marks]** > **Q: What is ABC analysis? Explain its significance in inventory management.** **Model Answer:** ABC analysis is an inventory classification technique based on the **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)**: - **A items** (10–20% of items, 70–80% of value) → tight control - **B items** (20–30% of items, 15–25% of value) → moderate control - **C items** (50–70% of items, 5–10% of value) → loose control **Significance:** It helps managers focus attention and resources on high-value items, reduces carrying costs, improves service levels for critical items, and optimizes working capital. --- ### Part B Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Module 4, 14 marks]** > **Q (a)** A dairy cooperative purchases cattle feed at the rate of 8,000 kg per year. The cost of placing one order is ₹400 and the holding cost is ₹10 per kg per year. Determine: > (i) Economic Order Quantity > (ii) Number of orders per year > (iii) Time between orders > (iv) Total minimum annual cost > [7 marks] **Model Solution:** **Given:** D = 8,000 kg/year, S = ₹400/order, H = ₹10/kg/year **(i) EOQ:** $$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 400}{10}} = \sqrt{\frac{6{,}400{,}000}{10}} = \sqrt{640{,}000} = 800 \text{ kg}$$ **(ii) Number of orders:** $$N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{8{,}000}{800} = 10 \text{ orders/year}$$ **(iii) Time between orders:** $$T^* = \frac{365 \text{ days}}{10} = 36.5 \text{ days}$$ Or: $T^* = Q^*/D = 800/8{,}000 = 0.1$ year = 36.5 days **(iv) Minimum total cost:** $$TC^* = \sqrt{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 400 \times 10} = \sqrt{64{,}000{,}000} = ₹8{,}000 \text{ per year}$$ **Verification:** Ordering cost = (8,000/800)(400) = ₹4,000. Holding cost = (800/2)(10) = ₹4,000. Sum = ₹8,000 ✓ > **Q (b)** What is Reorder Point? Explain the concept of safety stock with reference to a farm input supply chain. A pesticide retailer sells 150 bottles/day on average with σ = 20 bottles/day. Lead time is 4 days (σ_L = 0.5 days). Find the ROP for 95% service level. [7 marks] **Model Answer:** **Reorder Point (ROP):** The inventory level at which a new order must be placed to replenish stock before it runs out. It equals expected demand during lead time plus safety stock. **Safety Stock:** Extra inventory maintained as a buffer against: - Sudden demand surges - Unreliable supplier deliveries - Transit damage or delays In a **farm input supply chain**, safety stock ensures that critical inputs (pesticides, vaccines, seeds) are available during peak agricultural seasons when demand is volatile and lead times from distant manufacturers can vary. **Numerical Solution:** Given: $\bar{d} = 150$ bottles/day, $\sigma_d = 20$ bottles/day, $\bar{L} = 4$ days, $\sigma_L = 0.5$ days, $Z_{0.95} = 1.65$ **Step 1:** Standard deviation of demand during lead time: $$\sigma_{DL} = \sqrt{(150)^2 (0.5)^2 + (4)(20)^2} = \sqrt{5{,}625 + 1{,}600} = \sqrt{7{,}225} = 85 \text{ bottles}$$ **Step 2:** Safety stock: $$SS = 1.65 \times 85 = 140.25 \approx 140 \text{ bottles}$$ **Step 3:** Reorder point: $$ROP = (150)(4) + 140 = 600 + 140 = 740 \text{ bottles}$$ **Answer:** The retailer should place a new order when stock falls to **740 bottles**. --- ### Part B Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* > **[KTU University Exam – Module 4, 14 marks]** > **Q (a)** Explain the concepts of EOQ, Reorder Point, and Safety Stock. Discuss the role of inventory management in agricultural technology transfer with suitable examples. [7 marks] **Model Answer:** **1. Economic Order Quantity (EOQ):** The order size that minimizes the sum of annual ordering and holding costs. Derived as $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$. Assumes constant demand and lead time. **2. Reorder Point (ROP):** The trigger inventory level. Formula: $ROP = \bar{d}\bar{L} + SS$. When stock falls to ROP, place a new order to avoid stockout. **3. Safety Stock (SS):** Buffer inventory = $Z \cdot \sigma_{DL}$. Protects against demand/lead time variability. Higher Z = higher service level but more holding cost. **Role in Agricultural Technology Transfer:** | Context | Inventory Challenge | EOQ/ROP Application | |---------|---------------------|---------------------| | **KVK biofertilizer unit** | Spoilage of microbial cultures | Short EOQ, high safety stock for peak sowing | | **Dairy cooperative** | Perishable cattle feed | Frequent small orders, ROP-based replenishment | | **Custom Hiring Center** | Tractor spare parts | ABC analysis: high-value parts monitored tightly | | **FPO produce aggregation** | Graded produce waiting for market | Just-In-Time to cold storage to avoid spoilage | | **Seed processing plant** | Hybrid seed packets seasonal demand | Production Order Quantity with seasonal forecasts | **Conclusion:** Inventory management ensures that the inputs/outputs of any transferred technology are available at the right time, right quantity, and right place — a precondition for adoption success. > **Q (b)** The annual demand for a farm tool is 4,000 units. Ordering cost is ₹200 per order and holding cost is ₹5 per unit per year. Compute EOQ. If the supplier offers a discount of 2% on orders of 1,000 units or more, should the cooperative accept the bulk order? Take unit price as ₹50. [7 marks] **Model Solution:** **Given:** D = 4,000 units, S = ₹200, H = ₹5/unit/year, C = ₹50, discount = 2% on Q ≥ 1,000 **Step 1: Compute EOQ** $$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 4{,}000 \times 200}{5}} = \sqrt{320{,}000} \approx 565.7 \text{ units}$$ EOQ (566 units) < 1,000 units → bulk order **eligible** for discount, but we must check whether it reduces total cost. **Step 2: Total cost at EOQ (no discount)** - Annual purchase cost = $4{,}000 \times 50 = ₹2{,}00{,}000$ - Ordering cost = $\frac{4{,}000}{565.7} \times 200 = ₹1{,}414.21$ - Holding cost = $\frac{565.7}{2} \times 5 = ₹1{,}414.21$ - **Total cost at EOQ = ₹2,00,000 + 1,414.21 + 1,414.21 = ₹2,02,828.42** **Step 3: Total cost with bulk order (Q = 1,000) at 2% discount** - Discounted price = $50 \times (1 - 0.02) = ₹49$ - Annual purchase cost = $4{,}000 \times 49 = ₹1{,}96{,}000$ - Ordering cost = $\frac{4{,}000}{1{,}000} \times 200 = ₹800$ - Holding cost = $\frac{1{,}000}{2} \times 5 = ₹2{,}500$ - **Total cost with bulk order = ₹1,96,000 + 800 + 2,500 = ₹1,99,300** **Step 4: Decision** Savings = ₹2,02,828.42 − ₹1,99,300 = **₹3,528.42 per year** **Conclusion:** Yes, the cooperative should accept the bulk order. The 2% discount generates a net saving of approximately **₹3,528 per year** even though holding cost increases (which is more than offset by the discount and reduced ordering cost). --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:** > > 1. **Confusing units of D, S, H:** D must be in units/year, S in ₹/order, H in ₹/unit/year. Mixing units (e.g., monthly demand with annual cost) is a **2-mark penalty** in most valuation scripts. > > 2. **Forgetting to add Safety Stock to ROP:** Many students write $ROP = d \times L$ and forget the SS term. This is a **critical conceptual error** worth 1–2 marks. > > 3. **Using wrong Z-value:** For 95% service level, Z = 1.65, **not 1.96** (which is 97.5%). Memorize the table. > > 4. **Skipping verification step:** Always show that at EOQ, ordering cost = holding cost. Examiners award bonus marks for this. > > 5. **Ignoring "discount eligibility" check in quantity discount problems:** Always verify if EOQ falls in the discount range. If not, the comparison must be made between Q* and the minimum discount quantity. > > 6. **In ABC analysis:** Don't mix up "percentage of items" with "percentage of value." Class A has few items but high value — this is the core concept. > > 7. **Misapplication of EOQ to JIT contexts:** EOQ assumes holding cost exists. JIT has near-zero inventory, so EOQ formulas are **not directly applicable** — explain this distinction in theory questions. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4: Inventory Management** **Core Definitions (Must Memorize):** - ☐ **Inventory Management** — planning, organizing, controlling stock to minimize total cost - ☐ **EOQ** — $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$, the cost-minimizing order quantity - ☐ **Reorder Point (ROP)** — inventory level triggering a new order = demand during lead time + safety stock - ☐ **Safety Stock (SS)** — buffer against variability = $Z \cdot \sigma_{DL}$ - ☐ **Lead Time (L)** — time gap between order placement and receipt - ☐ **Carrying Cost (H)** — typically 15–30% of unit cost per year - ☐ **Service Level** — probability of meeting demand from stock (Z-based) **Essential Formulas (Master All 14 from the Formula Sheet):** - ☐ EOQ, optimal N, optimal T*, minimum TC* - ☐ Holding cost H = I × C - ☐ ROP with SS (constant and variable demand/LT versions) - ☐ Inventory Turnover Ratio = COGS / Avg. Inventory - ☐ Production Order Quantity (gradual replenishment) **Classification Systems (Know Definitions and Use Cases):** - ☐ **ABC** — value-based (Pareto) - ☐ **VED** — criticality-based (spare parts) - ☐ **FSN** — movement-frequency - ☐ **HML** — unit-price based - ☐ **SDE** — scarcity-based - ☐ **GOLF** — sourcing-based **Key Theoretical Concepts:** - ☐ At EOQ, ordering cost = holding cost (symmetric U-curve property) - ☐ Total cost is U-shaped (convex function) — verify with 2nd derivative - ☐ Higher service level Z → higher safety stock → higher holding cost - ☐ Quantity discount trade-off: bulk savings vs. extra holding cost - ☐ JIT vs EOQ: when to use which **Real-World Connect (for 14-mark application questions):** - ☐ KVK input shops, FPO collective procurement, dairy cooperatives - ☐ Custom Hiring Centers, post-harvest storage, seed processing plants - ☐ Technology transfer programs depend on inventory availability **Numerical Problem Solving Checklist:** - ☐ List given data with correct units - ☐ Identify the model (basic EOQ, EOQ+SS, POQ, quantity discount) - ☐ Apply the correct formula - ☐ Show step-by-step calculation - ☐ Verify with ordering cost = holding cost (where applicable) - ☐ Interpret the result in the context of the problem **Z-Value Quick Reference (for Safety Stock problems):** - ☐ 90% → 1.28 | 95% → 1.65 | 97.5% → 1.96 | 99% → 2.33 | 99.87% → 3.00 **One-Line Exam-Ready Statements:** - ☐ "EOQ minimizes total inventory cost by balancing ordering and holding costs." - ☐ "Safety stock compensates for randomness in demand and lead time." - ☐ "ABC analysis applies the Pareto principle to prioritize inventory control." - ☐ "ROP = expected lead-time demand + safety stock." - ☐ "JIT minimizes inventory by synchronizing supply with demand." > **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Evaluation of Technology: Inventory Management**

topics.title: ABC and VED Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT416 - EXTENSION AND TRANSFER OF TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** ABC and VED Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 ABC Analysis — Formal Academic Definition

**ABC Analysis** is an inventory classification and management technique rooted in the **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)**, which categorizes inventory items (or technologies, inputs, and resources in an extension system) into three distinct classes — **A**, **B**, and **C** — based on their **Annual Consumption Value (ACV)** relative to the total inventory investment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** ABC Analysis is a material management tool used to classify items according to their relative consumption value so that managerial control, storage effort, and inventory investment can be proportionately directed toward high-value items.

The classification thresholds are:

- **Category A (High-Value, Low-Volume):** ~10–20% of total items that account for ~70–80% of cumulative annual consumption value.
- **Category B (Moderate-Value, Moderate-Volume):** ~20–30% of total items that account for ~15–25% of cumulative annual consumption value.
- **Category C (Low-Value, High-Volume):** ~50–70% of total items that account for ~5–10% of cumulative annual consumption value.

## 1.2 VED Analysis — Formal Academic Definition

**VED Analysis** is a **criticality-based** inventory classification technique that categorizes items into three classes according to the consequence of their stock-out on the operational continuity of the system:

- **V — Vital:** Stock-out halts production/extension operations completely (irreparable loss).
- **E — Essential:** Stock-out seriously affects operations but does not halt them; substitutes may exist.
- **D — Desirable:** Stock-out causes minor inconvenience; operations can continue without serious disruption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU:** ABC Analysis classifies by **monetary value (economic importance)**, while VED Analysis classifies by **operational criticality (functional importance)**. They are **complementary**, not competing methods.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pantry & Surgery" Analogy

Imagine a **hospital pantry** stocked with both everyday biscuits and life-saving surgical equipment.

- **Biscuits** are bought in huge volumes but cost almost nothing. Losing a packet is a nuisance, not a disaster. → **C-category / Desirable**
- **High-end antibiotics** may sit on the shelf for months, but if they are missing when a critical patient arrives, the loss is catastrophic. → **A-category / Vital**
- **Cotton bandages** are moderately expensive and operationally important — running out delays work but doesn't kill anyone. → **B-category / Essential**

In a **technology transfer program** (e.g., distributing hybrid seed packets, drip-irrigation kits, or solar pumps to farmers), the same logic applies: a small number of high-cost or mission-critical inputs need **tight control**, while a large pool of cheap, easily replaceable items can be **loosely managed**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in Extension:** Extension officers managing input supply chains, Krishi Bhavan stores, or community resource centers use ABC-VED to **prioritize procurement budgets, storage space, and supervision effort** — a direct application of scarce-resource optimization in technology dissemination programs.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Reference

| Term | Full Form | Meaning |
|------|-----------|---------|
| ACV | Annual Consumption Value | $\text{Annual Usage} \times \text{Unit Price}$ |
| EOQ | Economic Order Quantity | Optimal reorder quantity to minimize total cost |
| FSN | Fast-Slowing-Non-moving | Movement-based classification |
| SDE | Scarce-Difficult-Easy | Procurement difficulty classification |
| HML | High-Medium-Low | Unit price classification |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Pareto Foundation of ABC Analysis

The method operationalizes **Vilfredo Pareto's 19th-century observation** that in many natural and economic systems, roughly **80% of effects come from 20% of causes**. In inventory systems, this translates to a small fraction of items consuming a disproportionately large share of the working capital.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **List all inventory items** (e.g., I1, I2, I3, …, In) along with their annual usage quantity and unit cost.
2. **Compute Annual Consumption Value** for each item: $\text{ACV}_i = \text{Annual Usage}_i \times \text{Unit Cost}_i$.
3. **Sort items in descending order** of ACV.
4. **Cumulate** the ACV values, expressing each as a **percentage of total ACV**.
5. **Apply cut-off thresholds** (typically 70–80% for A, next 15–25% for B, balance for C) to assign categories.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The cumulative percentage is what defines the class boundaries, **not the absolute number of items**. A and C are determined by the *running total*, not by item count alone.

## 2.2 The Criticality Logic of VED Analysis

VED shifts the lens from **"how much does it cost?"** to **"how much does it hurt if it is missing?"** This makes it indispensable in domains where:

- A stock-out can cause **irreversible harm** (e.g., vaccine cold-chain failure in livestock extension).
- Substitutes are **unavailable or inadequate** (e.g., a specific micro-irrigation emitter model).
- Operations are **time-sensitive** (e.g., sowing-window criticality for seed distribution).

### 2.2.1 Decision Logic for VED Classification

- **Vital (V):** Is there an immediate, severe, irreversible consequence of stock-out? → **YES** → V
- **Essential (E):** Does stock-out cause significant operational disruption, even if work continues? → **YES** → E
- **Desirable (D):** Is stock-out a mere inconvenience with negligible operational impact? → **YES** → D

> [!NOTE]
> **Contextual Nature:** The V-E-D label is **not intrinsic to the item**; it is a function of the **operational context**. A given fertilizer bag may be "Vital" during sowing season but "Desirable" off-season.

## 2.3 The Combined ABC-VED Matrix (3 × 3)

When both analyses are overlaid, a **9-cell matrix** emerges, prescribing differentiated control strategies:

| ABC \\ VED | **V (Vital)** | **E (Essential)** | **D (Desirable)** |
|------------|---------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **A (High-Value)** | Top priority — tight control, frequent review, safety stock mandatory | High priority — periodic review, moderate safety stock | Moderate priority — orderly management |
| **B (Moderate-Value)** | High priority — regular review, mandatory safety stock | Moderate priority — periodic review | Low priority — bulk ordering |
| **C (Low-Value)** | Moderate priority — buffer stock, regular review | Low priority — bulk ordering, minimal review | Lowest priority — minimum attention |

### 2.3.1 Strategic Categorization of Cells

- **Group I (Critical Core):** AV, AE, BV, CV, BE, AD → Demand **stringent managerial attention**.
- **Group II (Moderate):** BD, CE, CD → Require **moderate control**.
- **Group III (Negligible):** The remaining cells → Permissible to operate with **minimal oversight**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick Question:** "Which cell demands the most stringent control?" — The correct answer is **Category A & V intersection (AV)**, not just A or just V. Memorize this combined-class priority order.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Annual Consumption Value | $\text{ACV} = Q \times P$ | Where $Q$ = annual usage quantity, $P$ = unit price |
| 2 | Cumulative ACV % | $\text{CumACV}\% = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{ACV}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{ACV}_i} \times 100$ | Sum up to $k$-th ranked item |
| 3 | Pareto Ratio | $\text{PR} = \dfrac{\text{Cum \% of items}}{\text{Cum \% of ACV}}$ | Lower PR → higher priority |
| 4 | Category A Boundary | First $n_A$ items where $\text{CumACV}\% \le 75\%$ (typical) | Roughly top 10–20% of items |
| 5 | Category C Boundary | Remaining items where $\text{CumACV}\% > 95\%$ (typical) | Roughly bottom 50–70% of items |
| 6 | Safety Stock (Vital items) | $\text{SS} = Z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ | Where $Z_\alpha$ is service-level constant, $\sigma_{LT}$ is lead-time demand std. dev. |
| 7 | EOQ (for any item) | $\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | $D$ = annual demand, $S$ = ordering cost, $H$ = holding cost per unit per year |
| 8 | Number of Combinations | $3 \times 3 = 9$ cells in ABC-VED matrix | 3 categories $\times$ 3 criticality classes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This analytical framework is widely applied in **agricultural input supply chains, hospital pharmacy management, manufacturing inventory control, and public distribution systems (PDS)**. In extension contexts, it directly supports **resource-constrained technology transfer** to farmer groups, FPOs (Farmer Producer Organizations), and rural cooperatives.

## 2.5 Comparison Table: ABC vs. VED vs. FSN vs. SDE vs. HML

| Method | Classification Basis | Number of Classes | Primary Use |
|--------|----------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| **ABC** | Annual consumption value | 3 (A, B, C) | Capital investment focus |
| **VED** | Criticality of stock-out | 3 (V, E, D) | Operational continuity focus |
| **FSN** | Issue/usage rate | 3 (F, S, N) | Obsolescence detection |
| **SDE** | Procurement difficulty | 3 (S, D, E) | Sourcing strategy |
| **HML** | Unit price | 3 (H, M, L) | Storage & security focus |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** Examiners often ask students to **differentiate ABC and VED** in 3-mark questions. Memorize the **value vs. criticality** distinction verbatim.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — ABC Analysis

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A **Krishi Bhavan (agricultural extension office)** stocks 8 different items used in a technology demonstration program. The annual usage quantity and unit cost of each item are given below. Perform **ABC Analysis**.

| Item Code | Annual Usage (units) | Unit Cost (₹) |
|-----------|----------------------|---------------|
| I1 | 50 | 800 |
| I2 | 200 | 50 |
| I3 | 100 | 400 |
| I4 | 400 | 20 |
| I5 | 25 | 1200 |
| I6 | 300 | 30 |
| I7 | 150 | 60 |
| I8 | 80 | 200 |

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Compute Annual Consumption Value (ACV)

For each item, we compute:
$$\text{ACV}_i = \text{Annual Usage}_i \times \text{Unit Cost}_i$$

**Row-by-row evaluation:**

- I1: $50 \times 800 = 40{,}000$ ₹
- I2: $200 \times 50 = 10{,}000$ ₹
- I3: $100 \times 400 = 40{,}000$ ₹
- I4: $400 \times 20 = 8{,}000$ ₹
- I5: $25 \times 1200 = 30{,}000$ ₹
- I6: $300 \times 30 = 9{,}000$ ₹
- I7: $150 \times 60 = 9{,}000$ ₹
- I8: $80 \times 200 = 16{,}000$ ₹

**Total ACV:**
$$\text{Total ACV} = 40000 + 10000 + 40000 + 8000 + 30000 + 9000 + 9000 + 16000 = 1{,}62{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Sort in Descending Order of ACV

| Item Code | ACV (₹) | Rank |
|-----------|---------|------|
| I1 | 40,000 | 1 |
| I3 | 40,000 | 2 |
| I5 | 30,000 | 3 |
| I8 | 16,000 | 4 |
| I2 | 10,000 | 5 |
| I6 | 9,000 | 6 |
| I7 | 9,000 | 7 |
| I4 | 8,000 | 8 |

> [!NOTE]
> When two items share the same ACV, we follow the convention of ranking by the higher individual usage quantity first, or alternatively by item code alphabetically. Either is acceptable in KTU evaluations.

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Compute Cumulative ACV and Cumulative Percentage

| Rank | Item | ACV (₹) | Cumulative ACV (₹) | Cumulative % |
|------|------|---------|---------------------|--------------|
| 1 | I1 | 40,000 | 40,000 | 24.69% |
| 2 | I3 | 40,000 | 80,000 | 49.38% |
| 3 | I5 | 30,000 | 1,10,000 | 67.90% |
| 4 | I8 | 16,000 | 1,26,000 | 77.78% |
| 5 | I2 | 10,000 | 1,36,000 | 83.95% |
| 6 | I6 | 9,000 | 1,45,000 | 89.51% |
| 7 | I7 | 9,000 | 1,54,000 | 95.06% |
| 8 | I4 | 8,000 | 1,62,000 | 100.00% |

**Mathematical justification of cumulative percentage calculation:**

$$\text{Cum\%}_k = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{ACV}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{ACV}_i} \times 100$$

For example, at rank 3:
$$\text{Cum\%}_3 = \frac{40000 + 40000 + 30000}{162000} \times 100 = \frac{110000}{162000} \times 100 \approx 67.90\%$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Apply Classification Thresholds

Using standard cut-offs:
- **Class A:** Items contributing to first ~70–80% of cumulative ACV → Items I1, I3, I5 (Cum% = 67.90%) + part of I8
- **Class B:** Items contributing to next ~15–25% → I8, I2 (Cum% goes from 67.90% to 83.95%)
- **Class C:** Remaining items → I6, I7, I4 (Cum% goes from 83.95% to 100%)

**Final Classification:**

| Item | ACV (₹) | Cum % | Category |
|------|---------|-------|----------|
| I1 | 40,000 | 24.69% | **A** |
| I3 | 40,000 | 49.38% | **A** |
| I5 | 30,000 | 67.90% | **A** |
| I8 | 16,000 | 77.78% | **B** |
| I2 | 10,000 | 83.95% | **B** |
| I6 | 9,000 | 89.51% | **C** |
| I7 | 9,000 | 95.06% | **C** |
| I4 | 8,000 | 100.00% | **C** |

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** 3 items (37.5%) — I1, I3, I5 — constitute 67.9% of total inventory value. These deserve **maximum managerial attention, frequent stock reviews, and tight EOQ-based ordering**. The bottom 3 items (37.5%) — I6, I7, I4 — consume only ~16% of value and can be managed with **bulk ordering and minimal review**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Combined ABC-VED Matrix

### 3.2.1 Extension to VED Classification

Suppose the extension officer (after consulting field staff) classifies the same 8 items by criticality as follows:

| Item | ABC Class | VED Class | Combined Cell | Control Strategy |
|------|-----------|-----------|----------------|------------------|
| I1 | A | V | **AV** | Top priority — strict EOQ, safety stock, monthly review |
| I3 | A | E | **AE** | High priority — quarterly review, moderate safety stock |
| I5 | A | V | **AV** | Top priority — strict EOQ, safety stock, monthly review |
| I8 | B | D | **BD** | Low priority — bulk ordering, bi-annual review |
| I2 | B | E | **BE** | Moderate priority — quarterly review |
| I6 | C | D | **CD** | Lowest priority — bulk ordering, minimal review |
| I7 | C | V | **CV** | Moderate priority — buffer stock mandatory despite low value |
| I4 | C | D | **CD** | Lowest priority — bulk ordering, minimal review |

### 3.2.2 Why Item I7 (CV) is Important

Item I7 is low-value (only ₹9,000 ACV) but **vital** for extension operations — perhaps a unique pheromone trap insert that has no substitute. A stock-out would halt the entire pest-monitoring program. The CV cell therefore demands **mandatory buffer stock** even though its monetary value is low.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the central insight of combining ABC and VED:** A low-value item can still be operationally critical, and a high-value item can still be operationally dispensable. Both lenses together yield a **balanced, risk-aware inventory policy**.

## 3.3 Verification of the Pareto Pattern

To mathematically verify that the distribution follows Pareto's principle:

$$\text{Pareto Ratio} = \frac{\text{Cumulative \% of items}}{\text{Cumulative \% of ACV}}$$

At rank 3 (boundary of A-class):
$$\text{PR}_3 = \frac{37.5\%}{67.90\%} \approx 0.552$$

A Pareto Ratio **less than 1** confirms that items are over-represented in value relative to their count — the essence of Pareto concentration. KTU expects students to **state the Pareto Ratio** when explaining why ABC works.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — ABC Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: List all inventory items"] --> B["Step 2: Compute Annual Consumption Value ACV = Q x P"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Sort items in descending order of ACV"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Compute Cumulative ACV and Cumulative Percentage"]
    D --> E{"Step 5: Apply threshold cutoffs"}
    E --> F["Class A: Top items covering 70 to 80 percent of value"]
    E --> G["Class B: Next items covering 15 to 25 percent of value"]
    E --> H["Class C: Remaining items covering 5 to 10 percent of value"]
    F --> I["Step 6: Apply differentiated control policies"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["A items: Tight control, frequent review, EOQ"]
    I --> K["B items: Moderate control, periodic review"]
    I --> L["C items: Bulk ordering, minimal oversight"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Combined ABC-VED 3x3 Matrix Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph rowA["ROW A: High Value Items"]
        AV["AV Cell: Top Priority\nStrict EOQ, Safety Stock\nMonthly Review"]
        AE["AE Cell: High Priority\nModerate Safety Stock\nQuarterly Review"]
        AD["AD Cell: Moderate Priority\nOrderly Management\nBi-annual Review"]
    end

    subgraph rowB["ROW B: Moderate Value Items"]
        BV["BV Cell: High Priority\nRegular Review\nSafety Stock Mandatory"]
        BE["BE Cell: Moderate Priority\nPeriodic Review\nNormal Stocking"]
        BD["BD Cell: Low Priority\nBulk Ordering\nSemi-annual Review"]
    end

    subgraph rowC["ROW C: Low Value Items"]
        CV["CV Cell: Moderate Priority\nBuffer Stock Mandatory\nDespite Low Value"]
        CE["CE Cell: Low Priority\nBulk Ordering\nMinimal Review"]
        CD["CD Cell: Lowest Priority\nMinimum Attention\nAnnual Review"]
    end

    style AV fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style BV fill:#ffa94d,color:#fff
    style CV fill:#ffd43b,color:#000
    style AE fill:#ff8787,color:#fff
    style BE fill:#ffc078,color:#000
    style CE fill:#ffe066,color:#000
    style AD fill:#ffa8a8,color:#000
    style BD fill:#ffd8a8,color:#000
    style CD fill:#fff3bf,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Color Coding Logic:** Red shades indicate **highest priority** (Group I critical core), orange shades indicate **moderate priority** (Group II), and yellow shades indicate **lowest priority** (Group III). This visual hierarchy directly maps to the differentiated control strategy required by the KTU 2024 syllabus.

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree — VED Classification Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Is the item in stock?"] -->|Yes| Q2["Proceed to use normally"]
    Q1 -->|No - Stockout occurred| Q3["Evaluate severity of stockout"]
    Q3 --> Q4{"Does production halt completely?"}
    Q4 -->|Yes - Irreversible loss| Vclass["Classify as VITAL"]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{"Is there significant disruption but work continues?"}
    Q5 -->|Yes| Eclass["Classify as ESSENTIAL"]
    Q5 -->|No - Minor inconvenience| Dclass["Classify as DESIRABLE"]
    Vclass --> P1["Mandatory Safety Stock\nStrict EOQ\nFrequent Review"]
    Eclass --> P2["Moderate Safety Stock\nPeriodic Review"]
    Dclass --> P3["Bulk Ordering\nMinimal Review"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Integration Map — ABC-VED in Extension Program Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EXT["Extension Program Launch"] --> IDENT["Identify input items\nseeds, tools, kits, fertilizers"]
    IDENT --> VAL["Compute ACV for each item\nQuantity x Unit Price"]
    VAL --> SORTABC["Sort by ACV descending\nClassify as A, B, C"]
    SORTABC --> CRIT["Assess criticality via field staff\nClassify as V, E, D"]
    CRIT --> MATRIX["Place each item in 3x3 ABC-VED cell"]
    MATRIX --> POLICY["Apply cell-specific control policy"]
    POLICY --> MONITOR["Periodic review and re-classification"]
    MONITOR --> IDENT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Define ABC Analysis. State the principle on which it is based.**

**Model Answer:**

ABC Analysis is an inventory classification technique that categorizes items into three groups — **A, B, and C** — based on their **Annual Consumption Value (ACV)**.

It is based on the **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)**, which states that roughly **80% of the total inventory value** is contributed by only **20% of the total items**, while the remaining 80% of items contribute only 20% of the value. This principle guides the proportional allocation of managerial attention, storage resources, and control effort to high-value items. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for principle, 1 for managerial implication)**

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

**Differentiate between ABC Analysis and VED Analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis of Difference | ABC Analysis | VED Analysis |
|----------------------|--------------|--------------|
| Classification Basis | Annual Consumption Value (monetary) | Criticality of stock-out (operational) |
| Number of Classes | 3 (A, B, C) | 3 (V, E, D) |
| Primary Focus | Capital investment optimization | Operational continuity assurance |
| Determining Authority | Quantitative computation (ACV) | Qualitative judgment (expert input) |
| Typical Application | Working capital management | Risk-based stock management |

ABC tells us **what to spend on**; VED tells us **what we cannot do without**. **(3 marks — table for 2 marks, concluding statement for 1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question B1 (Choice A) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the procedure of conducting ABC Analysis with a suitable numerical example. State the criteria for classifying items into A, B, and C categories.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the VED Analysis technique. How is it different from ABC Analysis? Explain with an example relevant to agricultural extension.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Procedure of ABC Analysis:**

1. **Item Listing:** Enumerate all inventory items (e.g., seeds, fertilizers, tools, equipment spares) with annual usage quantity and unit price.
2. **ACV Computation:** For each item $i$, compute $\text{ACV}_i = Q_i \times P_i$.
3. **Sorting:** Arrange items in descending order of ACV.
4. **Cumulative Calculation:** Compute cumulative ACV and convert to cumulative percentage of total ACV.
5. **Classification:** Apply thresholds — typically:
   - **Class A:** Items whose cumulative ACV ≤ 75% (covers top 10–20% of items)
   - **Class B:** Items whose cumulative ACV is between 75% and 95% (covers next 20–30%)
   - **Class C:** Remaining items (covers bottom 50–70%)

**Numerical Example (Concise):** Consider 5 items with ACVs: ₹50,000, ₹30,000, ₹12,000, ₹5,000, ₹3,000. Total = ₹1,00,000. Cumulative percentages: 50%, 80%, 92%, 97%, 100%. **Items 1 and 2 → Class A**, **Item 3 → Class B**, **Items 4 and 5 → Class C**. **(7 marks — 3 for procedure, 2 for numerical demonstration, 2 for threshold logic)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**VED Analysis Description:**

VED Analysis classifies inventory based on the **severity of consequence** when an item is out of stock:
- **V (Vital):** Stock-out causes immediate, irreparable halt to operations.
- **E (Essential):** Stock-out causes significant disruption, but operations continue with reduced efficiency.
- **D (Desirable):** Stock-out causes only minor inconvenience; production is largely unaffected.

**Key Differences from ABC:**

| Feature | ABC | VED |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Decides | Where to invest money | Where to maintain buffer stock |
| Driven by | Cost data | Risk assessment |
| Focus | Economic | Functional |

**Agricultural Extension Example:** A Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) stores:
- **V:** Hybrid paddy seed treated with specific fungicide — missing it before sowing window ruins the entire demo.
- **E:** Standard urea bags — operations continue but yield is suboptimal.
- **D:** Decorative posters — stock-out is purely cosmetic. **(7 marks — 3 for VED description, 2 for difference table, 2 for contextual example)**

---

### Question B1 (Choice B — Alternative) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Construct the ABC-VED combined matrix. Explain how the combined analysis helps in prioritizing inventory management in a technology transfer program.

**(b) [7 marks]** "ABC Analysis alone is insufficient for inventory management in technology extension programs." Discuss this statement with suitable justification and explain how VED analysis supplements ABC.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**ABC-VED Combined Matrix (3 × 3):**

The matrix cross-tabulates ABC classes (rows) with VED classes (columns), yielding 9 cells. The **AV cell** (A-class, V-class) represents the **most critical items** — high value AND vital for operations. These demand **strict EOQ, mandatory safety stock, and monthly review**.

Conversely, the **CD cell** (C-class, D-class) represents the **least critical items** — low value and operationally dispensable, justifying **bulk ordering and minimal review**.

**Application in Technology Transfer Program:**

In a Farmer Producer Organization (FPO) distributing solar pumps, drip kits, and bio-fertilizers:
- **AV** = Solar pump inverter (high cost, mission-critical for irrigation demo)
- **AE** = Drip lateral pipes (high cost, important but substitutable)
- **BV** = Specific emitter nozzles (moderate cost, no substitute)
- **CD** = Promotional leaflets

The combined analysis enables **risk-adjusted budgeting** — protecting against stock-out of vital items regardless of their cost, and avoiding over-investment in expensive but dispensable items. **(7 marks — 2 for matrix, 3 for technology transfer application, 2 for prioritization logic)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Discussion of the Statement:**

ABC Analysis optimizes **capital allocation** but is **blind to operational criticality**. A low-cost but irreplaceable input (e.g., a specific vaccine vial for livestock extension) may be misclassified as a low-priority C-item purely on monetary grounds — yet its stock-out could collapse the entire vaccination drive.

**How VED Supplements ABC:**

VED injects a **risk and continuity dimension** that pure monetary classification misses. By overlaying VED on ABC, the manager gains a **two-dimensional view**:
- **Dimension 1 (Value):** Where is the money locked up?
- **Dimension 2 (Criticality):** Where is the operational risk concentrated?

**Justification:** The combined analysis converts a one-dimensional monetary classification into a **two-dimensional risk-value classification**, enabling decisions that are simultaneously **cost-efficient and operationally resilient**. In technology extension — where program success often hinges on timely availability of niche inputs — this dual perspective is **indispensable**. **(7 marks — 2 for statement analysis, 3 for VED supplementation logic, 2 for extension-specific justification)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing ACV with total cost:** ACV = annual usage × unit price, NOT just unit price alone. Many students incorrectly rank items by unit cost, leading to a **flawed ABC classification**. Loss: **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Treating VED as intrinsic to items:** VED classification depends on **operational context and timing**. A seed packet may be Vital during sowing season but Desirable off-season. Failure to mention this contextual nature costs **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting the cumulative percentage basis:** The A-B-C boundary is set by **cumulative ACV percentage**, not by the number of items. Examiners specifically look for the word "cumulative" — missing it is a common deduction point.
> 4. **Not drawing the 3×3 matrix in the combined question:** A textual description without a labelled 3×3 grid is considered incomplete. Always include the matrix **structure** in your answer.
> 5. **Ignoring EOQ and safety stock links:** For 14-mark questions, students who only classify items but do not discuss **what control action follows** lose marks on the "application" component.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ABC Analysis** = classification by **Annual Consumption Value**; based on the **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)**.
- **VED Analysis** = classification by **stock-out criticality**; based on **operational impact** (Vital, Essential, Desirable).
- Standard ABC thresholds: **A ≈ 10–20% of items / 70–80% of value; B ≈ 20–30% / 15–25%; C ≈ 50–70% / 5–10%**.
- The **ACV formula** is $\text{ACV} = \text{Annual Usage} \times \text{Unit Price}$.
- Classification uses **cumulative ACV percentage**, not absolute counts.
- The **ABC-VED combined matrix** is a 3×3 grid yielding 9 distinct control cells.
- The **AV cell** (A-class + V-class) is the **most critical** and demands the **strictest control**.
- The **CD cell** (C-class + D-class) is the **least critical** and permits **minimal oversight**.
- A **low-value item can still be Vital** (e.g., CV cell), and a **high-value item can still be Desirable** (e.g., AD cell) — this is why both analyses must be combined.
- **VED classification is contextual**, not intrinsic — it varies with **season, geography, and operational phase**.
- ABC-VED is **directly applicable in technology transfer** programs (KVKs, FPOs, agricultural extension input supply chains) for **resource-efficient procurement and risk-managed stock policies**.
- The **Pareto Ratio** $\text{PR} = \dfrac{\text{Cum \% of items}}{\text{Cum \% of ACV}}$ is a verification metric — values **less than 1** confirm Pareto concentration.
- Related classification methods to know for KTU: **FSN** (movement rate), **SDE** (procurement difficulty), **HML** (unit price), **GOLF** (government/local/foreign/imported origin) — but ABC and VED are the most frequently examined pair.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CPM and PERT Network Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT416 - EXTENSION AND TRANSFER OF TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** CPM and PERT Network Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**CPM (Critical Path Method)** and **PERT (Programme Evaluation and Review Technique)** are two closely related project management techniques used for planning, scheduling, coordinating, and controlling complex projects comprising a large number of interrelated activities. They fall under the umbrella of **Network Analysis** in Operations Research and Project Management.

> [!NOTE]
> **CPM (Critical Path Method):** Developed in **1957** by James E. Kelley of Remington Rand and Morgan R. Walker of DuPont (USA). Used for projects where activity durations are **deterministic** (known with reasonable certainty), such as construction, manufacturing, and routine engineering projects. Focuses on **time-cost trade-off**.

> [!NOTE]
> **PERT (Programme Evaluation and Review Technique):** Developed in **1958** by the U.S. Navy's Special Projects Office for the Polaris missile project. Used for projects involving **research, development, and uncertainty**, where activity durations are **probabilistic** (estimated using three-time estimates). Focuses on **time** rather than cost.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **cooking a multi-dish festive meal** for a wedding banquet (say, Biryani, Salad, Raita, Curry, and Dessert). Some dishes can be cooked in parallel (rice boils while you chop salad), while others MUST be sequential (you cannot fry onions until you have sliced them). 

- The **Network Diagram** is like the **recipe sequence chart** you draw on your kitchen wall.
- The **Critical Path** is the **longest, most time-consuming chain** of tasks — the one dish that, if delayed by even 5 minutes, delays the entire wedding feast.
- **CPM** is like cooking a familiar family recipe where you know exactly that boiling rice takes **20 minutes** (deterministic).
- **PERT** is like cooking an experimental dish for the first time, where you estimate boiling time as **15 minutes optimistically, 20 most likely, and 30 if things go wrong** (probabilistic).

In project terms, the **Critical Path** is the sequence of activities that determines the **minimum project completion time**. Any delay on this path delays the entire project. Activities NOT on the critical path have **slack (float)** — they can be delayed without affecting the project deadline.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Vocabulary for KTU Board Exams — Memorize These Exact Terms:**

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Activity** | A task or job that consumes time and resources; represented by an **arrow (→)** in arrow diagrams. |
| **Event (Node)** | A specific milestone — the **start or finish** of one or more activities; represented by a **circle or rectangle**. It consumes **no time or resources**. |
| **Network** | The logical sequence of activities and events drawn from project start to finish. |
| **Predecessor Activity** | An activity that must be completed **before** another activity can begin. |
| **Successor Activity** | An activity that cannot start until another activity is **completed**. |
| **Dummy Activity** | A fictitious activity with **zero duration**, used to show logical dependency between events. Represented by a **dashed arrow (⇢)**. |
| **Critical Path** | The **longest path** through the network, determining minimum project duration. |
| **Critical Activity** | An activity lying on the critical path; has **zero slack**. |
| **Float (Slack)** | The amount of time an activity can be delayed without delaying project completion. |
| **Earliest Start (ES)** | The earliest time an activity can start. |
| **Earliest Finish (EF)** | The earliest time an activity can finish (ES + Duration). |
| **Latest Start (LS)** | The latest time an activity can start without delaying the project. |
| **Latest Finish (LF)** | The latest time an activity can finish without delaying the project. |

### 1.4 Differences Between CPM and PERT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direct KTU Exam Favourite — Memorize the Differences:**

| Parameter | CPM | PERT |
|---|---|---|
| **Duration Type** | Deterministic (single estimate) | Probabilistic (three estimates) |
| **Focus** | Time-Cost trade-off | Time only |
| **Best Suited For** | Construction, manufacturing | R&D, research projects |
| **Critical Path** | Generally well-defined | May change across iterations |
| **Crashing** | Yes (cost vs time analysis) | Not applicable |
| **Origin Year** | 1957 | 1958 |
| **Developed By** | DuPont & Remington Rand | U.S. Navy |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Steps Involved in CPM/PERT Analysis

The complete methodology follows a structured, sequential flow:

**Step 1 — Project Definition:** Identify all activities required to complete the project. Define the scope clearly.
